gmw_00001321.name: IRAS F21013-0739
gmw_00001321.overview: IRAS F21013-0739 is a massive, luminous Lyman Break Analog galaxy with faint, asymmetric spiral arms and a potential companion to the south-west [arXiv:0906.1012, arXiv:1407.0914]. Its unique infrared spectrum combines starburst-driven polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon emission with silicate emission heated by a young active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1605.06999, arXiv:1407.0914].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IRAS F21013-0739 as one of three spectroscopically anomalous galaxies whose Spitzer spectra combine steep 5-8 micron continua, strong PAH bands, and silicate emission linked to starbursts and AGN heating.

quotes.quote: We investigate the dust properties in three spectroscopically anomalous galaxies (IRAS F10398+1455, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948). Their Spitzer /IRS spectra are characterized by a steep 5--8 emission continuum, strong emission bands from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) molecules, and prominent 10 silicate emission. The steep 5--8 continuum and strong PAH emission features suggest the presence of starbursts, while the silicate emission is indicative of significant heating from AGNs. The simultaneous detection of these two observational properties has rarely been reported on galactic scale.

We have modeled the Spitzer /IRS spectra of three spectroscopically anomalous galaxies (IRAS F10398+1455, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948) by decomposing them via , subtracting the starlight, PAH and atom-line components, and then modeling the residual dust component with the \ torus model. The IR spectral characteristics of these galaxies are unique in the sense that they show silicate emission which is characteristic of AGNs, while they also show a steep 5--8 continuum and strong PAH emission features typical for starburst galaxies. In contrast, AGNs exhibit a flat emission continuum at 5--8 and lack the PAH emission features. The 9.7 and 18 silicate features seen in emission in these three galaxies are seen in absorption in starbursts.

sources.summary: The paper finds ScUVLG210358 is massive and luminous, with faint asymmetric arms, a regular velocity gradient and ordered nuclear rotation despite merger-like morphology.

quotes.quote: ScUVLG210358 While morphologically similar to ScUVLG143417, we find that the kinematics in this galaxy are quite different. Again, the optical image reveals faint, irregular and asymmetric arms with a bright, unresolved star-forming core. However, in this case, there is a single bright, symmetric core. This galaxy is one of the most massive and luminous ScUVLGs in our sample.

The velocity gradient across the highest S/N region of this galaxy is very regular, maintaining similar structure to the outer, fainter regions of the system. Hints of a merger are apparent from HST images, displaying irregular, clumpy light distribution and a potential faint companion to the south-west. Yet the dynamics of this galaxy's central region show ordered rotation, with the peak corresponding with the galaxy's nucleus. Therefore this galaxy has dynamics consistent with disks.

sources.summary: The paper reports anomalous Spitzer/IRS dust properties in IRAS F21013-0739, identifying it as a Lyman Break Analog with silicate emission, red continuum, PAH bands, and young AGN/starburst activity.

quotes.quote: Here we report the detection of anomalous dust properties by Spitzer /Infrared Spectrograph in three galaxies (IRAS F10398+1455, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948) which are characterized by the simultaneous detection of a red 5--8 emission continuum, the 9.7 and 18 silicate emission features as well as strong PAH emission bands. These apparently contradictory dust IR emission properties are discussed in terms of iron-poor silicate composition, carbon dust deficit, small grain size and low dust temperature in the young AGN phase of these three galaxies.
Here we report the simultaneous detection of a low, red 5--8 emission continuum and strong silicate emission features in three galaxies, IRAS F10398+1455, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948.

The 10.51 [ S IV ] line is prominent in IRAS F10398+1455 and IRAS F21013-0739, but not seen in SDSS J0808+3948. No obvious high ionization-potential lines like [ Ne V ] 14.32 and [ O IV ] 25.89 (which are typical in AGNs) are detected in our three galaxies. Finally, the 9.7 and 18 amorphous silicate emission features are clearly seen in all three galaxies. In Figure 1a we also compare the mid-IR spectra of these three galaxies with the averaged spectrum of starburst galaxies (Brandl 2006) and the average spectrum of quasars (Hao 2007). It is seen that our three sources have a comparable silicate emission strength as quasars, but they show a much steeper or ``redder'' 5--8 continuum than typical AGNs or quasars. The slope of the 5--8 continuum, defined as d F_ /d (where F_ is the observed flux and is wavelength), is 4.1--4.6 for the three sources, while the 5--8 continuum is flat or ``gray'' for AGNs (with a slope of 0.8). The ``red'' 5--8 emission continuum and the strong PAH emission features seen in these galaxies are also seen in starburst galaxies. However, the 9.7 and 18 emission features seen in these galaxies are never seen in starburst galaxies. Type 1 AGNs (and some type 2 AGNs; see Sturm et al. \ 2006, Mason et al. \ 2009, Nikutta et al. \ 2009, Shi et al. \ 2010) exhibit the silicate emission features seen in these galaxies, but their 5--8 emission continuum is often much flatter and do not show the PAH emission features which are prominent in these three galaxies. The exact profiles and strengths of the derived silicate emission features are sensitive to the assumed underlying continuum (e.g., see Sirocky et al. \ 2008, Baum et al. \ 2010, Gallimore et al. \ 2010). To subtract the continuum, we take two approaches: (1) we select five points at 5--7 , 14.5--15.0 and 29--30 to define a underlying continuum which is calculated with a spline function (see Figure 2); (2) we modify the PAHFIT software of Smith et al. \ (2007) to fit the observed IRS spectrum of each source with a combination of PAH features, amorphous silicate features, modified blackbodies, and starlight, with the sum of the modified blackbodies and starlight representing the continuum underneath the PAH and silicate features (see Figure 3). Not surprisingly, the silicate emission features derived from these two approaches differ substantially: the 9.7 feature derived from the spline method is considerably weaker than that derived from the PAHFIT method, while it is in the opposite for the 18 feature. Nevertheless, it is clear that both the 9.7 and 18 amorphous silicate emission features are present in these three sources. Crystalline forsterite silicates are also present in these galaxies as revealed by the sharp features at 19, 23, and 27.5 (see Figures 2, 3). The detection of crystalline silicate dust in extragalactic sources has been reported for some ULIRGs (Spoon et al. \ 2006), for PG 2112+059, a broad absorption line quasar (Kemper et al. \ 2007), for a distant absorber at redshift z_ abs 0.886 along the line of sight toward the gravitationally lensed quasar PKS 1830-211 (Aller et al. \ 2012), and for a z_ abs 0.685 absorber toward the gravitationally lensed blazar TXS 0218+357 (Aller et al. \ 2014). In the Galactic ISM the silicate dust is predominantly amorphous (Li Draine 2001, Kemper et al. \ 2004, Li et al. \ 2007). As shown in Figures 2, 3, the silicate features of these three galaxies are broad and smooth with sharp crystalline silicate features superimposed, suggesting that the silicate dust in these sources is mainly amorphous. These three galaxies are found when cross-matching the SDSS and Spitzer /IRS low resolution spectra (L. Hao 2014, in preparation). Xie (2014a) have studied the multi-wavelength properties of these three galaxies and demonstrated that they may harbor a young AGN at the center. Among these galaxies, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948 are Lyman Break Analogs (LBA; Heckman 2005, Hoopes 2007).

sources.summary: The paper models IRAS F21013-0739 as one of three anomalous infrared galaxies whose spectra combine starburst-like PAH/continuum emission with AGN-like silicate emission and comparatively cool dust.

quotes.quote: The Spitzer /Infrared Spectrograph spectra of three spectroscopically anomalous galaxies (IRAS F10398+1455, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948) are modeled in terms of a mixture of warm and cold silicate dust, and warm and cold carbon dust. Their unique infrared (IR) emission spectra are characterized by a steep 5--8 emission continuum, strong emission bands from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) molecules, and prominent silicate emission. The steep 5--8 emission continuum and strong PAH emission features suggest the dominance of starbursts, while the silicate emission is indicative of significant heating from active galactic nuclei (AGNs).

We have modeled the Spitzer /IRS spectra of three spectroscopically anomalous galaxies (IRAS F10398+1455, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948) in terms of a simple model consisting of a mixture of warm and cold silicate dust, and warm and cold carbon dust. The IR spectral characteristics of these galaxies are unique in the sense that they show silicate emission which is characteristic of AGN while they also show a steep 5--8 emission continuum and strong PAH emission features which are characteristic of starburst galaxies.

gmw_00001322.name: SDSS J0808+3948
gmw_00001322.overview: SDSS J0808+3948 is a Lyman Break Analog galaxy featuring a bright, unresolved central source within an extended diffuse structure [arXiv:1407.0914, arXiv:0709.3304]. It exhibits anomalous infrared spectra combining starburst-driven polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon emission with active galactic nucleus-heated silicate emission, and it may be interacting with a southern companion [arXiv:1605.06999, arXiv:0709.3304].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J0808+3948 as one of three spectroscopically anomalous galaxies whose Spitzer spectra combine steep 5-8 micron continua, strong PAH bands, and silicate emission linked to starbursts and AGN heating.

sources.summary: The paper reports anomalous Spitzer/IRS dust properties in SDSS J0808+3948, a Lyman Break Analog with silicate emission, PAH bands, no prominent [S IV], and young AGN/starburst activity.

quotes.quote: Here we report the detection of anomalous dust properties by Spitzer /Infrared Spectrograph in three galaxies (IRAS F10398+1455, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948) which are characterized by the simultaneous detection of a red 5--8 emission continuum, the 9.7 and 18 silicate emission features as well as strong PAH emission bands. These apparently contradictory dust IR emission properties are discussed in terms of iron-poor silicate composition, carbon dust deficit, small grain size and low dust temperature in the young AGN phase of these three galaxies.
Here we report the simultaneous detection of a low, red 5--8 emission continuum and strong silicate emission features in three galaxies, IRAS F10398+1455, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948.

The 10.51 [ S IV ] line is prominent in IRAS F10398+1455 and IRAS F21013-0739, but not seen in SDSS J0808+3948. To subtract the continuum, we take two approaches: (1) we select five points at 5--7 , 14.5--15.0 and 29--30 to define a underlying continuum which is calculated with a spline function (see Figure 2); (2) we modify the PAHFIT software of Smith et al. \ (2007) to fit the observed IRS spectrum of each source with a combination of PAH features, amorphous silicate features, modified blackbodies, and starlight, with the sum of the modified blackbodies and starlight representing the continuum underneath the PAH and silicate features (see Figure 3). Nevertheless, it is clear that both the 9.7 and 18 amorphous silicate emission features are present in these three sources. Crystalline forsterite silicates are also present in these galaxies as revealed by the sharp features at 19, 23, and 27.5 (see Figures 2, 3). The detection of crystalline silicate dust in extragalactic sources has been reported for some ULIRGs (Spoon et al. \ 2006), for PG 2112+059, a broad absorption line quasar (Kemper et al. \ 2007), for a distant absorber at redshift z_ abs 0.886 along the line of sight toward the gravitationally lensed quasar PKS 1830-211 (Aller et al. \ 2012), and for a z_ abs 0.685 absorber toward the gravitationally lensed blazar TXS 0218+357 (Aller et al. \ 2014). In the Galactic ISM the silicate dust is predominantly amorphous (Li Draine 2001, Kemper et al. \ 2004, Li et al. \ 2007). As shown in Figures 2, 3, the silicate features of these three galaxies are broad and smooth with sharp crystalline silicate features superimposed, suggesting that the silicate dust in these sources is mainly amorphous. These three galaxies are found when cross-matching the SDSS and Spitzer /IRS low resolution spectra (L. Hao 2014, in preparation). Xie (2014a) have studied the multi-wavelength properties of these three galaxies and demonstrated that they may harbor a young AGN at the center. Among these galaxies, IRAS F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948 are Lyman Break Analogs (LBA; Heckman 2005, Hoopes 2007). LBAs are rare in the local universe. They are selected based on their far-UV properties and they show similar properties as that of high-redshift Lyman Break Galaxies (Steidel et al. \ 1999). Among the 30 LBA galaxies, six are found to contain a young, very compact ( 10 ^2 ), highly massive (several 10 ^9 ) Dominant Central Object (DCO, Overzier 2009). Interestingly, both F21013-0739 and SDSS J0808+3948 are found to have DCOs.

sources.summary: The paper describes a bright unresolved central source inside an extended diffuse galaxy, possible interaction with a southern companion, and a much fainter companion nucleus.

quotes.quote: SDSS J080844.26+394852.4 . There is a bright, unresolved source seen in the \ image that is located in the centre of an extended, highly diffuse galaxy seen in . The unresolved source is remarkably bright as both \ and \ show the ACS diffraction spikes. The \ image suffers from poor point source subtraction. The object seen 2 to the South lies at a similar redshift, and may be interacting. The nucleus of this companion is also detected in , but its flux is 75 lower.

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J0808+3948 as one of three anomalous infrared galaxies whose spectra combine starburst-like PAH/continuum emission with AGN-like silicate emission and comparatively cool dust.

gmw_00001323.name: Mrk 6
gmw_00001323.overview: Mrk 6 is a radio-loud Seyfert 1.5 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1310.1179, arXiv:2312.11167] hosted within a lenticular galaxy [arXiv:2003.12809, arXiv:2312.11167]. It features a complex radio morphology with two perpendicular sets of radio lobes [arXiv:1402.1577, arXiv:1603.08364] and a precessing parsec-scale jet [arXiv:1310.1179, arXiv:1807.00979].

sources.summary: The paper estimates Mrk 6 polarimetric properties, noting its black-hole mass, Eddington ratio, inclination, stable mean polarization, and magnetic-field strengths inferred from its H-line behavior.

quotes.quote: According to Ho, Darling Greene (2008), the mass of the central black hole in Mrk 6 is (M_ BH /M_ )=7.97 0.5 , the ratio of the bolometric luminosity to the Eddington value is (L_ bol /L_Edd)=-1.72 . The inclination angle is i=62^ .7, =0.46 (Ho et al. 2008). It means that the standard (Sobolev--Chandrasekhar) magnitude of the polarization degree is p_c( )=2.52 .

The observed mean polarization has been found at the level of p_c=0.90 0.03 , p(H_ )=0.85 0.04 (Smith et al. 2002). The most remarkable fact is the jump of the mean position angle for two observational seasons of Feb 97 and Oct 98: 25^ . It is interesting that the value of the mean polarization remained the same.
Eqs.(11) and (58) give estimate: B_ 0.6 G and B_ 8.5 G.

sources.summary: The paper monitors Seyfert 1.5 galaxy Mrk 6 spectropolarimetrically, linking its variable Balmer lines, complex BLR kinematics, outflow signatures, and radio structures.

quotes.quote: We present the spectropolarimetric observations (monitoring) of Mrk 6 in more than two year period, from 2010 to 2013 (12 spectra) in the optical spectral range (covering the H and H wavelength region). Mrk 6 (IC 450) is a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy (z=0.0185, m(B)=14.29, M(B)=-20.41) that has been observed in the frame of the Spectropolarimetric monitoring campaign of AGN with the 6-m BTA Telescope af11 Mrk 6 is a good candidate for studying continuum and line polarization structure variation, since it is a bright object with the significant variability in broad emission lines and with the BLR that most-likely has a complex geometry, structure and kinematics Ro94, E93, sm02, DS03 . Previous optical monitoring has shown that the continuum and broad Balmer lines are strongly variable E93, Se99, DS03, Do12 , and the strong blue asymmetry is seen in Balmer-emission lines E93, Kh11, Do12 . The analysis of the line profiles variability and time-lags gives that the BLR kinematics in Mrk 6 is a combination of Keplerian (disc-like) and infall gas motion Do12 . Also, some signature of the outflowing dense hydrogen gas-cloud has been seen in Balmer-emission lines Kh11 .

This could be supported by the radio measurements as radio-jets have been observed in this object Ca95 . Recent VLA observations show spectacular radio structures Kh06 on kpc-scales, that cannot be explained by super-winds ejected by a nuclear starburst, but most preferably by an episodically powered precessing jet that changes its orientation Kh06 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk6 as a Seyfert with misaligned parsec-scale jets, two perpendicular lobe systems, low star formation, and shock-heated gas from an AGN outflow.

quotes.quote: Phase-referenced VLBI observations at 1.6 and 4.9 GHz of two Seyfert galaxies with lobes, namely NGC6764 and Mrk6, have revealed the presence of parsec-scale core-jet structures in them Kharb10a,Kharb14 . These jets show a large misalignment ( 50^ 60^ ) with the kpc-scale lobes.

Mrk6 possesses not just one set of radio lobes but two of them aligned perpendicular to each other Kharb06 . A flip in the jet direction powering the lobes can explain both sets of lobes. Star-formation rates (SFR) derived from Herschel (L250 m) data imply a SFR <0.8 M_ yr ^ 1 in Mrk 6. This is much lower than the SFR of 33 M_ yr ^ 1 expected if the lobes were inflated by starburst superwinds. Chandra observations of Mrk6 and NGC6764 indicate that the X-ray emitting gas in these Seyferts is shock-heated by an AGN outflow Mingo11,Croston08 .

sources.summary: The paper reports reverberation mapping of Mrk 6, emphasizing its changed H lag and line width and the complex velocity-resolved BLR lag structure.

quotes.quote: It has been known for more than 40 years that the \ profile of Mrk 6 is extremely asymmetric and has a blueshifted peak with the velocity of 3000 km s ^ 1 khachikian1971 . With its strange radio morphology (jet flips and jet precession as reported by, e.g., kukula1996, kharb2014 ), Mrk 6 is suggested to be a potential supermassive binary black hole system kharb2014 . Mrk 6 has been spectroscopically monitored by different groups in the past decades sergeev1999, doroshenko2003, doroshenko2012, grier2012 .

In our observation, the new \ time lag is 18.5_ 2.4 ^ +2.5 days in the rest frame, which is more similar to the result of . Moreover, the line width (FWHM =5274 km s ^ 1 ) in our rms spectrum is similar to the value (5445 km s ^ 1 ) in , and much smaller than the number (9744 km s ^ 1 ) in . These changes might be simply due to the BLR ``breathing'' effect e.g., peterson2002, korista2004, cackett2006 .
The velocity-resolved time lag measurements of Mrk 6 also shows very complex structures doroshenko2012, grier2013 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Mrk 6 as a radio-loud Seyfert in a lenticular galaxy, reporting XMM-Newton evidence for both ultrafast outflow and warm absorber disk-wind components.

quotes.quote: Mrk 6 is the Seyfert 1.5-type AGN in a lenticular galaxy Kharb14 . B band magnitude and 6 cm radio flux ( F_ 6 cm ) of Mrk 6 is B=15.16 and F_ 6 cm = 0.115 Jy Veron2010 , Mrk 6 can be classified as a radio-loud galaxy ( R = 30 ). Specifically, it has a black hole mass of (1.8 0.2) 10^8 M_ Doroshenko2012 with z = 0.019 Vaucouleurs1991 . In Mehdipour19 , the X-ray data analysis of the European Photon Imaging Camera EPIC, Struder2001, Turner2001 and Reflection Grating Spectrometer RGS, Herder2001 detectors on the XMM-Newton satellite for 16 radio galaxies reported WAs, among which Mrk 6 exhibited the highest density of WAs.

This study analyzed the disk wind of the radio galaxy Mrk 6 using XMM-Newton archival data with the SPEX code. Based on the results of the M3 model, the existence of both UFOs and WAs was suggested in Mrk 6. In addition to the radio galaxies 3C 390.3, 4C +74.26, Mrk 6 is the third radio galaxy in which both UFOs and WAs have been observed.

sources.summary: The paper describes Markarian 6 as a nearby Seyfert early-type galaxy whose double radio bubbles drive shocked X-ray shells, variable absorption, and strong feedback on the host-galaxy ISM.

quotes.quote: Markarian 6 is a nearby (D 78 Mpc) Seyfert 1.5, early-type galaxy, with a double set of radio bubbles. The outer set spans 7.5 kpc and is expanding into the halo regions of the host galaxy. We present an analysis of our new Chandra observation, together with archival XMM-Newton data, to look for evidence of emission from shocked gas around the external radio bubbles, both from spatially resolved regions in Chandra and from spectral analysis of the XMM data.

We conclude that there is a high probability that we are observing shells of strongly shocked gas around both radio bubbles of Mrk 6, with Mach numbers 3.2-5.5 consistent with the Rankine-Hugoniot conditions for a strong shock. This is the third clear detection of such a process in a low-power radio source, and therefore reinforces the hypothesis that this mechanism may be very common and may play a fundamental role in the process by which these young radio sources form their stellar populations. It is very likely that much of the gas in the shells will escape the gravitational potential of the host galaxy.

sources.summary: VLBI observations of Mrk 6 detect a compact inverted-spectrum radio core, jet-like components, and evidence that AGN jets formed changing radio lobes within about 10 million years.

quotes.quote: We present the results of high resolution VLBI observations at 1.6 and 4.9 GHz of the radio-loud Seyfert galaxy, Mrk 6. These observations are able to detect a compact radio core in this galaxy for the first time. The core has an inverted spectral index ( ^ 1.6 _ 4.9 =+1.0 0.2) and a brightness temperature of 1 10^8 K. Three distinct radio components which resemble jet elements and/or hot spots, are also detected. The position angles of these elongated jet elements point, not only to a curved jet in Mrk 6, but also towards a connection between the AGN and the kpc-scale radio lobes/bubbles in this galaxy.
We suggest that the jet axis has changed at least once in Mrk 6 within the last 10^7 yr .

sources.summary: The paper presents Mkn6 as a complex radio source with kpc jets and bubbles; its new maps show N-S elongation, a resolved double structure, and steep spectrum.

quotes.quote: Mkn6 Previous VLA studies reveal that Mkn 6 possesses radio emission on three spatial scales: i) 1 kpc scale jet NE-SW direction, with two components 1 arcsec apart; ii) bubbles on scales 1.5 kpc E-W direction; iii) bubbles on scales 4.5 kpc Kharb2006 . This complex structure may be due to either a jet precession and/or the jet interaction with ambient interstellar medium. Kharb2014 performed VLBA observations spotting the presence of a core with a high brightness temperature ( T_B 10 ^8 K), interpreted as the optically-thick base of the jet, the other components of the jets being elongated and having steeper spectra.

Our radio maps reveal a resolved morphology elongated in the N-S direction. The double structure seen by e.g. Ulvestad et al. is seen only in the full-resolution Q -band maps, with a separation of 1 arcsec. Considering the 22 GHz and the naturally-weighted, untapered Q-band maps, the flux densities are of 9.7 and 16.6 and the spectral index is steep, compatible with previous studies at similar resolution e.g. vanderHulstCraneKeel981 .

sources.summary: The paper uses a two-partially-covering-absorber model for MKN 6, finding only small luminosity differences and listing summed absorber columns for statistics.

quotes.quote: MKN 6 A detailed spectroscopic analysis of this object is given by Stefan2003 , who analyzed the same XMM-Newton data combined with Beppo-SAX data. Their joint analysis preferred a model (Model 4) with a fixed-index power law ( =1.81) and two partially covering neutral absorbers (56 2 of 8.1 0.5 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 and 91 1 of 2.3 0.1 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ), with a very small reflection component. We have re-analyzed the XMM-Newton using this model and checked the spectral fitting results, when was set to free parameter.

We found a slightly harder spectral index and small column density, but the difference of derived intrinsic X-ray luminosity is less than 10 . This has negligible effects on our HXLF and other statistical conclusions. We listed the sum of column densities of the dual absorbing gas and = 1.81 and used the spectral shape of Stefan2003 's Model 4 for the absorption correction.

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA imaging of Mrk 6, finding bubbles and a radio jet on several scales and favoring an episodically powered precessing jet over starburst winds.

quotes.quote: We have carried out an extensive radio study with the Very Large Array on the Seyfert 1.5 galaxy Mrk 6 and imaged a spectacular radio structure in the source. The radio emission occurs on three different spatial scales, from 7.5 kpc bubbles to 1.5 kpc bubbles lying nearly orthogonal to them and a 1 kpc radio jet lying orthogonal to the kpc-scale bubble. To explain the complex morphology, we first consider a scenario in which the radio structures are the result of superwinds ejected by a nuclear starburst.

However, recent Spitzer observations of Mrk 6 provide an upper limit to the star formation rate (SFR) of 5.5 M_ yr ^ 1 , an estimate much lower than the SFR of 33 M_ yr ^ 1 derived assuming that the bubbles are a result of starburst winds energized by supernovae explosions. Thus, a starburst alone cannot meet the energy requirements for the creation of the bubbles in Mrk 6. We show that a single plasmon model is energetically infeasible, and we argue that a jet-driven bubble model while energetically feasible does not produce the complex radio morphologies.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 6 as a bright-nucleus Seyfert 1.5 whose ionized gas explains much, but not all, of the residual extended near-UV emission after nuclear subtraction.

quotes.quote: Mrk 6 (Sy1.5) This is a Sy1 galaxy with a bright nucleus. After the best nuclear subtraction some positive residuals are left, which are seen as a compact, though slightly extended, source in the convolved image. The factor f is relatively small for this object. A value of 0.2 accounts for the brightest regions and blobs to the north and south of the nucleus. At the same time it does not leave strong negative residuals, as f=0.3 does.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 6 profile changes and H lag behavior as examples supporting anisotropic or off-axis continuum variability rather than matter redistribution.

quotes.quote: Different parts of line profiles show different responses to observed continuum variability zheng+91,sergeev+00,sergeev+01,sergeev+02,shapovalova+04 . sergeev+99 pointed out that the changes they observed in the Balmer-line profiles of Mrk 6 shapes cannot be caused either by matter redistribution or light-travel-time effects. They therefore made the important suggestion that they are probably caused by changes in the anisotropy of the ionizing continuum.

These theoretical curves can be compared with the four best examples of a strong velocity dependence in the H lag: the 1993-1995 monitoring of Mrk 6 sergeev+99 , the 2007 monitoring of NGC 3516 and NGC 3227 denney+09b , and the 2008 monitoring of Mrk 40 bentz+08 . NGC 3227 shows the opposite sign of the velocity dependence to the other three, so it has been plotted with the velocity scale reversed. The four AGNs have different line widths and total lags. To facilitate comparisons the lags of both observations and models have been normalized to a 15-day lag at zero velocity.
Mrk 6, for example, showed a much less marked red-wing/blue-wing difference in 1996-1997 sergeev+99 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 6 as a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy with early-detected emission-line variability, complex radio emission, complex nuclear X-ray absorption, and new optical spectroscopic monitoring from 1998 to 2008.

quotes.quote: Mrk 6 is a Seyfert 1.5 galaxy (Sy 1.5). This galaxy was one of the first galaxies in which the strong variability of the emission line profile was detected by KhW71 . Although there have not been many optical observations of Mrk 6, there are some radio studies of Mrk 6 available Kuk96, Kharb06 , which revealed the complex structure of the radio emission. The X-ray emission from the Mrk 6 nucleus Feldmeier99, Malizia03, Immler03, Schurch06 exhibits a complex X-ray absorption and some authors assume that the BLR is a possible location of this absorption complex Malizia03 . In this paper we present the results of our optical spectroscopic observations of Mrk 6 during the monitoring campaign from 1998 to 2008 performed after publishing our earlier papers with observations made from 1970--1997.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 6 as a Seyfert with an S-shaped radio jet, perpendicular kiloparsec-scale structures, an inverted-spectrum VLBA core, and precessing AGN jet episodes explaining its complex radio morphology.

quotes.quote: Mrk 6 is one such Seyfert galaxy showing an S-shaped radio jet in MERLIN observations Kukula96 . It is a peculiar galaxy in that it shows two sets of KSRs aligned nearly perpendicular to each other Kharb06 . Our two-frequency (1.6, 5 GHz) VLBA observations of Mrk 6 detected an inverted spectrum radio core at the higher frequency for the first time in this source, and resolved the knots in the S-shaped MERLIN jet into elongated jet-like features Kharb14a . The precessing jet model of could fit the parsec-scale emission as well as the bright edges of the north-south oriented KSR. Two episodes of AGN jet activity with precessing jets could explain the entire complex structure observed in Mrk 6.

sources.summary: The paper applies polarized broad-line reverberation mapping to the Type 1 AGN Mrk 6, describing it as a z=0.019 S0/Sy 1.5 galaxy with a roughly 100-light-day scattering region.

quotes.quote: The method was, for the first time, applied to the observations of the Type 1 AGN Mrk 6, and we found that the size of the scattering region in this AGN is around 100 light days. That is significantly smaller than the dusty region size estimated by the infrared interferometric observations and also larger than known broad line region (BLR) size. This indicates that the scattering region lies between the BLR and the dusty region and could be used as a probe of the dust sublimation radius.

Mrk 6 ( z=0.019 ) is a well-known S0 galaxy with an active nucleus of the intermediate Sy 1.5 type OK76 . In order to check the method availability we have used the spectropolarimetric data of Mrk 6 obtained within the 2010-2013 monitoring campaign Af14 .

sources.summary: The review describes spectropolarimetric monitoring of Mrk 6, giving BLR and polarized-continuum delays and using its H-line polarization to infer Keplerian BLR motion.

quotes.quote: The spectropolarimetric monitoring was conducted at BTA telescope for Sy 1.5 Mrk 6 (Afanasiev et al. 2014) and Sy 1 3C 390.3 (Afanasiev et al. 2015). According to the obtained observational data it was found for Mrk 6 that the delay in broad H _ line relative to the continuum at 5100 \ is 22 days, which corresponds to the size of the BLR region - 22 lt days (it is consistent with the reverberation mapping results by Bentz Katz (2015)). As well, the polarized continuum at 5100 \ shows only 2 days delay Yet the observations sampling was greater than 2 days (there were 13 epochs at 1000 days, the method of interpolated cross-correlation function was applied.
The example of polarized observations of H line in Mrk 6 is demonstrated in Fig. mrk6 .

Thus, observing clouds rotating at Keplerian orbits, the obtained dependence (v_ i /) vs. ( ( _i)) must be linear, which was discovered firstly by modeling, then by observations in the broad H _ line in Mrk 6 (Afanasiev et al. 2014) and in 3C 390.3 (Afanasiev et al. 2015), and for for 35 Sy 1 galaxies (Afanasiev et al. 2019).

sources.summary: The paper maps Mrk 6's central ionized gas, describing its strong-emission region, velocity field, and signs of nuclear outflow, rotation, density structure, or inflow.

quotes.quote: This Seyfert 1 galaxy shows the SER more extended along the N-S direction and a distorted rotation pattern in the emitting gas, as previously pointed out by Freitas2018 . The rotation component seems to have a KMA following the orientation of the PMA, more clearly seen in . In , the increase in the blueshifts to the North and redshifts to the South, indicate the presence of an outflow making an angle of 50^ with the PMA. This N-S outflow is approximately co-spatial with a radio structure seen in a 3.6 cm image Freitas2018 . High values of are observed over most of the FoV, and being highest in two regions aligned almost perpendicularly to the radio-structure and outflow, as in NGC 2110 and NGC 1386, probably due again to an equatorial outflow produced by the passage of the radio jet.

sources.summary: The review uses Mrk 6 as an AGN example where broad-line polarization constrains BLR size and where polarized-line reverberation implies a smaller scattering radius than K-band IR reverberation.

quotes.quote: For example, in Fig. fig02 , the maximal angle for Mrk 6 is around 40^ , that gives maximal dimension of the BLR is close to the inner part of the torus (the place of dust sublimation), i.e. R^ max 0.8R_ sc . This can be expected since at the edge of the BLR the flux density of the central source stays weaker, and photoionization rate becomes smaller.

Note here, that in the case of Mrk 6, the reverberation in polarized broad H line gives significantly smaller R_ sc (around 100 light days), that is two times smaller than obtained from the infra-red reverberation measured in the K-band ki11 . This fact indicates that scattering region is not the same as the photometric infrared (IR) region. This can be expected, since the IR reverberation show the dimension of the region that has maximal emission in the K-band (2.2 m ) and this region is probably larger than the innermost part of the tours.

sources.summary: The paper maps Mrk 6 ionized gas, finding faint 40 kpc filaments whose kinematics suggest externally accreted material illuminated by the active nucleus.

quotes.quote: Using various techniques of optical observations at the 6-m Russian telescope (emission-line images, long-slit and 3D spectroscopy), we have studied large-scale morphology and kinematics of the ionized gas in the Seyfert galaxy Mrk 6. Having a significantly deeper images and spectra than those in previous works, we have not only mapped the ionized gas in the stellar disc but also found a system of faint filaments elongated in the NE direction up to a projected distance of 40 kpc ( 4.3R_ 25 ). Kinematics as well as an ionization state of the filament gas suggest the scenario that hard radiation of the active nucleus illuminated the matter accreted from outside and orbiting almost orthogonally to the Mrk 6 stellar disc. A possible source of gas accretion is still unknown, a deep image taken with the 1-m Byrakan Schmidt telescope does not show any stellar counterparts at the level of 27--28 mag arcsec ^ 2 .

sources.summary: The paper finds broad ionised reflection in Mrk 6, improves Model SRC constraints, and says this observation marginally prefers Model ION over Model SRI.

quotes.quote: Evidence for a broad and ionised reflection component at 95 confidence is found in five observations: MCG+8-11-11, Mrk 110, NGC 7469(1), Mrk 6 and NGC 7213, with the first three being significant even at 99 confidence.
While the disc inclinations of Mrk 6 and NGC 5506(1) do not change from their Model N07 best-fit values, better constraints are placed on Mrk 6 with Model SRC.
The exceptions are Mrk 6 and NGC 7213, with the former marginally preferring Model ION over Model SRI.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 6 as the comparison Seyfert with established perpendicular radio lobes, parsec-scale jet evidence, and episodic AGN activity similar to NGC 4235.

quotes.quote: Sensitive multi-frequency VLA observations have detected two pairs of radio lobes/KSRs, perpendicular to each other, in the ``radio-loud'' Seyfert galaxy, Mrk 6 Kharb06 . (We will use KSRs and radio lobes interchangeably in the remaining text.) Very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) has revealed a high brightness temperature ( T_b 1 10^8 K), inverted spectral index core and jet in Mrk 6; this has similarly been observed in other Seyfert galaxies e.g., Falcke00,Ulvestad01,Kharb10,Kharb15 . These studies support the suggestion that radio lobes in Seyferts are AGN-driven and nonthermal (synchrotron) in origin. Additionally, Mrk 6 shows signatures of episodic AGN activity.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mkn 6 as a bright Seyfert galaxy in the field, with an IBIS/ISGRI spectral slope and flux level consistent with BeppoSAX PDS measurements.

quotes.quote: In the same field of our primary target we also detected the high redshift blazar S5 0836+710, belonging to the FSRQ sub-class ( ), and two bright Seyfert galaxies, Mkn 3 and Mkn 6, all with brighter fluxes than S5 0716+714 (Table 1).

The IBIS/ISGRI spectral slope of the Seyfert Mkn 6 is flatter, although consistent with that measured by the BeppoSAX PDS ( ; ), within our rather large errors. Similarly, the flux level in the IBIS/ISGRI observation is consistent with that measured by the PDS.

gmw_00001324.name: NGC 4565
gmw_00001324.overview: NGC 4565 is a nearby, edge-on barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1210.0609, arXiv:0907.4154] classified as a Seyfert 1.9 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1503.01836, arXiv:astro-ph/0611185]. It features a warped, marginally stable disk with a low star formation rate [arXiv:1012.0816, arXiv:1408.5905] and is undergoing a minor interaction with its companion IC 3571 [arXiv:1012.0816].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 4565 as a nearby low-SFR edge-on spiral important for extraplanar H i studies, with no massive global H i halo but vertical dispersion and an inner lag.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Kinematic Center (J2000.0) & 12h 36m 21s &This work Kinematic Center (J2000.0) & 25d 59m 10s &This work

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 NGC 4565 is classified as a SAb galaxy 1991trcb.book.....D with a SFR of 0.67 M _ yr ^ 1 calculated in 2012A&A...544C...1H using total infrared (TIR) flux measurements. This places it near the low star-forming end of the HALOGAS sample. However, based on its rotation curve, it is among the most massive, which would presumably hinder gas being ejected to large heights above the disk. This combination gives it substantial importance in determining overall trends involving extra-planar H i and star formation. At a distance of 10.8 Mpc 2011A&A...526A.118H , NGC 4565 is relatively nearby, and among the best studied edge-on spirals.

The Lag in NGC 4565 and Other Galaxies We do not detect substantial extra-planar H i in the form of a global halo, which indicates that there is no strong disk-halo cycling in NGC 4565. However, the H i has some vertical velocity dispersion and experiences a lag when it rises above the plane of the disk. The lag we detect is within the innermost 4.75' (14.9 kpc) and 4.25' (13.4 kpc) in the approaching and receding halves, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4565’s edge-on disk truncation, detecting it up to 4 kpc, finding radius changes with height, and connecting it to warp and star-formation thresholds.

quotes.quote: Our goal is to determine whether truncations can be detected above 3 kpc height in the Milky Way-like galaxy NGC 4565, thus establishing the actual disk thickness. We also aim to study how the truncation relates to disk properties such as star formation activity or the warp. We perform a vertical study of the disk of NGC 4565 edge in unprecedented detail. We explore the truncation radius at different heights above/below the disk mid-plane ( 0<z<8 kpc) and at different wavelengths.

We detect the truncation up to 4 kpc in the g , and i ultra-deep bands which is 1 kpc higher than in any previous study for any galaxy. The radial position of the truncation remains constant up to 3 kpc while higher up it is located at a smaller radius. This result is independent of the wavelength but is affected by the presence of the warp. We propose an inside-out growth scenario for the formation of the disk of NGC 4565.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4565 as a large edge-on Milky-Way-like spiral with detected H I, a warped disk, a minor interaction with IC 3571, and a modest radio continuum halo.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 is a large, edge-on spiral galaxy which is similar to the Milky Way. The new HALOGAS observations are combined with archival data presented by dahlem_etal_2005 . The other two galaxies in the frame are NGC 4562, a small companion classified in the RC3 as type SB(s)dm and visible to the southwest of NGC 4565, and IC 3571, the small galaxy located very close to NGC 4565 on its north side. In the PV diagram shown in Figure figure:pvdiagram_n4565 , the vertically extended emission in NGC 4565 is seen to have a smooth velocity gradient directly toward the systemic velocity of IC 3571.

The total \ mass detected in NGC 4565 itself is 7.43 10^9 M_ . The galaxy is obviously warped: this can be seen in the total \ map (particularly obvious on the north end of the disk) and also in the first-moment image contours. NGC 4562 has a regularly rotating \ disk with a total \ mass of 1.82 10^8 M_ . IC 3571 is also detected in \ ( M_ HI = 4.18 10^7 M_ ), and the \ data show clear signs of a minor interaction between it and NGC 4565.

sources.summary: The review describes NGC 4565 as an edge-on B/P-bulge galaxy with cylindrical rotation, boxy or X-shaped infrared morphology, and a massive boxy bulge component.

quotes.quote: Support for the scenario by Combes Sanders came from the observation that NGC 4565 and NGC 128, with obvious boxy bulges in morphology, appeared to have cylindrical rotation kormendyilling1982,kormendyilling1983 , which means that the rotational velocity depends only little on the vertical height from the equatorial plane.

Structural decompositions Two edge-on galaxies with B/P bulges, NGC 4565 and NGC 5746, have been decomposed into multiple structure components by kormendy2010 and barentine2012 . In the classification by buta2015 the Hubble types of these galaxies are SB _x ()ab sp, and (R')SB _x (,nd)0/a sp. In direct infrared images the bulges in both galaxies clearly have boxy or even X-shape morphology. The surface brightness profiles were decomposed into an exponential disk, and two bulges (a 'boxy bulge' and a 'disky pseudobulge') fitted with separate S ersic functions. In both galaxies the boxy bulges were assumed to be bars seen in nearly end-on view.
In NGC 4565 the boxy bulge-to-total mass ratio B_ boxy /T 0.4, and B_ disky /T 0.06 (i.e., a 'disky pseudobulge' in our notation).

sources.summary: The paper examines NGC 4565 as an edge-on barred galaxy with a peanut-shaped bulge, estimating its vertical disk scale-height profile and comparing it to bar models.

quotes.quote: To verify whether this limit is realistic, we examined the vertical profile of the edge-on galaxy NGC 4565 (see Fig. vert_thick_edge_on ), which is known to host a prominent peanut-shaped bulge 1986AJ.....91...65J . For this particular case, we estimated the disk scale height from the line-of-sight vertical dispersion: h_ z (x)= 1 2 <z^2> = 1 2 _ z_ s ^ z_ s S(x,z)z^2dz _ z_ s ^ z_ s S(x,z)dz , where x and z are the axial and vertical distances, S is the flux density, and z_ s is the height above which 90 of the light emission comes from the thick disk taken from 2012ApJ...759...98C . Varying z_ s by a factor 2 has no significant effect on the final h_ z radial profile, as shown in the same figure. We obtain a mean h_ z value in the bar region ( 7 arcsec) consistent with 2012ApJ...759...98C , who performed fits to the vertical luminosity profiles in the disk region outside the peanut-shaped bar area. More importantly, we observe a difference between the maximum h_ z at the peanut-shaped bulge and that of the surrounding thin bar/disk ( <50 ) that is much lower than the above tested values.

In the same figure we also show the force profile of NGC 4548 assuming it has the same scale height as NGC 4565, under the assumption that the peanut-shaped part covers one half of the bar size 2000A&A...362..435L . These two galaxies supposedly have similar bar lengths, and both are classified as Sa in B2015.

sources.summary: The paper discusses NGC 4565 as a well-studied warped, thin, disk-dominated galaxy whose measured stellar flaring improves the warp-onset model match.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565: NGC4565 is a very well studied galaxy in the literature. The disc in the 4.5 micron band is clearly warped. It also has an extended optical warp (Naeslund Joersaeter 1997).

The thickness parameter (see Table 4) indicates that the disc is fairly thin. Using the disc parameters, obtained from the photometric analysis, we model the CO + HI rotation curve obtained from Sofue (1997). The modelled bulge of the galaxy is quite massive, but NGC4565 is a disc dominated galaxy (see Table 1).

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4565 needs two disks plus a ring and bulge/nuclear components, lacks a required super-thin disk, and shows little extraplanar dust.

quotes.quote: To avoid NGC 4565's obvious inner truncation we first restrict our models to fit outside of R =5 kpc. We find as do Comeron11 that only two disks are needed to model most of the light outside of this radius, except for the contribution from the ring-like structure visible in Figure fig:ngc4565attencorr . We therefore chose to change our models to ones with two disks and a ring, adding to our fitting software a gaussian ring with the same functional description as used in deLooze12 : I(R,z) = _ 0 _ ^ exp &- ( R^ 2 +t^ 2 sin ^ 2 i R_ 0 )^ 2 2 _ 0 ^ 2 & |z sin i-t cos i| h_ z dt, where R_ 0 is the central ring radius and _ 0 is the radial dispersion of the ring.

For example, despite frequently being named as a good analog to the MW e.g. vanderKruit84,Shaw89,Jones89 , no super-thin component is necessary to fit NGC 4565's vertical light profile. NGC 4565 is known to be one of the few massive galaxies with little extraplanar dust, a feature which usually signifies outflows from young, massive stars Howk99 . A ring-like stellar feature with a radius of 4.5 kpc is clearly seen in NGC 4565 from even a cursory visual inspection of the attenuation-corrected NIR image; the ring has a thickness comparable to that of NGC 4565's thin disk.

sources.summary: The paper reports resolved stellar-halo detections for NGC 4565 to about 60 kpc, measures steep minor- and major-axis profiles, estimates halo mass, and identifies a thin outer stream.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 As far as we are aware, this work, , and preliminary results from are the first reported detection and characterisation of NGC 4565's resolved stellar halo. NGC 4565's stellar halo is detected out to 60 kpc along the minor axis, and more than 50 kpc along the major axis. NGC 4565's minor axis density profile has a power law slope of 2.87^ +0.08 _ 0.07 0.2 (random and systematic uncertainties respectively), and while the fit prefers 0.11 0.03 dex of intrinsic scatter, owing to the magnitude of the error bars in the outer parts of NGC 4565 it is also consistent with having no intrinsic scatter around its minor axis profile. NGC 4565 has a substantially steeper major axis profile, with a power law slope of 5.28^ +0.47 _ 0.45 0.2 .

The calculated stellar mass between 10 and 40 minor axis equivalent kpc is 7.2 0.3 2.2 10^8 M_ , corresponding to a estimated total stellar halo mass of 2.2 0.9 10^9 M_ or 2.8 1.2 of the total stellar mass of NGC 4565. We note that Field 06 (the outermost minor axis field in NGC 4565) has a significant overdensity (the high datapoint at minor axis radius 57 kpc), which we interpret to be a relatively thin stellar stream (also discussed in M16a ).

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 4565 with NGC 891, finding corrected C-band maps, diffusion-dominated halo transport, and high-resolution radio structure in its ring or inner arms.

quotes.quote: The two nearby spiral galaxies, NGC 891 and NGC 4565, differ largely in their detectable halo extent and their star formation rates. The question is how these differences are related to the (advective and/or diffusive) CRE transport in the disk and halo. In C-band, the total power emission of both galaxies has been corrected for missing zero spacing flux with the single-dish 100-m Effelsberg telescope. After subtracting off the thermal emission, maps of the total magnetic field strength and the nonthermal spectral indices as well as vertical nonthermal scale heights at different radii could be determined XVI .

For NGC 4565, however, the halo is diffusion-dominated up to a height of at least 1 kpc with a diffusion coefficient being only weakly energy dependent. The high-resolution total intensity radio map of NGC 4565 in C-band has a linear resolution of about 200 pc that allows the possibility of separating both sides of the large radio ring (which could also represent tightly wound inner spiral arms) in the disk, as shown in Figure  fig:n4565 .

sources.summary: The paper reports extended emission along NGC 4565's plane and a nuclear region dominated by two X-ray sources, including a brighter off-nuclear source with fitted spectrum.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 shows extended emission along the galactic plane. NGC 4565 (S1.9) . --- The nuclear region is dominated by two sources: the nucleus and an off-nuclear source which is brighter than the nucleus. The observed fluxes of these two source in the 2--10 keV band ( 3.2 10^ 13 and 5.8 10^ 13 ) are slightly lower than those obtained with ( 5.3 10^ 13 and 1.1 10^ 12 ; Mizuno et al. 1999; Terashima et al. 2002b), respectively.

These differences appear not to be significant given the statistical, calibration, and spectral-modeling uncertainties. The spectrum of the off-nuclear source can be fitted by an absorbed power law model with a photon index of =1.89^ +0.24 _ 0.19 and = 1.5^ +0.5 _ 0.3 10^ 21 . A multicolor disk blackbody model also provides a good fit with best-fit parameters kT_ in =0.90^ +0.14 _ 0.16 keV and = 4.2^ +3.5 _ 4.0 10^ 20 .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4565 as a nearby Seyfert 1.9 LLAGN and RIAF candidate, with visible optical nuclear radiation and low-luminosity accretion onto a supermassive black hole.

quotes.quote: We we further tested this picture by studying the nuclear spectral energy distribution of a galaxy, NGC 4565, that seems to be a perfect candidate for hosting a RIAF around the central supermassive black hole. The object is part of the ``Palomar sample'' of LLAGN , and it is included in both the and samples that were used to define the ``fundamental plane of black hole activity''. It is worth mentioning that NGC 4565 does not show any significant peculiarity in that plane.

NGC 4565 is a nearby (d=9.7 Mpc) LLAGN classified as a Seyfert 1.9 because of the possible presence of a faint, relatively broad (FWHM = 1750 km s ^ 1 ) H line. Although it is a Type 2 Seyfert, this object is only moderately absorbed, and the nuclear radiation is visible in the optical spectral region. NGC 4565 may thus represent the first clear example of low-luminosity accretion onto a supermassive black hole in the optical band.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4565 as a key edge-on case where truncation was first noticed, with a bulge, a moderate stellar warp, and little major-axis growth at faint levels.

quotes.quote: In the edge-on systems the truncation is seen best as a lack of growing diameter with fainter light levels. In fact, that is how it was first noted in NGC 4565 (van der Kruit, 1979). Freeman (2006) has reviewed the subject recently and documents that although truncations as in NGC 4565 are common, some (not edge-on) galaxies without truncations do exist, e.g. NGC 300 (Bland-Hawthorn et al. 2005).

Fig1 I show two of these galaxies to illustrate the way these data have been analysed. These are NGC 4565 (in which actually the evidence for truncations was first noticed) and NGC 5907. The first has a bulge, but the second is probably almost entirely disk.

The minor axes do grow significantly at faint levels, especially in the case of NGC 4565 due to the relatively prominent bulge. The stellar disk of NGC 4565 also has a moderate warp (van der Kruit \ Searle, 1981a; see also Wu et al., 2002) in the same direction as in the .

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 4565 as one of four S0/Sa disc galaxies with detailed kinematics, finding an isotropic bulge-plus-disc model fits without requiring an inner dark halo.

quotes.quote: The model is successfully applied to reproduce the stellar kinematic and photometry of the bulge of the 4 lenticular and early-type spiral galaxies NGC 4565, NGC 7814, NGC 5866 and NGC 4594. For these galaxies detailed stellar kinematical data are available at different positions across the galaxy. The application of our models shows that: 1) For all the galaxies considered in this work, an isotropic model is able to reproduce the whole dynamical data.

This is surprising in the special case of NGC 4565, for which previous models were unable to reproduce the velocity distribution without introducing strong anisotropies. 2) We do not need a dark mass halo to reproduce these data. This do not mean that a dark halo is not present, but just that its dynamical effects in these inner regions of the galaxies are negligible.

sources.summary: The review presents NGC 4565 as an edge-on Sb galaxy with dwarf companions, an asymmetric warp, and H I distortions pointing toward a companion.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 is a large edge-on galaxy of Hubble type Sb with a dwarf companion 6 ' ( 30 kpc) to the north of the center, F378-0021557, which has 7.4 10^ 7 of \ compared to an \ mass of 2.0 10^ 10 for NGC 4565 (using a distance of 17 Mpc) . An \ detection of this same companion has also been reported (with the name NGC 4565A) by Rupen (1991) . Another companion, NGC 4562, somewhat more massive in \ ( 2.5 10^ 8 ) and brighter optically is located 15 ' ( 75 kpc) to the south-west of the center of NGC 4565. The \ distribution, derived (by us) from a new sensitive observation with the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope (WSRT) by Dahlem et al. \ (2005) , is shown in Figure f_n4565 (top left panel) superposed on the DSS. The asymmetric warp is clearly visible.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4565 as an edge-on barred spiral; despite degraded BLAST05 optics, BLAST resolved it, made a 0.4 deg^2 observation, and produced beam-deconvolved maps.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 NGC 4565, presented in Fig. fig:ngc4565 , is an edge-on barred-spiral galaxy in the constellation Coma Berenices. It has an apparent size of about 12 \ 2 5. Although degraded by the large point-spread functions of BLAST05 186--189 , truch2008 , NGC 4565 is still resolved by BLAST. BLAST made one observation of a 0.4 deg ^2 area centered on NGC 4565, with a total integration time of 49 minutes. A beam-deconvolved image, using the method described in 2.4 of chapin2008 , is shown in the bottom row of Fig. fig:ngc4565 .

sources.summary: The paper finds central CO deficiency and ring-like morphology in NGC 4565, notes possible AGN-heated dust, and describes its disk as marginally stable with relatively low star formation.

quotes.quote: Nevertheless, the index of NGC 4565 is still smaller, but it is not much unexpected since the CO emission of NGC 4565 is deficient in the central regions as shown in the CO map (ring-like morphology) and the gradient of the 24 \ profile is flatter than the others. In addition, NGC 4565 has an AGN, which can heat the dust to produce 24 \ even without much star formation.
In particular, NGC 4565 shows much lower values for the radial profiles, due to its ring-like morphology leading to lower surface densities, especially in the central region.

In this regime, two galaxies (NGC 891 and 4157) show unstable regions predicting star formation in the inner disk, while the other galaxies (NGC 4565 and 5907) show a marginally stable disk. Considering the relatively low SFR in NGC 4565 and 5907 compared to the SFR in NGC 891 and 4157, the Q_ tot profile with constant velocity dispersions shows a reasonable prediction for the massive star formation.

sources.summary: The paper bins NGC 4565 fields to comparable spatial scales, finds a stellar-halo colour gradient, and notes a redder minor-axis colour jump near 38 kpc that may trace substructure.

quotes.quote: However, NGC 0891, NGC 4565 and NGC 7814 are at further distances, indicated in Table table:trgb . Therefore, the FoVs of the fields located around these galaxies cover larger linear extensions, having side lengths from 9 to 14 kpc. In order to obtain colour measurements that represent the properties of stars from similar spatial regions, we divide each field of the more distant galaxies in either three or four radial bins, such that each region for which a colour measurement is made covers 3 kpc on a side.

Half of the galaxies (NGC 4565, NGC 0891, and NGC 7814) show fits consistent with stellar halo colour gradients whereas the remaining three galaxies (NGC 0253, NGC 3031, and NGC 4945) have rather flat colour profile. However, it is interesting to note that both NGC 0891 and NGC 4565 show a jump, i.e. a redder colour, in the minor axis colour profile at approximately 38 kpc, which may be related to substructure in these galaxies likely either in the form of a stellar stream or shell.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4565 as having steeper non-thermal spectra, weaker magnetic fields, low CRE injection rates, and diffusion-dominated cosmic-ray transport rather than a strong wind.

quotes.quote: The non-thermal spectral index in NGC 4565 is consistently steeper than in NGC 891. Between 1.5 and 6 GHz, the disc-averaged non-thermal spectral index is 0.85 0.09 in NGC 891 and 1.10 0.14 in NGC 4565. The overall non-thermal spectral index distribution of NGC 891 is mostly consistent with continuous CRE injection. In NGC 4565, most of the local synchrotron spectra (also in the disc) feature a break between 1.5 and 6 GHz and are thus more in line with discrete-epoch injection (JP or KP models). This implies low CRE injection rates (as expected due to the low SFR and _ SFR ), which are possibly lower than the synchrotron loss rates. We detect a thin radio ring associated with the previously known ring of molecular gas and dust. The ring has a vertical thickness of 260 100 pc and a width in the galactic plane of 2.2 1.3 kpc. The synchrotron halo scale heights of NGC 891 increase with radius, which is in line with the dumbbell shape of its radio continuum halo and indicates significant synchrotron losses of the CREs. Still, there may be regions where CRE escape losses dominate. Assuming energy equipartition, the average (maximum) total magnetic field strength in the disc of NGC 891 is 10 G (15 G). In the disc of NGC 4565 we measure 6 G (7 G). The CRE transport in the halo of NGC 891 is probably dominated by advection. This advection flow is an accelerated galactic wind of velocity 150 km s^ 1 near the mid-plane, and reaches the escape velocity at a height of 9 to 17 kpc, depending on major-axis position. Moreover, this wind is likely to coexist with diffusion-dominated regions. Vertical emission gradients are dominated by the dilution of the CRs resulting from the adiabatic expansion, while synchrotron losses cause a steepening of the spectral index with height. NGC 4565 is diffusion-dominated at least to heights of z 5mu= 5mu1 kpc (and probably throughout the halo to even larger heights), with a diffusion coefficient 2 10^ 28 cm^2 s^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4565 SBF results mostly inconclusive, but identifies likely companions and explains candidate classifications using redshifts and morphology.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 The SBF results for NGC 4565 were inconclusive, as shown in Table tab:ngc4565_sbf . Only a few galaxies could be confirmed as either satellites or background due to NGC 4565's larger distance of D=11.9 Mpc and the poor seeing in the CFHT data. NGC 4562 is irregular, such that the SBF distance is likely underestimated. Given that the redshift is within 100 km/s of NGC 4565, this galaxy is likely a companion of NGC 4565. The candidate dw1234p2531 has an SDSS redshift which is 600 km/s less than NGC 4565.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4565 as disturbed, with three companions and four anomalous or candidate gas clouds, most co-rotating and plausibly tidal debris.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 is a disturbed galaxy with three companions. We find two anomalous gas clouds in this system that are close to the galaxy and we classify them as cloud candidates. Additionally we find another two cloud candidates in this data cube as well. All companions and cloud candidates are co-rotating with the disk of the galaxy except for source 8 which is furthest removed in velocity as well as projected distance. Tidal debris is a possible origin for the co-rotating cloud candidates, given the environment of interaction.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4565 as a highly inclined Seyfert with a prominent dust lane, disk emission-line regions, no clear extraplanar emission, and possible faint filaments.

quotes.quote: The R band image of this highly-inclined Seyfert galaxy (Figure f:ngc4565 ) 20 reveals a very prominent dust lane. Single chimney-like dust structures protrude from the northern plane perpendicular to the disk into the disk-halo interface. The map of NGC 4565 shows clumpy emission line regions and diffuse ionized gas scattered through the disk. No extraplanar emission is observed, in agreement with previous observations performed by ran92 . However, there is possible evidence for some faint filaments.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4565 as an edge-on galaxy in the 6 cm scale-height sample, showing its map and contrasting its weak halo and low star formation with NGC 253.

quotes.quote: We also determined the exponential scale heights of the total power emission at 6 cm for four edge-on galaxies (NGC 253, NGC 891, NGC 3628, NGC 4565) for which we have combined interferometer and single-dish data (VLA and the 100-m Effelsberg) at this wavelength. The map of NGC 4565 is shown in Fig. n4565 . In spite of their different intensities and extents of the radio emission, the scale heights of the thin disk and the thick disk/halo are similar in this sample (300pc and 1.8kpc) (Dumke Krause 1998, Heesen et al. 2009a). We stress that our sample includes the brightest halo observed so far, NGC 253, with a very high SFR as well as one of the weakest halos, NGC 4565, with a small SFR.

sources.summary: The survey finds NGC 4565 has a -2.5 outer profile at 20 kpc, no detected disk substructure, a smoother halo than M31, and high stellar-envelope metallicity.

quotes.quote: At 20 kpc NGC 891, NGC 4565, and NGC 7814 have power law profiles with a slope of about -2.5.

In stark contrast to these clear signs of substructure, we find no signs of any substructure surrounding the disk of NGC 4565. When comparing these density reconstructions with the substructure observed around M31 on the same physical scale, the NGC 4565 halo appears to be much smoother.
The more massive galaxies (NGC 891, NGC 3031, NGC 4565, NGC 7814) have significantly higher metallicities than the lower mass galaxies (IC5052, NGC4244, NGC7793) as has been seen before e.g., Mou07 .

sources.summary: The paper maps young and old stars in the 4565 gaseous warp, finding young stars but no old population, increased star formation about 300 Myr ago, and a long gas depletion time.

quotes.quote: We have mapped the distribution of young and old stars in the gaseous warp of 4565. We find a clear correlation of young stars ( <600 Myr) with the warp, but no coincident old stars ( >1 Gyr), which places an upper limit on the age of the structure. The formation rate of the young stars, which increased 300 Myr ago relative to the surrounding regions, is (6.3^ +2.5 _ 1.5 ) 10^ 5 yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 . This implies a 60 20 Gyr depletion time of the warp, similar to the timescales calculated for the outer disks of nearby spiral galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 4565 can dominate positive Q in stacked measurements, but has no especially strong halo or X-type field and only disk-proximate RM detections.

quotes.quote: For example, NGC 4565 becomes the dominant galaxy in positive Q , depending on where the measurement is made. Moreover, NGC 4565 does not show a particularly impressive halo or X-type field (Appendix A).

In three galaxies the magnetic field vectors and RM values could only be detected along the projected disk. These are NGC 5907 and NGC 4013 (see also ), and also probably NGC 4565 (see schmidt+2019 ) where we detect magnetic field vectors and RM values only up to about 1.0 kpc distance from the midplane.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 4565 as a surveyed host with deep coverage but limited satellite characterization because distance and seeing make fainter SBF confirmations ambiguous.

quotes.quote: The sixth host, NGC 4565, had good area coverage and deep survey data, but due to its larger distance ( D=11.9 Mpc) and worse seeing, the SBF distance results were ambiguous for all but the brightest candidates. All of the candidates brighter than M_V=-12 were confirmed by SBF or redshift such that there were no ambiguous candidates in NGC 4565 at this luminosity. Thus, we include NGC 4565 as well, but we note that its satellite system is only characterized down to M_V=-12 .

Several of the observed hosts (e.g. the MW, M101, NGC 4258, NGC 4631, NGC 4565, and NGC 1023) have their profiles at or just within the 2 (i.e. more centrally concentrated) scatter in the simulations whereas no host is correspondingly outside the +2 (i.e. less concentrated) scatter in the simulations.

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 4565 as external-galaxy evidence that young stars trace H I warps while older populations remain symmetrically distributed around the mid-plane.

quotes.quote: In NGC 4565, used Hubble Space Telescope resolved stellar populations to show that the H i warp is indeed traced by young (age < 600 ) populations, while older ( > 1 ) populations are symmetrically distributed around the mid-plane. The results of and of in the MW support the view that some star formation occurs in warps, and that older stars do not trace the gas warp. These results therefore suggest that the presence of young stars in the warp is not due to bending waves, which would produce a similar warp signature in all stellar populations.
The study of stellar populations in NGC 4565 by showed that the H i warp is traced by young stars ( age 600 ), while older ( >1 ) stars are symmetrically distributed around the mid-plane.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 4565 among four galaxies whose rotation curves are fit with the modified acceleration law, reporting good agreement and the galaxy's fitted luminous mass and core radius.

quotes.quote: In Fig. 4, we show fits to the four galaxies NGC 1560, NGC 2903, NGC 4565 and NGC 5055 using the modified acceleration law ( coremodel ) including the galaxy core with =2 . As can be seen from the data fitting, the agreement with the core and extended rotation curves data is good for the two fitting parameters M and the core radius r_c . In Fig. 5, we display a 3-dimensional plot of v versus the range of distance 0.1 kpc < < 10 kpc and the range of total galaxy mass M used in the fitting of rotational velocity data.
For NGC 4565 the luminous mass is M=1.81 10^ 11 M_ , while the core radius is r_c=3.11 kpc .

sources.summary: The paper applies the same vertical disk model to NGC 4565, using observed gas densities and concluding that its stellar scaleheight flares rather than remaining radially constant.

quotes.quote: We next applied this general model to two external, prototypical edge-on spiral galaxies: NGC 891 and NGC 4565, for which gas density values are known from observations - see Narayan Jog (2002a) for details. Hence, our model is applied to NGC 891 and NGC 4565, treating the above ratio as a free parameter. The resulting stellar scaleheight is not strictly constant but instead shows flaring by a factor of 2 within the optical radius (Fig. 2).
The above model is applied to two external galaxies, NGC 891 and NGC 4565, and the resulting stellar scaleheight is shown to be not constant with radius, instead it shows flaring.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4565 as a highly inclined SAb galaxy with a central cold stellar population, weak optical and infrared emission lines, and stellar absorption features.

quotes.quote: NGC 4565 (S1.9) NGC 4565 is an SAb galaxy that is highly inclined to the line of sight. Proctor00 present evidence for a kinematically cold population at the centre of NGC 4565 that may be associated with ongoing star formation. As might be expected from the relatively weak emission lines in the optical spectrum of NGC 4565, the GNIRS spectrum (Fig.

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 4565 as a deep-imaging halo test where low-surface-brightness colors give metallicities consistent with resolved-star measurements at similar radii.

quotes.quote: For example, deep imaging of the halo of NGC 4565 by Infante-Sainz et al. (in prep) achieves color uncertainties of 0.05 mag at _r 28 , and indicates metallicities in the range [ Fe/H ] = 0.7 to 1.7 . This compares favorably to the resolved star analysis of Monachesi16 , which derives a halo metallicity of [ Fe/H ] = 1.2 0.3 for fields at similar radii. The consistency in the inferred metallicity is promising, although the quality of the match is still sensitive to systematic details (such as the population age) in the modeling of the integrated colors.

gmw_00001325.name: Leo T
gmw_00001325.overview: Leo T is a gas-rich, dark-matter-dominated transition-type dwarf galaxy located in the outskirts of the Milky Way [arXiv:0911.2792, arXiv:2101.03189]. It is one of the lowest-mass systems with ongoing star formation and contains both young and old stellar populations [arXiv:1607.03127, arXiv:2002.07311].

sources.summary: The review presents Leo T as a gas-rich ultra-faint dwarf and possible pre-reionization fossil, citing recent star formation, velocity dispersion, large distance, and possible late gas accretion.

quotes.quote: For instance, the dwarf galaxy Leo T resembles all the other ultra-faints but has gas and recent star formation Irwinetal07, SimonGeha07 . We argue that Leo T could be a true ``fossil'' with v_ max (t)<20 km s ^ 1 , but may have experienced a late phase of gas accretion and star formation due to the mechanism discussed in sec:infall . Leo T has a stellar velocity dispersion of _ Leo T = 7.5 1.6 km s ^ 1 SimonGeha07 , or an estimated dynamical mass of 10^7 M _ within the stellar spheroid (although its total halo mass may be much larger). Leo T shows no sign of recent tidal destruction by either the Milky Way or M31 deJongetal08 and is located in the outskirts of the Milky Way at a Galactocentric distance of 400 kpc. Leo T photometric properties are consistent with those of pre-reionization fossils.

On the other hand, the halo of Leo T could be sufficiently massive ( v_ max (t) >20 km s ^ 1 ) to retain or accrete mass after reionization and not be a pre-reionization fossil. However, as discussed in sec:infall it is also possible that Leo T is a pre-reionization fossil that has been able to accrete gas from the IGM at late times due to an increase in the concentration of its dark halo and a decrease in the IGM temperature Ricotti:09 . Under this scenario, Leo T stopped forming stars after reionization, but was able to start accreting gas again from the IGM at z 1-2 and therefore would have a bimodal stellar population.

sources.summary: The paper estimates Leo T’s halo mass from simulations, notes its ongoing star formation and likely low inclination, and predicts many similar gas-rich dwarfs in low-mass halos.

quotes.quote: We estimate M_ 200 for Leo T by direct comparison to the simulations in R16a. There, we showed that Leo T gave a poor match to our M_ 200 = 10^8 M _ and M_ 200 = 10^9 M _ simulations, but an excellent match to the photometric light profile; star formation history; stellar metallicity distribution function; and star/gas kinematics of our M_ 200 = 5 10^8 M _ simulation. From this comparison, we estimate M_ 200, Leo T = 3.5 - 7.5 10^8 M _ (see Table tab:data ). (A similar analysis for the Aquarius dwarf yields a mass M_ 200 10^9 M _ in good agreement with its rotation curve derived value; see Table tab:data .) For Carina, we use the pre-infall `tidal mass estimate' from 2015NatCo...6E7599U . This is derived by directly fitting N body simulations of Carina tidally disrupting in the halo of the Milky Way to data for the positions and velocities of `extra-tidal' stars reported in 2006ApJ...649..201M . Leo T is interesting because it is the lowest mass dwarf discovered to date with ongoing star formation 2008MNRAS.384..535R . In R16a, we argued that its lack of a visible HI rotation curve owes to it having a low inclination ( i < 20^ ).

Extrapolating the Bolshoi mass function to low mass, we predict that there should be 2000 galaxies like Leo T in a typical 10 Mpc ^3 volume, with halo mass 5 10^8 < M_ 200 / M _ < 10^9 ; stellar mass 2 10^5 < M_*/ M _ < 6 10^5 ; and HI gas mass 3 10^5 < M_ HI / M _ < 3 10^6 . In practice, this will be an upper bound because many of these `Leo T'-like dIrrs will have been ram pressure stripped by a nearby host galaxy. Nonetheless, it is tantalising that Leo T lies right on the edge of the SDSS survey footprint 2009ApJ...696.2179K . Any closer to the Milky Way and Leo T would have been stripped of its gas, similarly to the recently discovered Eridanus II galaxy 2016ApJ...824L..14C . Any further away, and it would have been too faint to be seen.

sources.summary: The paper uses Leo T as a gas-rich, dark-matter-dominated dwarf whose ultra-low cooling rate constrains heating from dark matter decay or annihilation.

quotes.quote: In this section, we discuss properties of Leo T galaxy and the reason why it is an ideal astrophysical candidate to probe heating due to decaying and annihilating DM. Leo T is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy located outside the virial radius of the Milky Way (MW), nearly 420 kpc from the MW center. Leo T is both dark-matter dominated and gas-rich, it is well-studied observationally and has garnered modelling attention . Ref. analyzed high-resolution Giant Meterwave Radio Telescope (GMRT) and Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope (WSRT) observations to determine the column density and velocity profile (see also ).

For reference, Leo T has M_ HI =4.1 10^5 M_ and M_* 10^5 M_ . Gas and DM density profiles Ref. (FSM13 hereafter) modelled the DM and gas density profiles of Leo T. They assumed that in Leo T is an isothermal gas sphere, and its DM halo has a well-motivated flat-core (Burkert) profile. They fit their model to the column density and temperature measurements of Leo T by , and inferred the DM profile by assuming the gas to be in hydrostatic equilibrium.

sources.summary: The paper announces Leo T as a faint Local Group dwarf at about 420 kpc, with intermediate-age stars, recent star formation, and an associated HI cloud, resembling a low-luminosity transition galaxy.

quotes.quote: The purpose of this Letter is to announce the discovery of an additional dwarf galaxy in the Local Group. At a heliocentric distance of 420 kpc, this is at the very outskirts of the Milky Way's sphere of influence. The dwarf is faint with roughly circular isopleths from the majority intermediate-age stellar population. These properties are shared by other Local Group dwarf spheroidal (dSph) galaxies. However, also has a population of bright, blue stars, which is evidence for an epoch of recent star-formation.

Coincident with the position of the dwarf is an HI cloud of 2 10^5 . The dwarf has some of the characteristics of transition galaxies like Phoenix or Pisces, but, with an absolute magnitude of M_V 7.1 , it is much fainter. It is the least luminous galaxy found to date with recent star-formation.

sources.summary: The paper presents deep Leo T photometry, confirming young and old stellar populations, then uses CMD fitting to infer extended early star formation, a later burst, and nearly uniform metallicity.

quotes.quote: In the left panel of Fig. \ fig2 we present the CMD of Leo T. Shown are all stars within a 1.4' radius from the centre of this very distant object ( 420 kpc). The previously observed very young ( < 1 Gyr) and a much older ( > 5 Gyr) stellar populations are confirmed by our deeper photometry.

As already implied by the overlayed isochrones, an exact age is not found for the older stars, but rather continuous star formation starting in the oldest age bin and continuing until roughly 5 Gyr ago. The young stars seem to have formed in a burst starting slightly more than 1 Gyr and ending a few hundred Myr ago. Remarkably, we find a uniform metallicity of [ Fe/H ] 1.5 during the early star forming phase. For the young stars we get a similar metallicity.

sources.summary: The paper focuses on Leo T as an ultra-faint dwarf with variable stars, a new RR Lyrae distance, transition-type CMD morphology, complex star-formation history, and asymmetric stars and gas.

quotes.quote: We present results from the first combined study of variable stars and star formation history (SFH) of the Milky Way (MW) ``ultra-faint" dwarf (UFD) galaxy Leo T, based on F606W and F814W multi-epoch archive observations obtained with the Wide Field Planetary Camera 2 on board the Hubble Space Telescope. We have detected 14 variable stars in the galaxy. They include one fundamental-mode RR Lyrae star and 10 Anomalous Cepheids with periods shorter than 1 day, thus suggesting the occurrence of multiple star formation episodes in this UFD, of which one about 10 Gyr ago produced the RR Lyrae star.

A new estimate of the distance to Leo T of 409 ^ +29 _ 27 kpc (distance modulus of 23.06 0.15 mag) was derived from the galaxy's RR Lyrae star.
