sources.summary: System 10 is identified as a newly recognized multiply lensed background galaxy whose spectroscopic redshift agrees with the lens-model prediction and whose morphology is reproduced by the model.

quotes.quote: Among the new redshifts, the spectroscopic redshift of our new system 10 is of particular interest as this system has not hitherto been recognized as a multiply-lensed system (we identify the counterimage in this work). The spectroscopic redshift agrees remarkably well with the geometric redshift inferred (before we knew about the spectroscopic redshift) from our initial lens model based on all previously known systems, listed in Table tab1

In addition, our new system 10 (which is not recognised as a lensed system by any previous work) had a predicted z_ model =0.78 while in the brightest counterimage of this system has a spectroscopic redshift z_ spect =0.73 in good agreement with our prediction. System 10 is a good illustration of the power of the lens model to uncover new systems and to correctly predict their redshifts. Despite being clearly detected in previous images from the CLASH program, it was overlooked as a multiply lensed system by several authors, probably due to the fact that only a portion of a background galaxy is multiply lensed. A slightly different version of the driver model (similar to the driver model described in section Sect_Driver but excluding system 10 from the constraints) correctly predicts the redshift and the morphology of the lensed image as shown in Fig. fig_Syst10 . The sensitivity to the redshift is impressive and is comparable to the precision attained by photometric redshifts.

gmw_00001355.name: system 14
gmw_00001355.overview: System 14 is a gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z = 3.118 [arXiv:1512.07916]. It lies at the center of a 23-kiloparsec linear structure containing several nearby galaxies, which may be undergoing jet-induced star formation [arXiv:1512.07916].

sources.summary: System 14 is discussed as a z=3.118 lensed system in a 23 kpc linear structure, with nearby galaxies considered under a jet-induced star-formation scenario.

quotes.quote: Among the newly discovered multiple images, we find several distant galaxies at the same redshift of system 14 (z=3.118) that form a linear structure spanning 23 kpc with system 14 at its centre. We discuss the possibility that this system is an example of jet-induced star formation at redshift z 3 , an scenario that can be tested with radio obsrvations.

The possibility that the extra galaxies found around system 14 are seen due to jet-induced star formation, could be directly examined with deep radio observations of the radio emission associated with the alleged jet. Unfortunately, to the best of our knowledge, no deep radio data is available for this cluster. When comparing the predicted distribution of the galaxies around system 14 in the source plane we find that around 14.3, one of the galaxies is significantly brighter than the other two (marked with a yellow circle in Fig. fig_Syst14_source ). To explore the jet-induced star formation scenario, we compute the time delays between the different counterimages of system 14. The lens model predicts that the light from 14.3 arrives approximately 90 years before the light in 14.2 and approximately 96 years before the light observed in 14.1 (the uncertainty in the time delays is about 2 years based on the dispersion of the six lens models). Consequently, if the galaxy marked with a yellow circle in Fig. fig_Syst14_source is indeed at the same redshift as system 14, the jet-induced star formation in the galaxy in 14.3 is unlikely to be responsible for its relative brightness. We can not, however, rule out sort-lived events, like supernova for which the formation of their progenitor stars may have been triggered by the jet. As in the case of the recent supernova Refsdal Diego2016,Kelly2016 , this scenario can be tested in the future although we will have to wait for 90 years. Other less exotic explanations, like microlensing, could be worth exploring.

gmw_00001356.name: DDO170
gmw_00001356.overview: DDO170 is a dwarf irregular disc galaxy [arXiv:2102.09078, arXiv:1301.3517] characterized by low surface brightness [arXiv:1301.3517]. Modeling of its dark matter halo shows a temperature profile that increases with distance from the center before decreasing [arXiv:2102.09078].

sources.summary: DDO170 is used as a worked example for dark-matter halo modeling, showing mass and temperature profiles whose derived temperature changes with radius and can add inner attraction.

quotes.quote: For the sake of ease in presentation, we only show the results for galaxy DDO170. To show that the dispersion velocity of DM at the center of halos is independent of the edge, we invoke equation Eq:CentralDispersion and plot _0 in terms of R . The result can be seen in figure Fig:Sig0IndependenceOfR and indicates that after a small distance from the center, the right hand side of equation Eq:CentralDispersion reaches a constant value.

Figure Fig:iGal_8_profiles shows the mass and temperature profiles of the halo of DDO170. It should be noted that the former is the observed mass model reported in . The latter is the combination of the observations and the theoretical framework presented in this paper. As can be seen from the figure, the temperature increases with the distance from the center up to a point before it starts to decrease. This temperature profile can lead to an additional attractive force in the inner region of the halo.

sources.summary: The paper treats DDO 170 as a dwarf disc galaxy whose modeled rotation curve and stellar escape speeds help compare mirror and cold dark matter predictions.

quotes.quote: We then construct in Section 3 CDM models for the galaxies NGC 2403, DDO 170 and DDO 154 (whose rotational curves have previously been modelled using MDM), and calculate and compare escape speeds from the stellar disc as a function of galactic radius for both types of model. We show that the MDM models predict specific values for the escape speeds that are generally different from those in the CDM models.

As in the BPR model, we use data from , , and for the intermediate spiral galaxy NGC 2403 and the dwarf galaxies DDO 170 and DDO 154. Again assuming a constant mass-to-light ratio for each galaxy and taking M_ L and r_ 1,d as fixed parameters, we fit the rotational curves of these three disc galaxies by using , v_ max and r_ s as fit parameters.

We have taken the galaxies NGC 2403, DDO 170 and DDO 154 (whose rotational curves have been modelled by BPR using MDM) along with a couple of synthetic galaxies as representative of spiral galaxies with a range of sizes, and used them to calculate and compare stellar escape speeds between the MDM and CDM(IT) models.

gmw_00001357.name: host galaxy of GRB 100621A
gmw_00001357.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 100621A is a bright, compact, and very blue galaxy [arXiv:1304.5852]. It hosts a young stellar population with an age of only 0.05 Gyr and exhibits active star formation [arXiv:1304.5852].

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 100621A host as a bright, compact, very blue galaxy with young stellar population, moderate host SED extinction, active star formation, and sub-mm/radio limits.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of GRB 100621A was extensively covered in , including in addition to the GROND and Swift /UVOT data. In short, the ' 21.5 mag galaxy is well detected from the UV (all Swift /UVOT filters) up to the K_ s band showing a very blue spectral energy distribution with ( R-K ) _ AB 0.3 mag. The stellar population synthesis fitting of the host SED returns an age of the dominating stellar population of only 0.05 Gyr, and an intrinsic extinction of A _ V ^ host = 0.6 ^ +0.1 _ 0.2 mag, in stark contrast to the large afterglow (AG) extinction of A _ V ^ AG = 3.61 0.06 mag.

The absolute magnitude of the host is M _ B = -20.68 0.08 mag, and the star formation rate was determined as 13 ^ +6 _ 5 /yr. The APEX and ATCA non-detections of any flux at the position of GRB 100621A at > 5 days after the GRB also provide first crude limits on the sub-mm and radio emission of the host galaxy, of < 6.8 mJy at 345 GHz, < 170 Jy at 5.5 GHz, and < 200 Jy at 9 GHz (all 2 confidence). Assuming that the dominant fraction of the radio emission would be of non-thermal origin, and using the formalism of , this implies an upper limit on the star formation rate of 100 /yr.

gmw_00001358.name: PKS 0637-752
gmw_00001358.overview: PKS 0637-752 is a radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.651 [arXiv:1702.00015, arXiv:1511.00258]. It features a well-collimated, 100-kiloparsec one-sided jet that produces anomalous, hard-spectrum X-ray emission from several distinct knots [arXiv:1702.00015, arXiv:1511.00258].

sources.summary: The paper uses Fermi data to rule out IC/CMB gamma rays for PKS 0637-752, arguing its anomalous kpc-scale X-ray jet requires another emission origin.

quotes.quote: Using over six years of Fermi monitoring data, we show that the expected hard, steady gamma-ray emission implied by the IC/CMB model is not seen in PKS 0637-752, the prototype jet for which this model was first proposed. IC/CMB emission is thus ruled out as the source of the X-rays, joining recent results for the jets in 3C 273 using the same method; mey14 and PKS 1136-135 using UV polarization; cara2013 . We further show that the Fermi observations give an upper limit of < 6.5 for the four brightest X-ray knots of PKS 0637-752, and derive an updated limit of < 7.8 for knots A and B1 of 3C 273 (assuming equipartition).

In August 1999, the Chandra X-ray Observatory observed its first celestial target, quasar PKS 0637-752, during the initial focusing of the telescope schwartz2000,chartas2000 . Along with the bright quasar core, Chandra unexpectedly detected X-rays from the kiloparsec (kpc) scale relativistic jet (previously known from radio imaging, Figure 1). Unlike the synchrotron spectrum of lower-power FR I jets like M87 which easily extend up to X-ray energies e.g. wilson2002 , the synchrotron spectrum of powerful kpc-scale jets (including PKS 0637-752) generally peak at or below the IR/Optical band. The X-rays detected in the kpc-scale jet of PKS 0637-752 were much brighter than expected from the radio-optical synchrotron spectrum, or indeed from either synchrotron self-Compton (SSC) or inverse Compton upscattering of ambient CMB photons (IC/CMB) assuming mildly relativistic kpc-scale flows under equipartition conditions chartas2000 . Further, the X-ray spectrum of the jet was remarkably hard, with a photon index of 1.76 0.1 (Figure fig:schematic ).

sources.summary: The paper discusses prior Bethe-Heitler modeling of PKS 0637--752, where X-rays can be reproduced with kpc-scale, 0.1 mG knots but require super-Eddington proton power and limited core-photon influence.

quotes.quote: Recently, to account for the second population of electrons, we proposed an alternative model in which high energy electrons/positrons produced by Bethe-Heitler process and applied this model to PKS 0637--752 kt2017 . Protons obeying a power-law spectrum produce electrons/positrons (hereafter we call shortly electrons) in collisions with radio-IR photons emitted by synchrotron radiation in a knot. Then produced electrons emit X-rays and rays by synchrotron radiation. We showed that X-ray emission is explained by this model with a size a few kpc and a magnetic field 0.1 mG. However, the proton power an order of magnitude larger than the Eddington limit is required because of the low efficiency of electron production in the radiation field given by the radio-IR spectrum by primary electrons.

As for PKS 0637--752, ed2006 reported superluminal motion of the pc-scale jet. Core optical luminosity is about three orders of magnitude higher than that of the knot. The projected distance to the knot from the core is about 70 kpc so that the radiation from the core can be ignored if the beaming effect is neglected.

sources.summary: The paper reports ALMA and Fermi observations of PKS 0637 752, whose anomalous kpc-scale X-ray jet and gamma-ray limits rule out the IC/CMB model.

quotes.quote: GeV-band upper limits from Fermi /LAT for the well-known anomalous X-ray jet in PKS 0637 752 were previously shown in to violate the predictions of the IC/CMB model.

galaxies: active --- galaxies: jets --- quasars: individual (PKS 0637 752) The radio-loud quasar PKS 0637 752 ( z =0.651) was the first astrophysical source observed by the Chandra X-ray observatory, shortly after its launch in 1999. This source also gave us Chandra's first scientific discovery, as it unexpectedly detected a kpc-scale X-ray jet associated with PKS 0637 752, with a very high X-ray flux level and hard ( _x=0.85 0.1 ) spectrum which precluded it being the tail of the radio-optical synchrotron spectrum chartas2000,schwartz2000 . Nearly twenty years later, Chandra has now discovered several dozen more `anomalous' X-ray jets, typically in powerful quasar sources, where the hard-spectrum X-ray flux is clearly from a second component, distinct from the radio-optical synchrotron spectrum which turns over before the infrared see, e.g., for reviews harris2006,hardcastle2006 . In the initial discovery papers on PKS 0637 752, and ruled out thermal brehmstrahlung, synchrotron self-Compton, and inverse Compton off the Cosmic Microwave Background (IC/CMB) from a mildly relativistic flow as possible explanations for the anomalous X-ray flux in the jet, leaving open the possibility of a second synchrotron spectrum from an apparently co-spatial second electron energy distribution.

In this letter, we present new ALMA observations and updated limits from Fermi /LAT which show that our original claims can be strengthened, and that IC/CMB is now ruled out in PKS 0637 752 at the 8.7 level. We describe here new observations obtained from ALMA and the Fermi /LAT for PKS 0637 752.

sources.summary: The review describes PKS 0637-0752 as the first Chandra-discovered X-ray jet case, requiring two emission components and favoring IC/CMB emission from a relativistic large-scale jet.

quotes.quote: Even in the source selected for the first light, the distant ( z=0.6 ) quasar PKS 0637-752, a prominent jet has been discovered (Chartas et al. 2000; Schwartz et al. 2000). Soon after the discovery of the X-ray jet in PKS 0637-0752 other jets have been detected both in radio galaxies (both of the FRI and FRII type) and in quasars.

This is the case of the first jet discovered in PKS 0637-0752, for which two separated emission components are clearly required by the shape of the convex radio-optical-X-ray spectrum. The SSC model cannot explain the level of the X-ray emission, since it would require very extreme conditions. Tavecchio et al. (2000b) and Celotti, Ghisellini Chiaberge (2000) proposed that the mechanism responsible for the observed X-ray emission is the IC scattering of the CMB radiation by relativistic electrons in the jet. The key point in this model is the assumption that the jet in these (FRII) sources is still highly relativistic (with bulk Lorentz factors =5-10 ) at these scales.

sources.summary: The review presents PKS 0637-752 as the quasar jet that sparked debate over beamed iC-CMB X-rays, requiring fast bulk motion over hundreds of kiloparsecs.

quotes.quote: It was thus fortuitous that a radio-loud quasar was the chosen target for in-flight focus calibration, since this led to the detection of resolved jet emission from the z = 0.651 quasar PKS 0637-752 and see Fig. fig:pks0637 schwartz-pks0637, chartas-pks0637 . Several possible origins for PKS 0637-752's jet X-rays were considered. The level of optical emission was too low to explain the X-rays as the synchrotron radiation from a single population of electrons, and SSC was disfavoured as it would require strong dominance of the energy in relativistic electrons over that in magnetic field, giving a total energy in particles and field that is 1000 times that given by minimum energy schwartz-pks0637 . A more promising explanation allowed the jet to be at minimum energy but required it to have fast bulk motion (a Lorentz factor of 20 at 5^ to the line of sight), in which case it would see boosted CMB in its rest frame and emit beamed X-rays in the observer's frame tav-pks0637, cel-pks0637 . Although such a speed and angle are consistent with VLBI measurements on pc scales lovell-pks0637 , the fast speed must persist up to hundreds of kpc from the core (after projection is taken into account) for the X-rays to be produced by this mechanism, which I will call ``beamed iC-CMB''.

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 0637--752 as a benchmark FSRQ jet, arguing updated EC/CMB cooling fits its multiwavelength knots without violating Fermi limits.

quotes.quote: The benchmark jet of PKS 0637--752 is one of the two for which the -ray flux predicted by EC/CMB was reported to be higher than that measured by Fermi /LAT (Meyer et al. 2015). It is also the jet with the highest quality data available, as it has been observed in the radio, X--ray, mid--IR and optical bands. The result of fitting all the jet knots with the updated EC/CMB model, with the parameters listed in Table 1, is shown in Fig. 0637 . Such parameters correspond to equipartition between the magnetic field and relativistic electrons. All knots are reproduced satisfactorily, but only the brightest (knot wk8.9) and the integrated flux of the jet are shown in figure for clarity.

sources.summary: The paper models the large-scale jet of PKS 0637-752, arguing Fermi limits rule out IC/CMB expectations and Bethe-Heitler secondary leptons can reproduce the X-rays only with super-Eddington proton power.

quotes.quote: Models based on inverse Compton scattering off CMB photons by relativistically beamed jets have recently been ruled out, since Fermi LAT observations for 3C 273 and PKS 0637-752 give the upper limit far below the model prediction. Synchrotron emission from a separate electron population with multi-hundred TeV energies remains a possibility although its origin is not well known. We examine a photo-hadronic origin of such high energy electrons/positrons, assuming that protons are accelerated up to 10^ 19 eV and produce electrons/positrons through Bethe-Heitler process and photo-pion production. These secondary electrons/positrons are injected at sufficiently high energies and produce X-rays and rays by synchrotron radiation without conflicting with the Fermi LAT upper limits. We find that the resultant spectrum well reproduces the X-ray observations from PKS 0637-752, if the proton power is as large as at least 10^ 49 erg s ^ 1 , which is highly super-Eddington.

sources.summary: The paper applies a proton synchrotron model to PKS 0637-752, estimating jet powers for its X-ray knots that are comparable to the quasar's Eddington luminosity.

quotes.quote: Assuming a simple power law proton spectrum of spectral index 2 in the energy range of 10^ 15 - 10^ 18 eV we have calculated the photon fluxes for the extended jets of 3C 273, PKS 0637-752 and B3 0727+409 to explain the X-ray data with the proton synchrotron model. The emission region is assumed to be of radius 1 kpc and its Doppler factor is fixed at 1 for simplicity.

The jet power required to explain the observed X--ray data is 1.28 10^ 48 ergs/sec (corresponding to 4.25 mG) for 3C 273, 3 10^ 48 ergs/sec for knot wk8.9 (corresponding to 6.5 mG) 5.75 10^ 48 ergs/sec for the brightest knot (corresponding to 9 mG) of PKS 0637-752. The Eddington's luminosity of 3C 273 and PKS 0637-752 could be as high as 10^ 48 erg/sec ( , ). Thus the jet powers required to explain the X-ray data from the extended jets of 3C 273 (for magnetic field 4.25 mG) and PKS 0637-752 (for magnetic fields of 6.5 mG 9 mG) are comparable to their Eddington's luminosities. Hence it is possible that proton synchrotron emission could be the underlying mechanism of X-ray emission from their extended jets.

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 0637-752 as a luminous quasar X-ray jet whose emission motivated iC/CMB modeling but faces Fermi gamma-ray upper-limit evidence for distinct electron populations.

quotes.quote: The X-ray emission of the luminous jet in PKS 0637-752 could not be explained by reasonable models of synchrotron, inverse Compton, or thermal mechanisms ( ). and provided the insight of invoking relativistic bulk motion of the jet with Lorentz factor , and using the result from that this increased the energy density of the cosmic microwave background in the rest frame of the jet by the factor ^2 . Subsequently the iC/CMB model has been widely used to interpret the X-ray emission from the jets of powerful quasars ( ).

However, powerful evidence against the iC/CMB mechanism in the case of PKS 0637-752 was presented by , and , while presented similar evidence for 3 jets for which the iC/CMB model had not been indicated. They showed Fermi upper limits to GeV ray emission was below the level expected if the electron spectrum producing the GHz radio emission also produced the jet emission observed in the ALMA and IR/optical regions.

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 0637-752 jet knots with shear acceleration, using knot wk8.9 multiwavelength data and requiring magnetic fields and weak beaming compatible with jet power limits.

quotes.quote: In this section we apply the model described above to the multifrequency emission from knots of jets associated to the benchmark sources PKS 0637-752 (the first detected by Chandra ) and PKS 1136-135 (the target of several deep multifrequency observations). PKS 0637-752 : we use the multifrequency data of the well-defined knot wk8.9 reported by Mehta et al. (2009) and Meyer et al. (2017), see Fig. fig:0637 . This dataset is particularly reach, including, besides data at radio and X-ray frequencies, a good coverage of the low-energy emission component in the mm (ALMA), IR ( Spitzer ) and optical ( HST ) bands.

For PKS 0637-752, to keep the power at acceptable values we assume that the emission is weakly beamed, quite compatible with properties of the jet. In both cases the inferred power is of the order of 10^ 47 erg s ^ 1 , on the high-power tail of the powerful quasars population (Ghisellini et al. 2014).

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 0637 -- 752 as a resolved, well-collimated quasar-jet test case for bulk-Comptonized CMB emission, emphasizing that Spitzer can resolve the bridge emission.

quotes.quote: We apply our method to PKS 0637 -- 752, a superluminal quasar with an one -- sided Chandra - detected large scale jet. What makes this source particularly suited for such a procedure is the absence of significant non-thermal jet emission in the bridge , the region between the core and the first bright knot, guaranteeing that most of the electrons in the bridge are cold, and leaving the BC scattered CMB radiation as the only significant source of photons in this region. At =3.6-8.0 m , the most likely band for the BC scattered emission to appear, the angular resolution of Spitzer ( 1''-3'' ) is considerably smaller than the bridge of PKS 0637 -- 752 ( 8'' ), making it possible to both measure and resolve this emission.

The jet of PKS 0637--752, which is resolved and is found to be well collimated, propagates through a well understood photon field: the CMB. The source exhibits radio, optical, and X-ray emission from the quasar core and then from well separated knots along the jet at angular distances 8'' .

sources.summary: The paper models PKS 0637-752 as a quasar with a 100 kpc one-sided jet whose hard X-ray/gamma-ray knot emission can be explained by proton synchrotron radiation.

quotes.quote: Here we propose the proton synchrotron origin of the X ray -gamma ray flux from the knots of PKS 0637-752 with a reasonable budget in luminosity, by considering synchrotron radiation from an accelerated proton population.

The first discovery of high-energy X-ray emission from the kpc-scale relativistic jet of quasar PKS 0637-752 by the Chandra Observatory (Chartas et al. (2000)) has led to many similar significant discoveries later (Harris Krawczynski (2006)). PKS 0637-752, located at redshift z = 0.651 (Savage,Browne and Bolton (1976)) was the first X-ray target of the Chandra Observatory (Weisskopf et al. (2000); Schwartz et al. (2000); Chartas et al. (2000)) which accidentally discovered a 100 kpc one-sided jet from the source coincident with the radio jet reported by Tingay et al. (1998). The strong X-ray flux observed from this jet is difficult to explain via standard mechanisms such as Synchrotron Self Compton (SSC) or thermal Bremsstrahlung (Schwartz et al. 2000).

sources.summary: The paper includes PKS 0637 752 to test central-source inverse Compton emission, noting its prototype role, rejected beamed IC/CMB model, and inferred kpc-jet geometry.

quotes.quote: In one of the first works, it was shown for the PKS 0637 752 quasar jet that the most probable mechanism of its high-frequency radiation is inverse Compton scattering (IC) of self synchrotron radiation Schwartz2000 . However, from a comparison of the radio and X-ray fluxes, it followed that the condition of the energy equipartition between the magnetic field and the particles is not fulfilled: a lager energy is contained in the emitting particles. Tav00,Cel01 suggested that the X-ray radiation of the PKS 0637 752 jet is formed due to inverse Compton scattering of cosmic microwave background (IC/CMB).

Despite the fact that the X-ray intensity of the knots of the quasar PKS 0637 752 kpc-scale jet does not exhibit a distinct decrease with the distance from the core, we included this object in the sample: it is interesting as a prototype for which the beamed IC/CMB scenario was first suggested and then rejected.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0637 752 as the prototype core-dominated quasar with an X-ray jet, whose beamed CMB/IC modelling implied little bulk deceleration from parsec to 100-kpc scales.

quotes.quote: But the most widely studied class of objects to exhibit X-ray jets, following the discovery of the prototype, PKS 0637 752 (Schwartz \ 2000), are core-dominated quasars (CDQ), and these often have broad-band spectra in which the optical data points preclude a one-zone synchrotron model.

A key result of these early analyses of PKS 0637 752 was that the angle to the line of sight, and the bulk Lorentz factor , were consistent with constraints on the pc-scale speed from superluminal motion observations. Thus the model implied little or no bulk deceleration between the parsec and 100-kpc scales in these jets, a point emphasised by Tavecchio \ (2004).

At the same time, depending on the low-energy electron energy spectrum assumed (and it is important to bear in mind that these calculations require an enormous extrapolation from the observable electron energies: e.g. Harris 2004), there may be a lower limit on _ from the requirement that the CMB/IC prediction should not exceed the observed optical flux density of the jet, and there is a definite limit on the number of cold ( 1 ) electrons from bulk Comptonization of the CMB (Georganopoulos \ 2005) which in the case of PKS 0637 752 may provide a stringent constraint (Uchiyama \ 2005).

sources.summary: The paper selects PKS0637-752 as a possible polarization calibrator, noting its 97.5 GHz flux, stable ATCA polarization angle, and ALMA-frequency Faraday rotation.

quotes.quote: The first object we selected is PKS0637-752 (also known as AT20GJ063546-751616), already suggested by Massardi2013 as a potential leakage calibrator, being at 1 Jy and 1.6 polarized at 97.5 GHz. Our ATCA observations show that the polarization angle is quite constant across the 5.5-38 GHz frequency range and stable within 8^ at 38 GHz between 2014 September and 2016 July (see Fig. fig:Spettri1 ). However, this object displays Faraday rotation in different frequency regimes (see Table tab:ALMARMdetlist ): at ALMA frequencies, between 90 and 105 GHz the polarization angle absolute variation is 14^ .

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 0637 752 as an exception to the thermal-IR result, models its steep high-frequency spectrum without a cutoff, and reports differing mm power-law slopes from two epochs.

quotes.quote: The result of this method suggests that for all the selected objects, with the exception of PKS 0637 752, the IR emission is thermal in origin.
In the case of PKS 0637 752 (Low) (see section unc_rad ) the cut off was not included in the model ( e ) since the high frequency part of the spectrum is very steep.

For PKS 0637 75 the flattest mm power law, chosen among several simultaneous observations ( ), was measured on February 15th, 1990 ( (3.0-1.3 mm) = 0.77), and the steepest one was measured on April 4th, 1991 ( (3.0-1.3 mm) = 1.47).

sources.summary: PKS0637 is analyzed as an IC/CMB jet case where knot distance and beaming assumptions imply a low maximum surviving electron energy.

quotes.quote: We take 3 values of the bulk Lorentz factor for the jet: =1 (no beaming, just for reference), =3.16 (a typical value for synchrotron models), and =10 (the classic solution for the PKS0637 IC/CMB model).
In the case of PKS0637, stronger limits could be found for the end of the radio jet, but we use the distance of the strong radio/X-ray knots 8 '' from the quasar.
With =10 and =5^ , we find a max( ) value of 1700.

gmw_00001359.name: PKS 1136-135
gmw_00001359.overview: PKS 1136-135 is a radio-loud, steep-spectrum quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0606659, arXiv:1805.10764] that is also classified as a blazar [arXiv:1906.11339, arXiv:2308.10071]. The galaxy hosts a kiloparsec-scale jet with X-ray emitting knots [arXiv:1805.10764, arXiv:1906.11339] and exhibits a short optical tidal tail that indicates past interaction or merger activity [arXiv:1008.2683, arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: The paper applies the same shear-acceleration framework to PKS 1136-135 knot A, finding q=5/3 viable, q=1 too slow, and acceptable jet power constraints.

quotes.quote: PKS 1136-135 : this source has been the target of several dedicated observations in different bands. In particular, we consider the brightest feature, called knot A. Deep Chandra and HST observations (Sambruna et al. 2004, 2006) and IR data from Spitzer (Uchiyama et al. 2006, 2007) allows us to have a good description of its multifrequency emission (Fig. fig:1136a ). The knot radius derived by Sambruna et al. (2020) is r_j=2 10^ 21 cm.

Also in this case we considered the two cases q=1 and q=5/3 . The situation is very similar to the previous case, since for q=1 the acceleration is too slow to counterbalance cooling (for simplicity we do not report the plots with the timescales, very similar to those discussed before). Only the case q=5/3 offers a viable solution. The jet power poses a less tight constraint than for PKS 0637-052.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1136--135 as a powerful radio-loud quasar with a kpc-scale X-ray jet, diffuse thermal emission near its core, and a curved X-ray continuum.

quotes.quote: Here we tackle this issue using new, deep \ and \ observations of the cores of three powerful radio loud quasars: 1136--135, 1150+497 ( ), and 0723+679 ( ), in the redshift range z =0.3--0.8. These sources are known from our previous \ snapshot survey to have kpc-scale X-ray jets. In 1136--135 and 1150+497, evidence is found for the presence of diffuse thermal X-ray emission around the cores, on scales of 40--50 kpc and with luminosity L _ 0.3-2 keV 10^ 43 , suggesting thermal emission from the host galaxy or a galaxy group. The X-ray continua of the cores in the three sources are described by an upward-curved (concave) broken power law, with photon indices _ soft 1.8-2.1 and _ hard 1.7 below and above 2 keV, respectively. There is evidence for an unresolved \ line with EW 70 eV in the three quasars.

Follow-up \ observations were carried out on April 16, 2003 for 1136--135 and on July 18, 2003 for 1150+497, with total exposures 81 ks and 70 ks, respectively. Screening was performed using standard criteria (S06). After screening, the effective exposure times are 70.2 ks for 1136--135 and 61.7 ks for 1150+497. The net count rates of the cores in the energy range 0.3--10 keV from an extraction circle with radius 1.5 \ are 0.27 0.02 /s for 1136--135 and 0.55 0.03 /s for 1150+497.

sources.summary: The paper rules out IC/CMB emission for PKS 1136-135 using gamma-ray upper limits, multiwavelength jet SEDs, UV upturns, and prior high polarization measurements.

quotes.quote: PKS 1136-135 PKS 1136-135 is a powerful FR II source previously observed by with Chandra and HST , where they modeled the X-ray emission with the IC/CMB model. Our multi-wavelength images are shown in Figure fig:1136 images , where we see a straight and knotty radio jet, with knots brighter towards the hot spot in the radio. Knots B, C, D, and E are not separately resolved by ALMA so we do not include ALMA flux densities for the jet SED. We show in Figure fig:jet_SEDs the SED of knots , A, and B, where our gamma-ray upper limits are well below the extrapolated IC/CMB emission in all three cases.

In knots and A there is an upturn in the SED at 815 nm which lies above a single power-law fit for the synchrotron spectrum. Fitting these UV-upturns with an IC/CMB model is problematic since this would require very high levels of X-ray emission not observed by Chandra . Therefore, these UV-upturns imply a second higher energy electron distribution also responsible for the X-ray emission, similar to that seen in 3C 273.

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 1136--135 as a z=0.556 steep-spectrum quasar with X-ray knots 30-60 kpc from the core, optical polarization, and evidence disfavoring IC/CMB emission.

quotes.quote: Another kpc-scale jet is found in PKS 1136--135, a steep-spectrum quasar located at z=0.556 . The X-ray emitting knots of PKS 1136--135 are at projected distances of 30 - 60 kpc from the core cara2013 . sam2006 and tav2006 supported the IC/CMB model for the X-ray emission from the knots. On the other hand, uchi2007 extensively studied the emission mechanisms in this source and interpreted the X-ray emission as synchrotron radiation from a second nonthermal electron population. Later cara2013 found that several kpc-scale knots of PKS 1136--135 are highly polarized at optical.
Combining this high polarization and the spectral shape, they concluded that IC/CMB models are not favored to explain the X-ray emission.

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 1136-135 as an extended-jet blazar with optical excess, polarized rising UV emission, and a synchrotron-dominated UV/X-ray component.

quotes.quote: New insight from the SPITZER near-Infrared imaging of the kiloparsec-scale jets of the blazars 3C 273 and PKS 1136-135 Optical flux densities of the knots in the kiloparsec-scale jets of these blazars are found to be distinctly in excess over the extrapolation from the SPITZER near-infrared data points but connecting smoothly to the X-ray data points, thus revealing a clear spectral upturn in the optical/UV region (Fig. 2). This leads to the surmise that the optical emission from these extended blazar jets is made of two components: one connecting to the radio and a dominant one which connects to the X-ray emission (Uchiyama et al. \ 2006; 2007).

Using the Hubble Space Telescope, Cara et al. \ (2013) have measured > 30 polarization for the rising UV component of the extended jet of PKS 1136-135 (which is clearly linked to the high energy hump of its SED). This strongly suggests that the synchrotron mechanism is the dominant contributor to the jet's emission even in the UV/X-ray regime.

sources.summary: For 1136-135, the model matches the two brightest AGN jet knots; Knot A has X-ray flux below the radio/optical extrapolation, consistent with synchrotron emission and an X-ray-regime break.

quotes.quote: This criterion was satisfied by the two brightest knots of the AGN: 1136-135 , 1150+497 , 1354+195 , 3C 273 and 3C371 . In this work, the knot closer to the nucleus is referred to as Knot A and the further one as Knot B.

The knots of 3C371 and Knot A of 1136-135 are unique in this sample since their the X-ray flux lies below the extrapolation of the radio/optical spectra to X-ray wavelengths. This allows for the interpretation that the X-ray flux is due to synchrotron emission samb02,pesce,sahay . For Knot A of 1136-135 and Knot B of 3C371, this implies that the spectral break for the synchrotron emission occurs at the X-ray regime (Figure 1) which in turn indicates that these sources are relatively younger ones.

sources.summary: Deep Chandra, HST, and radio observations show 1136--135 has an FRII quasar jet whose X-ray emission fades while radio emission brightens, consistent with systematic relativistic-flow deceleration.

quotes.quote: We present deep (70--80 ks) \ and multicolor \ ACS images of two jets hosted by the powerful quasars 1136--135 and 1150+497, together with new radio observations. The sources have an FRII morphology and were selected from our previous X-ray and optical jet survey for detailed follow up aimed at obtaining better constraints on the jet multiwavelength morphology and X-ray and optical spectra of individual knots, and to test emission models deriving physical parameters more accurately.

In 1136--135 an interesting behavior is observed, whereby, downstream of the most prominent knot at 6.5 \ from the core, the X-ray emission fades while the radio emission brightens. The X-ray spectrum also varies, with the X-ray photon index flattening from _x 2 in the inner part to _x 1.7 to the end of the jet. We intepret the jet behavior in 1136--135 in a scenario where the relativistic flow suffers systematic deceleration along the jet, and briefly discuss the major consequences of this scenario.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1136-13 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: Our GMOS-S image of this QSO/FRII at redshift z=0.55 (Figure pks1136_online ) reveals the optical counterpart of a radio jet extending up to 60 kpc in the NW from the radio galaxy Uchiyama07 . We also detect a shorter tidal tail pointing to the West of the quasar, for which we measure a surface brightness of _V =22.6 mag arcsec ^ 2 . It is also possible to unveil another tail SE of the nucleus. The lack of either optical long-slit spectra or infrared data suitable for assessing the degree of emission line contamination of the detected features prevents us from confirming whether the extended structures are dominated by continuum radiation.

sources.summary: G4Jy 933 is described as a QSO with a long optical and X-ray jet, a shorter optical tidal tail, and a blazar classification in Roma-BZCAT.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 933: is listed in the sample of Parkes half-Jansky flat-spectrum radio sources drinkwater97 , being a QSO with a radio jet extending up to 60 kpc in the north-west direction detected in the optical band uchiyama07 as well as in the X-rays sambruna02 , and with a z =0.55646 tadhunter93,drinkwater97,jones09 . A shorter tidal tail pointing to the west of the QSO is also detected in the optical band ramos11 . G4Jy 933 is also classified as blazar of uncertain type in the Roma-BZCAT a.k.a. BZU J1139-1350 massaro15b .

gmw_00001360.name: SMM J16359+6612
gmw_00001360.overview: SMM J16359+6612 is a submillimeter galaxy at a redshift of approximately 2.5 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501095, arXiv:astro-ph/0309184] that is triply imaged and highly magnified by the foreground cluster Abell 2218 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501095, arXiv:1207.0492]. The galaxy exhibits a double-peaked CO profile [arXiv:astro-ph/0508037, arXiv:2207.06967] and hosts an intrinsic star formation rate of about 500 solar masses per year [arXiv:astro-ph/0501095].

sources.summary: The paper detects lensed radio emission from the triply imaged z=2.516 submillimetre galaxy, deriving a steep spectrum, intrinsic microJy fluxes, and a star formation rate near 500 solar masses per year.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of discrete, lensed radio emission from the multiply imaged, z=2.516 submillimetre selected galaxy, SMM J16359+6612. All three images are detected in deep WSRT 1.4 GHz and VLA 8.2 GHz observations, and the radio positions are coincident with previous sub-mm SCUBA observations of this system.

SMMJ16359+6612 is a sub-millimetre galaxy (SMG) located at z=2.516 that is triply imaged and highly magnified by the core of a massive foreground cluster, Abell 2218 (Kneib et al. 2004a, Knudsen 2004). The three lensed images of the background source are highly magnified (by factors of 22, 14 9) and the maximum image separation is 41 arcseconds. All three images are detected by the Submillimeter Common-User Bolometric Array (SCUBA) at 850 450 microns with the brightest image having a measured 850 micron flux density of 17 mJy (Kneib et al. 2004a).

Using the relation between Star formation rate (SFR) and radio luminosity (Condon 1992), we derive an intrinsic star formation rate (SFR) for SMM J16359+6612 of 500 M _  yr ^ 1 . This is in good agreement with the value obtained by Kneib et al.

sources.summary: The paper treats SMM J16359+6612 as a triply imaged lensed SMG, deriving its infrared luminosity, double-peaked CO profiles, and subthermal excitation.

quotes.quote: Here, we follow this familiar pattern. We exploit the recent upgrade to the National Radio Astronomy Observatory's (NRAO's 1 ) Karl G. \ Jansky Very Large Array (VLA), which includes the provision of Ka-band receivers (26.5--40 GHz), to study the redshifted \ \ emission from three lensed SMGs: (i) SMM J14009+0252 lying at z=2.9344 ivison00, weiss09 , lensed by Abell 1835 and one of the brightest SMGs detected in the SCUBA Cluster Lens Survey SCLS; smail02 (ii) SMM J16359+6612, a bright SMG a z=2.5168 kneib04, kneib05 and lensed into three images by the Abell 2218 cluster, and (iii) SMM J02399--0136 at z=2.808 ivison98 , the first SCUBA galaxy and the brightest of the SCLS galaxies, lensed by Abell 370.

For SMM J16359 we use published flux densities for the three images at 24 and 70 m from kneib04 . The three images are resolved by \ up to 350 m but become blended at longer wavelengths. Where the three images are blended, we divide up the observed flux in proportion to the known magnification factors. We derive an estimate of L_ IR for each of the three images of this source and combine our three estimates to derive the magnification-weighted mean L_ IR = (6.4 1.0) 10^ 11 L _ .
For SMM J16359, the magnification-weighted mean T_ b ratio, r_ (3-2)/(1-0) = 0.72 0.12 , is entirely consistent with that of the SMG population harris10, ivison11 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies SMM J16359+6612 as a multiply imaged very red submillimetre galaxy, giving its lensing magnification, corrected 850 micron flux, estimated redshift, and Arp 220-like SED.

quotes.quote: Three submm sources were identified with three images of the same very red galaxy, SMM J16359+6612, detected both in optical and near-IR. The submm source is detected both at 850 m and 450 m with the same colour for all three images. The magnification factor in total for all three images is 40.

The lensing corrected flux density is f_ 850 = 0.9 mJy . Based on detailed lensing models the galaxy has an estimated redshift of 2.5. Its spectral energy distribution is similar to that of Arp220 and HR10, though about a factor 5 fainter.

sources.summary: The paper reports multiple CO detections in the lensed high-redshift submillimeter galaxy, finding double-peaked profiles, low gas excitation, and molecular gas likely in an overlap region of merging galaxies.

quotes.quote: Henkel 4 IRAM, Avenida Divina Pastora 7, 18012 Granada, Spain IRAM, 300 rue de la Piscine, 38406 St-Martin-d'H eres, France MPIA, K onigstuhl 17, 69117 Heidelberg, Germany MPIfR, Auf dem H ugel 69, 53121 Bonn, Germany Using the IRAM 30 m telescope, we report the detection of the CO(3--2), CO(4--3), CO(5--4) and CO(6--5) lines in the gravitational lensed submm galaxy SMM J16359+6612 at z=2.5 . The CO lines have a double peak profile in all transitions. From a Gaussian decomposition of the spectra we show that the CO line ratios, and therefore the underlying physical conditions of the gas, are similar for the blue and the redshifted component.

The CO line Spectral Energy Distribution (SED; i.e. flux density vs. rotational quantum number) turns over already at the CO 5--4 transition which shows that the molecular gas is less excited than in nearby starburst galaxies and high--z QSOs. This difference mainly arises from a lower average \ density, which indicates that the gas is less centrally concentrated than in nuclear starburst regions in local galaxies. We suggest that the bulk of the molecular gas in SMM J16359+6612 may arise from an overlap region of two merging galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper reports an EVLA search for water megamaser emission in the strongly lensed z=2.517 submillimeter galaxy, finding no detection but setting a lensing-aided luminosity limit.

quotes.quote: Using the Expanded Very Large Array, we have conducted a search for 22.2 GHz H _2 O megamaser emission in the strongly lensed submm galaxy, SMM J16359+6612 at z=2.517. This object is lensed into three components, and after a correction for magnification is applied to its submm-wavelength flux density, it is typical of the bulk of the high-redshift, submm galaxy population responsible for the 850 m extragalactic background ( S_ 850 m 1 mJy). We do not detect any H _2 O megamaser emission, but the lensing allows us to place an interesting constraint on the luminosity of any megamasers present, L_ H_2 O < 5305 L_ for an assumed linewidth of 80 km s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper treats J16359 as a previous HCN-bright lensed DSFG, using its high HCN/CO ratio and double-peaked CO profile as comparison evidence for dense gas and merger state.

quotes.quote: The two extant HCN(1--0) detections in DSFGs seem to conform to this trend, with HCN/CO ratios of 0.11 (J16359) and 0.3 (SDP.9), higher than most ULIRGs.

The HCN-bright J16359 and SDP.9 are consistent with the Krumholz2007 ``intermediate'' model, however, for four DSFGs out of our sample, the ``normal'' model is preferred (HXMM.02 and J0209 have only very weak upper limits).
Encouragingly, J16359 shows a prominent double-peaked profile in CO(3--2) and higher-J lines IRAM Weiss2005 with a peak separation of 300 km s ^ 1 , indicative of a major merger.

gmw_00001361.name: Mrk 1456
gmw_00001361.overview: Mrk 1456 is a luminous, late-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0607385] with an Sc-type disk morphology [arXiv:0909.5651]. It features a star-forming nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0607385] and exhibits typical neutral hydrogen properties for its morphological type [arXiv:0909.5651].

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 1456 as a low-redshift spiral DLA candidate, measuring its small QSO impact parameter, strong Ca II absorption, Sc morphology, luminosity, SFR, and typical HI properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1456 has an impact parameter, b=4.9 kpc, compared with the galaxy's -band Petrosian radius, =7.1 kpc. Mrk 1456 and SDSS 21-00 were chosen for follow-up HI observations because they are strong DLA candidates that are within the redshift limits of HI 21 cm observations. koenig reported a Ca II K EW of 1.24 0.15 for Mrk 1456, well above the threshold for DLA candidates.

koenig reported on Mrk 1456 as a typical, L_* , giant spiral galaxy with a spectrum of an Sc type disk galaxy. Assuming M_B^* =-20.9 mar99 , Mrk 1456 and SDSS 21-00 are 0.5 L_B^* galaxies, and Mrk 1457 is a 1.4 L_B^* galaxy. We derive 3.1 0.2 and 5.6 1.2 M_ yr^ 1 for Mrk 1456 and SDSS 21-00, respectively, both in good agreement with the average SFRs for late-type spirals kenn83,kenn98 .
We find that both Mrk 1456 and SDSS 21-00 have typical HI properties, ie. HI mass, gas richness, gas mass fraction, for galaxies with their given morphological types rob,sal,broeils97 .

sources.summary: The paper presents Mrk 1456 as a luminous late-type spiral foreground to a QSO, with Ca II absorption, star-forming nucleus spectroscopy, and inferred central chemical abundances.

quotes.quote: The chance projection of the disk of Mrk 1456 onto a background QSO is similar to the case of SBS 1543+593/HS 1543+5921. Mrk 1456 is a luminous, late-type spiral at z 0.05 . Though the QSO, SDSS J114719.90+522923.2 at z 2 , has not yet been observed with ultraviolet spectroscopy, it shows strong Ca II absorption at the redshift of Mrk 1456 which gives evidence that it is a possible Damped Lyman Alpha absorber. Spectroscopy of the star-forming nucleus of Mrk 1456 allows us to apply emission-line diagnostics to infer the chemical abundances at the center of the galaxy, and to make a prediction of the expected metallicity on the sightline to the QSO.

gmw_00001362.name: SDSS J211701.26-002633.7
gmw_00001362.overview: SDSS J211701.26-002633.7 is an Sb-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:0909.5651] with a morphology characteristic of a late-type disk [arXiv:0909.5651]. It is a 0.5 L_B^* galaxy that exhibits a near-solar metallicity [arXiv:0909.5651].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J211701.26-002633.7 as a low-redshift spiral DLA candidate with a small impact parameter, strong Ca II absorption, near-solar metallicity, typical SFR, and high HI mass.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1456 and SDSS J211701.26-002633.7 (SDSS 21-00 for brevity) were selected from this set because the background QSOs are within the stellar disks of the galaxy. For SDSS 21-00, b=5.7 kpc while =7.5 kpc. For SDSS 21-00, we measure a rest Ca II K EW of 1.1 0.2 , also above threshold.

For SDSS 21-00, comparison of it's spectrum with the templates of kenn and using the inverse concentration index and u- color park05,shima01 suggests that it is an Sb type spiral galaxy. A morphological examination of the optical image for SDSS 21-00 shows it is a late-type disk galaxy. Assuming M_B^* =-20.9 mar99 , Mrk 1456 and SDSS 21-00 are 0.5 L_B^* galaxies, and Mrk 1457 is a 1.4 L_B^* galaxy.
We find that the O3N2 and N2 derived abundances are in good agreement with each other. Assuming a solar abundance of 8.74 0.08 from holweger , SDSS 21-00 has a metallicity near the solar value.

gmw_00001363.name: Mrk 1457
gmw_00001363.overview: Mrk 1457 is a Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:0909.5651] located 2.3 arcminutes south of Mrk 1456 [arXiv:0909.5651]. It has an inclination of 25 degrees [arXiv:0909.5651] and exhibits an unusually large gas-mass fraction compared to other Seyfert galaxies [arXiv:0909.5651].

sources.summary: The paper reports Mrk 1457 as a nearby Seyfert 2 companion in the GBT beam, deriving its inclination and luminosity and finding unusually high gas fraction for a Seyfert.

quotes.quote: The beam of our HI observations also included Mrk 1457 so we present the data for this galaxy as well. Mrk 1457 is classified as a Seyfert 2 galaxy in NED 2 NASA Extragalactic Database and lies 2.3'( 2.2 kpc) south of Mrk 1456. This gives 45 ^ , 25 ^ , and 52 ^ , for the inclinations of Mrk 1456, Mrk 1457, and SDSS 21-00 respectively. Assuming M_B^* =-20.9 mar99 , Mrk 1456 and SDSS 21-00 are 0.5 L_B^* galaxies, and Mrk 1457 is a 1.4 L_B^* galaxy.

Mrk 1457, when compared to other Seyferts, lies at the high end of the normal range of HI masses ( [ 0.2-9.0 ] x10 ^9M_ ) and appears to have an unusually large gas-mass fraction relative to the average f_ gas of 0.009 0.002 for Seyferts haan . Using the value of M_ HI ^ =6.3 10^9 from zwaan , Mrk 1456 and Mrk 1457 are slightly sub- M_ HI ^* galaxies, while SDSS 21-00 is twice an M_ HI ^* galaxy.

gmw_00001364.name: SDSS J0058 0722
gmw_00001364.overview: SDSS J0058 0722 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.87 [arXiv:1302.2663]. The system is a bright gravitationally lensed source that appears as a series of blue arcs around three early-type galaxies [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0058 0722 as a bright lensed source at z=1.87, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0058 0722, z=1.87: This system is composed of a number of blue arcs around three bright early type galaxies at wide separation ( 15 ). We detected the brightest source continuum flux from the arc to the west of the lenses. Redshift confirmation is achieved through identification of absorption lines (Si IV, Si II, CIV, Fe II, Al II) in the MMT blue channel spectrum.

gmw_00001365.name: SDSS J0105+0145
gmw_00001365.overview: SDSS J0105+0145 is a gravitationally lensed source located at a redshift of z=2.13 [arXiv:1302.2663]. A strong stellar continuum and absorption lines are observed in the spectrum of the arc behind the galaxy [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0105+0145 as a bright lensed source at z=2.13., reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0105+0145, z=2.13. Strong stellar continuum and prominent absorption lines are seen in the MMT blue channel spectrum of the arc behind SDSS J0105+0145 (Figure 2). We measure a redshift of z=2.126 from CII 1334 absorption.

gmw_00001366.name: SDSS J0143+1607
gmw_00001366.overview: SDSS J0143+1607 is a bright, strongly gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.50 [arXiv:1302.2663]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy reveal a strong continuum along with absorption features from CIV 1549 and Al II 1671 [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0143+1607 as a bright lensed source at z=1.50., reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0143+1607, z=1.50. Strong continuum is detected in the MMT blue channel spectrum, but some blending with the lens ( 2 arcseconds away from the centre of the slit) is present. Nevertheless we detect CIV 1549 in absorption as well as weak absorption corresponding to Al II 1671.

gmw_00001367.name: SDSS J0232 0323
gmw_00001367.overview: SDSS J0232 0323 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.51 [arXiv:1302.2663]. It is observed as a bright, strongly gravitationally lensed source that exhibits Lyman-alpha emission and interstellar absorption lines [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0232 0323 as a bright lensed source at z=2.51, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0232 0323, z=2.51 As seen in Figure 1, the MMT slit targeting SDSS J0232 0323 covers the source in two locations, to the NW and SE of the lens. We detect Ly emission and interstellar absorption lines from the source on both sides (Figure 2). The escaping Ly radiation has a mean redshift of z_ Ly =2.515.

gmw_00001368.name: SDSS J0800+0812
gmw_00001368.overview: SDSS J0800+0812 is a bright gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.41 [arXiv:1302.2663]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy reveal absorbing gas at z=1.407 and strong [OII] emission at z=1.408 [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0800+0812 as a bright lensed source at z=1.41, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0800+0812, z=1.41: Both blue and red channel data were obtained for SDSS J0800+0812, providing spectral coverage between 3600 and 9070 . Absorption from Fe II 2600 and Mg II 2796,2803 is seen in the MMT red channel spectra, indicating a redshift of z=1.407 for the absorbing gas. Additionally, we detect strong [ OII ] emission (rest-frame EW of 60 ) at 8983 , revealing a nebular redshift of z=1.408 assuming that the rest-frame centroid of the unresolved [ OII ] doublet is 3728.3 .

gmw_00001369.name: SDSS J0807+4410
gmw_00001369.overview: SDSS J0807+4410 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.54 [arXiv:1302.2663]. It is observed as a bright gravitationally lensed arc that exhibits a weak stellar continuum, Lyman-alpha absorption, and multiple interstellar absorption lines [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0807+4410 as a bright lensed source at z=2.54, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0807+4410, z=2.54: We detect weak stellar continuum, Ly absorption, and multiple interstellar absorption lines in our MMT blue channel spectrum of the faint arc around SDSSJ0807+4410. The low ionisation absorbing gas has a mean redshift of z=2.540 in the MMT spectra. Its close proximity to the lens ( 3 arcsec to the centre of the elliptical galaxy) results in some blending at wavelengths greater than 5500 .

gmw_00001370.name: SDSS J0846+0446
gmw_00001370.overview: SDSS J0846+0446, also known as CSWA 141, is a strongly gravitationally lensed galaxy at a redshift of z=1.43 [arXiv:1302.2663, arXiv:1805.01484]. The system is characterized by low metallicity, low stellar mass, a high specific star formation rate, and strong high-ionization nebular emission lines [arXiv:1302.2663, arXiv:1805.01484].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0846+0446 as a bright lensed source at z=1.43, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0846+0446, z=1.43: Redshift confirmation was first achieved through the detection of numerous emission lines with DEIMOS. In addition to [ OII ] and [ Ne III ] 3869, we identified Hydrogen Balmer series lines H , H8, H9, H10, H11, H12. Subsequent infrared observations with Magellan/FIRE yielded many further rest-optical emission features, including the temperature-sensitive [ OIII ] 4363 auroral line (Figure 3) and the electron density sensitive [ SII ] emission lines. We derived a nebular redshift of z=1.425 from the observed hydrogen Balmer emission lines in the FIRE spectrum. The observed spectrum points to emission from a very young and metal-poor system.

sources.summary: The paper treats CSWA 141 as a lensed high-redshift galaxy with smoothed spectra, limited velocity coverage, low metallicity, and strong high-ionization emission.

quotes.quote: This results in final FWHM spectral resolution of 85 \ for CSWA 141, 102 \ for CSWA 40, and 157 \ for CSWA 2, improving the signal-to-noise ratio to 6--8 in each case.
The adopted velocity ranges encompass a median 73 of the velocity-integrated covering fraction, varying from 63--85 for individual sources (except CSWA 141 where the fraction is likely < 50 ).

CSWA 141 is an outlier in terms of having strong high-ionization nebular emission lines and low metallicity (as well as low stellar mass and high sSFR), with [ O/H ] =-0.7 measured via the direct method from 4363 stark2013 .

gmw_00001371.name: SDSS J0854+1008
gmw_00001371.overview: SDSS J0854+1008 is a high-redshift galaxy system that acts as a bright gravitationally lensed source [arXiv:1302.2663]. Spectroscopic observations of the system have detected resolved oxygen emission lines at redshifts of 1.44 and 1.27 [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0854+1008 as a bright lensed source at z=1.44, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0854+1008, z=1.44 and z=1.27. DEIMOS observations of the SDSS J0854+1008 system yielded detections of [ OII ] emission at two different redshifts. We detect resolved [ OII ] emission from the blue arc to the NE of the lens (see Figure 1), yielding a nebular redshift of z=1.271 .

gmw_00001372.name: SDSS J0921+1810
gmw_00001372.overview: SDSS J0921+1810 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.49 [arXiv:1302.2663]. It is observed as a bright, strongly gravitationally lensed source that exhibits resolved [OII] doublet emission [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0921+1810 as a bright lensed source at z=1.49, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0921+1810 z=1.49: Redshift confirmation is achieved through detection of [ OII ] emission in both the DEIMOS and MMT red channel spectra. The doublet is fully resolved in the 1200 line/mm grating DEIMOS spectrum. We derive a nebular redshift of z=1.486 from the centroids of the [ OII ] doublet.

gmw_00001373.name: SDSS J1002+6020
gmw_00001373.overview: SDSS J1002+6020 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a confirmed redshift of z=1.114 [arXiv:1302.2663]. It is observed as a bright gravitationally lensed source that exhibits detectable [OII], H-beta, and Ne III 3869 emission lines [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1002+6020 as a bright lensed source at z=1.11, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1002+6020, z=1.11: We detect [ OII ] emission in each of the three spectra taken of the arc lensed by the galaxy SDSS J1002+6020. Based on the wavelength of [ OII ] emission, we confirm a redshift of z=1.114 for the source. We detect emission at the wavelength of H and Ne III 3869 in the MMT red channel spectrum.

gmw_00001374.name: SDSS J1009+1937
gmw_00001374.overview: SDSS J1009+1937 is a bright, strongly gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.16 [arXiv:1302.2663]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy reveal Lyman-alpha emission alongside Silicon II and Carbon IV absorption lines [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1009+1937 as a bright lensed source at z=2.16, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1009+1937, z=2.16: Ly emission is present at 3850 \ in the MMT blue channel spectrum, indicating a Ly redshift of 2.167. While the continuum S/N is not as strong as in other sources, we confirm the presence of the Si II 1526, and CIV 1549 absorption lines. The CIV centroid is consistent with a redshift z=2.158 .

gmw_00001375.name: SDSS J1110+6459
gmw_00001375.overview: SDSS J1110+6459 is a strongly lensed galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=2.48 [arXiv:1707.00704, arXiv:1302.2663]. Although it appears as a smooth exponential disk under normal resolution, gravitational lensing reveals that its star formation is concentrated in two dozen sub-100 pc clumps [arXiv:1707.00704].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes this z=2.481 lensed galaxy, showing lensing reveals two dozen sub-100 pc star-forming clumps that would appear as a smooth exponential disk without magnification.

quotes.quote: Ellis Ave., Chicago, IL 60637 7 Kavli Institute for Cosmological Physics, University of Chicago, 5640 South Ellis Ave., Chicago, IL 60637 8 Space Telescope Science Institute, 3700 San Martin Dr., Baltimore, MD 21218 9 Department of Physics, Massachusetts Institute of Technology 10 ArmenTeKort, Antwerp, Belgium For lensed galaxy \ at redshift z=2.481 , which is magnified by a factor of , we analyze the morphology of star formation as traced by rest-frame ultraviolet emission, in both the highly-magnified source plane, and in simulations of how this galaxy would appear without lensing magnification. Were this galaxy not lensed but drawn from an \ deep field, we would conclude that almost all its star formation arises from an exponential disk ( \ index of 1.0 0.4 ) with an effective radius of r_e = 2.7 0.3 kpc measured from two-dimensional fitting to F606W using Galfit, and r_e = 1.9 0.1 kpc measured by fitting a radial profile to F606W elliptical isophotes. At the normal spatial resolution of the deep fields, there is no sign of clumpy star formation within . However, the enhanced spatial resolution enabled by gravitational lensing tells a very different story: much of the star formation arises in two dozen clumps with sizes of \ spread across the \ length of the galaxy.

The color and spatial distribution of the diffuse component suggests that still smaller clumps are unresolved. Despite this clumpy, messy morphology, the radial profile is still well-characterized by an exponential profile. In this lensed galaxy, stars are forming in complexes with sizes well below 100 pc; such sizes are wholly unexplored by surveys of galaxy evolution at 1<z<3 . galaxies: star formation --- gravitational lensing: strong --- ultraviolet: galaxies The diffraction limit of the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ), / D = 0.033 \ and 0.052 \ at wavelengths of 3900 \ and 6060 , corresponds to physical scales of 270 pc and 420 pc at z=2.5 .

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1110+6459 as a bright lensed source at z=2.48, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1110+6459, z=2.48: Ly emission is clearly visible in the MMT spectrum of SDSS J1110+6459. The equivalent width is moderate in strength (rest-frame EW 3 ) with a redshift of z=2.483 . We detect absorption from OI+SiII 1303, CII 1334, and Al II 1671 with a mean redshift of z=2.476 .

gmw_00001376.name: SDSS J1111+5308
gmw_00001376.overview: SDSS J1111+5308 is a bright, gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.95 [arXiv:1302.2663]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy reveal strong continuum emission and low-ionization absorbing gas at a mean redshift of z=1.946 [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1111+5308 as a bright lensed source at z=1.95, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1111+5308, z=1.95: Strong continuum and absorption are detected in the MMT blue channel spectrum, as is seen in Figure 2. The mean redshift for the low ionisation absorbing gas is z=1.946 . We detect absorption at 4245 \ which would correspond to 1441 \ if in the rest-frame of the lensed source toward SDSS J1111+5308.

gmw_00001377.name: SDSS J1115+1645
gmw_00001377.overview: SDSS J1115+1645 is a bright gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.72 [arXiv:1302.2663]. Spectroscopic observations of a bright blue knot within the galaxy have confirmed this redshift through the detection of H-beta, H-alpha, and [OII] emission lines [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1115+1645 as a bright lensed source at z=1.72, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1115+1645, z=1.72: Our initial MMT optical spectra of this target suffered contamination from a foreground red galaxy (see postage stamp Figure 1), making redshift confirmation difficult. After placing the FIRE slit directly on the bright blue knot to the SW of the lens, we were able to confirm the systemic redshift as z=1.717 through detection of H , H , and [ OII ] emission. Emission from [ OIII ] 5007 and 4959 falls in the low transmission region between the J and H-bands and is not detected.

gmw_00001378.name: SDSS J1138+2754
gmw_00001378.overview: SDSS J1138+2754 is a bright gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.91 [arXiv:1302.2663]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy reveal extended emission from both [OII] and [Ne III] [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1138+2754 as a bright lensed source at z=0.91, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1138+2754 z=0.91: Extended [ OII ] emission is detected in both blue and red channel spectra, revealing the source redshift as z=0.909 , as previously reported in Wutys et al. (2012). We measure a rest-frame [ OII ] EW of 60 , similar to SDSS J0800+0812. We also detect extended [ Ne III ] in the red channel spectrum.

gmw_00001379.name: SDSS J1147+3331
gmw_00001379.overview: SDSS J1147+3331 is a bright gravitationally lensed source located at a redshift of z=1.21 [arXiv:1302.2663]. Its redshift was confirmed through the detection of [OII] emission, although blending with its nearby lens complicates the characterization of its faint stellar continuum [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1147+3331 as a bright lensed source at z=1.21, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1147+3331, z=1.21: Redshift confirmation ( z=1.205 ) was obtained through detection of [ OII ] emission with our MMT red channel spectrum. The brightness of the lens ( z=16.1 ) and its reasonably close proximity to the source (see Figure 1) cause significant blending in the MMT spectrum. While we detect faint stellar continuum (particularly in the blue channel data), it is difficult to reliably characterise absorption lines as a result of the blending.

gmw_00001380.name: SDSS J1156+1911
gmw_00001380.overview: SDSS J1156+1911 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.54 [arXiv:1302.2663]. It is observed as a bright, strongly gravitationally lensed source that exhibits a strong continuum with several absorption features [arXiv:1302.2663].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1156+1911 as a bright lensed source at z=1.54, reporting spectroscopic emission or absorption features that establish its redshift and characterize its observed gas or continuum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1156+1911, z=1.54: Strong continuum in the MMT blue channel spectrum. We detect absorption features at 3870 , 3927, 4076, and 4236 . The most likely classification of these features is Si II 1526, CIV 1549, Fe II 1608, and Al I I 1671, respectively.

gmw_00001381.name: SDSS J1237+5533
gmw_00001381.overview: SDSS J1237+5533 is a gravitationally lensed, star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.87 [arXiv:2402.00942, arXiv:1302.2663]. It exhibits a clumpy, tadpole-like morphology with a bright head and tail, hosting a young galactic wind that drives spatially resolved outflows [arXiv:2402.00942].

sources.summary: The paper studies CSWA13 as a lensed star-forming galaxy at z=1.87, mapping its clumpy morphology, nebular kinematics, and young galactic wind with KCWI spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: We study the spatially resolved outflow properties of CSWA13, an intermediate mass ( M_*=10^ 9 ), gravitationally lensed star-forming galaxy at z=1.87 . We use Keck/KCWI to map outflows in multiple rest-frame ultraviolet ISM absorption lines, along with fluorescent Si 2 * emission, and nebular emission from C 3 ] tracing the local systemic velocity. The spatial structure of outflow velocity mirrors that of the nebular kinematics, which we interpret to be a signature of a young galactic wind that is pressurizing the ISM of the galaxy but is yet to burst out.

Our target is CSWA13, a bright star-forming galaxy discovered as part of the Cambridge And Sloan Survey Of Wide ARcs in the skY CASSOWARY; belokurov2009 . The lensing nature of this system was spectroscopically confirmed by danstark-cswa-confirmation with redshifts of z_d=0.41 for the foreground deflector galaxy and z_s=1.87 for the background bright arc. An analysis of the stellar populations and UV nebular emission of this target is presented by ramesh_CSWA . Here, we use integral field spectroscopy from the Keck Cosmic Web Imager KCWI; KCWI_Morrissey_2018 at the W. M. Keck Observatory to spatially map the outflowing gas from this galaxy. This paper is organized as follows. Section sec:kcwi-observations describes the KCWI observations and data reduction. Section sec:lensmodel describes the lens model used to obtain accurate intrinsic properties of the source galaxy. The spectroscopic analysis methodology is described in Section sec:methodology . We discuss the results in Section sec:results-and-discussion with conclusions in Section sec:conclusions . Throughout this work, we assume a flat CDM cosmology with H_0 = 70 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 and _ m = 0.3 . We observed CSWA13 with KCWI on 06 April 2022 with the BL grating and Medium slicer configuration. The total wavelength coverage is 3229--5825 \ corresponding to 1127--2032 \ in the z=1.87 galaxy rest frame, with spectral resolution of 2.4 \ FWHM ( R 1800 ). The total exposure time was 2 hours, with six exposures of 1200 seconds each. Three exposures were taken at each of two orthogonal position angles (PA) of 0 and 90 degrees, both of which covered the entire lens system (Figure fig:CSWA13-system-intro ). The observing conditions ranged from clear to thin clouds with seeing of 1-1 3 FWHM. For sky subtraction, a nearby blank sky reference area was observed for 300 seconds with each set of PA exposures. The data were reduced using the IDL version of the KCWI data reduction pipeline (KDERP-v1.0.2) www.github.com/Keck-DataReductionPipelines/KcwiDRP . We initially ran stages 1--4 of the pipeline, which perform bias, gain, and dark current corrections as well as cosmic ray rejection. We use the observed sky frame for sky subtraction in stage 5 of the pipeline, as there is insufficient blank sky background in the lensing field of CSWA13 itself. Flux calibrated and DAR (differential atmospheric refraction) corrected datacubes are obtained by running stages 6--8. Flux calibration was carried out using observations of the standard star BD26D2606 taken on 20 June 2020 using the same instrumental setup. The flux calibration is uncertain due to the non-photometric conditions, but the analysis and results of this paper do not require absolute flux calibration. We correct for any nonzero residual background in each wavelength slice in the following way: we consider the median flux within a 48 \ wavelength bin centered on each slice, mask out regions containing detected sources, fit the resultant background profile with a 2D first-order polynomial, and remove its contribution from the 2D slice. This is similar to the procedure described in Mortensen2021 , which was applied to a narrow wavelength range, while here we are interested in features that span the entire wavelength range of the KCWI spectra. The processed datacubes were then resampled to a common grid, aligned, averaged, and rebinned to a spatial pixel size of 0 3 0 3. We also converted the pipeline output from air to vacuum wavelengths. We use the 1 standard deviation of flux from blank regions of the sky to estimate the uncertainty of our spectra. Figure fig:CSWA13-system-intro shows a color composite image generated using HumVI phil-humvi from the reduced KCWI data cube by summing three broadband wavelength regions centered at 3730, 4430 and 5130 , each with a width of 400 . Multiple images of the arc are clearly identifiable by their blue color in the KCWI data, as well as prominent spectral features. Figure fig:CSWA13-system-intro (Left panel) also shows the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) near-infrared image obtained with WFC3-IR using the F140W filter. We identify three distinct star-forming complexes in the HST image (A, B, and C), which are multiply imaged across the entire arc. The integrated KCWI spectrum from summing spaxels containing the arc is shown in Figure fig:spectra-arc (a) for the rest-frame wavelength range 1200--1930 . Metal absorption lines from three intervening galaxy systems at z=1.67, 1.69 , and 1.74 are also detected in the arc spectra, with prominent C 4 and other features. We mask their contribution in all the analyses presented in the rest of this paper. Gravitational lens modeling is necessary to reconstruct the galaxy's source plane morphology and intrinsic properties. We use the lens modeling software lenstronomy Birrer18, Birrer21b to build the lens model. We adopt the conjugate point modeling method which is commonly used in cluster-scale lens modeling see and references therein Kneib11 , with multiple image positions for the A, B, and C components of the source (Figure fig:CSWA13-system-intro ). Our KCWI data confirm the multiple image nature of components A, B, and C, for example via their distinct velocity structure and \ profiles. The lens model is optimized with the nested sampler dynesty Speagle19 . Our lens model consists of an elliptical Navarro--Frenk--White NFW; Navarro96, Navarro97 halo profile for the lensing galaxy group, a double Chameleon profile for the stellar mass distribution of the central galaxy Dutton11 , a point mass to account for the supermassive black hole (SMBH) at the center of this galaxy, eight singular isothermal ellipsoid profiles to model the eight brightest galaxies within 20 arcsec of the central galaxy Kassiola93 , a residual shear field and a residual flexion field. The ellipticity of the projected NFW halo is parameterized in the convergence or the surface density and not in the potential Oguri21 . The Chameleon profile is a combination of two non-singular isothermal ellipsoids that provide a good approximation to the S ersic profile within 0.5 - 3R_ eff , where R_ eff is the half-light radius Sersic68, Dutton11 . We find that a superposition of two S ersic profiles is necessary to fit the light distribution of the central galaxy well Claeskens06, Suyu13 . We convert the best-fit parameters of the double S ersic profile into the parameters of the double Chameleon profile. The pre-fitted parameters determining the angular and radial shape of the double Chameleon profile are fixed during the lens model optimization, and only the mass-to-light ratio is free. We fix the NFW halo mass to be 10^ 14 M_ and impose a Gaussian prior on the concentration parameter ( c_ 200 = 5.0 0.8 ) following the results of Newman15 for group-scale halos. The ellipticity and centroid of the NFW halo are fixed to be the same as the ellipticity and centroid, respectively, of the central galaxy's light distribution. Thus, the concentration is the only free parameter for the NFW profile. We impose a prior on the stellar mass to SMBH mass relation using the results from Li23 . For the eight nearby galaxies included explicitly in the model, we use the photutils package to measure aperture photometry and ellipticity. We fix the centroid and ellipticity of these galaxies and only allow the Einstein radii of each galaxy as free parameters. The residual shear (also called ``external shear'' in the literature) has two free parameters (i.e., the shear magnitude _ shear and angle _ shear ) and the residual flexion field has four parameters. These residual shear and flexion fields account for both the ``internal'' angular structure of the central lensing galaxy that is not fully captured by the ellipticity parametrization of the NFW and Chameleon profiles, and also ``external'' contributions from mass structure beyond the galaxies, which are explicitly accounted for in our model. The residual flexion field is also necessary to correctly reproduce the atypical image configuration of the multiple components in this system.
