Finally, if the N/O is constant at spatial scales of the order of several kpc, as our analysis indicates, we conclude that the number of WR stars estimated from spectroscopy, is not sufficient to pollute the ISM and to produce the observed N/O excess in these objects.

gmw_00001416.name: Mrk 930
gmw_00001416.overview: Mrk 930 is a blue compact dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1105.4516, arXiv:1107.0583] in the advanced stages of a merger [arXiv:1208.5461]. It exhibits a perturbed morphology [arXiv:1105.4516] and hosts a starburst episode that began within the last 30 to 40 million years [arXiv:1107.0583].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 930 as a disturbed, nitrogen-enhanced blue compact dwarf with southern star formation, super star clusters, WR evidence, and possible interaction signatures.

quotes.quote: HS 0128+2832 is a compact galaxy studied by Izotov Thuan (2004), who derived a low O abundance [ 12+log(O/H) = 8.12 ] combined with an elevated abundance of N [ log(N/O) = -1.23 ] . sources.summary: MK930 is classified as an iI,M Wolf-Rayet BCG in an advanced merger, with an old stellar population, nebular contamination, and likely dust effects.

quotes.quote: MK930 This is a Wolf-Rayet galaxy 1999A&AS..136...35S whose multiple nuclei e.g. 2004AJ....128...62G indicate the advanced stages of a merger. We therefore classify it as an iI,M BCG. The presence of an old ( 4 Gyr) stellar population can be deduced from all color profiles. Nebular emission is implied by all color--color diagrams, but at the same time neither of the models is well--suited for the burst, total, or central mixed colors, indicating either a prolonged burst, or severe dust effects. The latter seems more likely since non-negligible amounts of warm dust are present in this galaxy 2008ApJ...676..970W .

sources.summary: Mrk 930 is treated as a luminous blue compact host whose starburst began within 30-40 Myr; its optically bright young clusters include partially embedded systems with near-infrared excess.

quotes.quote: The age distributions of the YSCs suggest that the starburst episode in Haro 11, ESO 185-IG13, and Mrk 930 started not more than 30-40 Myr ago.

In all the three host galaxies, the analysis is limited to the optically brightest objects, i.e., systems that are only partially embedded by their natal cocoons (since deeply embedded clusters are probably too faint to be detected).

A significant fraction of young star clusters in Haro 11 2010MNRAS.407..870A , ESO 185-IG13 2011MNRAS.414.1793A , and Mrk 930 2011MNRAS.tmp..739A shows a clear signature of a flux excess at near-IR wavebands.

gmw_00001417.name: J0131 0321
gmw_00001417.overview: J0131 0321 is an optically luminous, radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z=5.18 [arXiv:2006.13762]. It exhibits a single compact radio component, a moderate Doppler boosting factor, and flux density variation that suggests it may be a blazar [arXiv:2006.13762].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0131 0321 as an optically luminous z=5.18 radio-loud quasar with high black-hole mass, compact EVN emission, variability, Doppler boosting, and blazar-like evidence.

quotes.quote: J0131 0321 This is an optically luminous radio-loud quasar at z=5.18 discovered by Yi2014 using the Lijiang 2.4 m and Magellan telescopes. These authors measured the BH mass to be 2.7 10^9 M_ with an i band magnitude of 18.47 mag. It is the second most luminous object in our sample with 1.4 GHz flux density of 33.69 0.12 mJy from the VLA FIRST ( Helfand2015 ; observation date: 2009 March 21; angular resolution: 5 4 ).

Ghisellini2015 conducted a spectral energy distribution (SED) fit with an accretion disk, a torus, and a jet to constrain the viewing angle of the jet, which is very close to the line of sight (e.g., 3 5 ). Gabanyi2015 observed this target on 2014 December 2 using EVN VLBI at 1.7 GHz. They found a single compact radio component with a flux density of 64.40 0.30 mJy, and estimated a relatively high brightness temperature ( T_ B 2.8 10^ 11 K) and a moderate Doppler boosting factor ( 6 ). In addition, these authors noted a significant flux density variation by comparing the FIRST 1.4 GHz and VLBI 1.7 GHz measurements, which together suggest that this object may be a blazar.

gmw_00001418.name: J0741+2520
gmw_00001418.overview: J0741+2520 is a radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z=5.194 [arXiv:2006.13762]. Discovered by combining FIRST and SDSS data, the source has been detected as an unresolved radio continuum source at 323 MHz [arXiv:2006.13762].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0741+2520 as a z=5.194 radio-loud quasar discovered from FIRST and SDSS data and detected as an unresolved 323 MHz GMRT source.

quotes.quote: SDSS J074154.72+252029.6 (hereafter J0741+2520) McGreer2009 discovered this target by combining data from the FIRST radio survey and the SDSS. The i band magnitude is 18.45 mag at z=5.194 . We detected the 323 MHz radio continuum emission for this target with a peak flux density of 1.73 0.09 mJy beam ^ 1 . It is an unresolved target.

gmw_00001419.name: J0836+0054
gmw_00001419.overview: SDSS J0836+0054 is a radio-loud quasar at a redshift of approximately 5.8 [arXiv:2006.13762, arXiv:2111.05446] that exhibits a compact, jetted radio structure [arXiv:2101.05585] and variable radio flux [arXiv:2006.13762, arXiv:1609.00575]. It is situated within a dense galaxy structure [arXiv:2111.05446] and displays an excess of X-ray emission [arXiv:2211.13820].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0836+0054 as a z=5.82 quasar with variable radio flux, compact VLBI detections, central radio emission within 40 pc, and unresolved GMRT emission.

quotes.quote: SDSS J083643.85+005453.3 (hereafter J0836+0054) Fan2001 selected this target from multicolor imaging data of SDSS. It has a strong and broad yet partially absorbed Ly emission line, which indicates a redshift of 5.82. Stern2003 observed this target using FLAMINGOS on the Gemini-South 8 m telescope and calculated a slightly lower redshift of 5.774 from broad emission lines of C iv 1549 and C iii ] 1909 . The 1.4 GHz flux density is 1.11 0.15 mJy from FIRST ( Helfand2015 ; observation date: 1998 August; angular resolution: 5 4 ), 2.5 0.5 mJy from NVSS ( Condon1998 ; observation date: 1993 November 15; angular resolution: 45 ), 1.75 0.04 mJy from VLA A-configuration ( Petric2003 ; observation date: 2002 March 6; angular resolution: 1 5 ), and 1.96 0.31 mJy from VLA B-configuration ( Frey2005 ; observation date: 2003 October 5; angular resolution: 6 ).

This source shows significant variability through 10 years of monitoring. Frey2003 carried out VLBI 1.6 GHz observations at 10 mas angular resolution and found this target to be compact. Frey2005 also observed this source at mas resolution with the VLBI Network at 5 GHz, which confirms that it is a compact source with a flux density of 0.34 0.04 mJy. In addition, these authors also conducted an almost simultaneous VLA B-configuration, arcsecond resolution observations at 5 GHz ( f_ =0.43 0.06 mJy) toward this target. By comparing the 5 GHz radio emission from observations at two different resolutions, the authors constrained the radio emission to arise from within the central 40 pc. This target is unresolved by our 323 MHz GMRT observations and has a peak flux density of 1.94 0.17 mJy beam ^ 1 . The low surface brightness 6 tail-like structure showing in the Figure GMRT323 of this target may be due to a low-redshift radio source in the foreground at a projected distance of 10 , which is resolved by Frey2005 .

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J0836+0054/J0836 as a z=5.8 radio-loud quasar in a dense galaxy structure, with a young compact radio source and massive black hole.

quotes.quote: Here we present new evidence that the radio-loud quasar SDSS J0836+0054 at z=5.8 could be part of a surprisingly rich structure of galaxies. This conclusion is reached by combining a number of findings previously reported in the literature. bosman20 obtained the redshifts of three companion galaxies, confirming an overdensity of dropouts found by zheng06 . By comparing this structure with those found near other quasars and large overdense regions in the field at z 6-7 , we show that the SDSS J0836+0054 field is among the densest structures known at these redshifts.

J0836 is also one of the most distant known radio-loud quasars: it has a flux density of 1.1 mJy at 1.4 GHz, a 5 GHz (rest-frame) luminosity of 1.1 10^ 25 W Hz ^ 1 sr ^ 1 , a radio spectral index of -0.9 between 1.5 and 5 GHz, and is marginally resolved at VLBI resolutions of 10 mas ( 40 pc) petric03,frey03,frey05 . wolf21 presented new observations at low radio frequency, showing that the radio spectral index flattens significantly below 1.4 GHz, consistent with a peaked radio spectrum. The compact radio size, steep spectral index and the evidence for a peaked spectrum may suggest that this is a young radio source in which the jets are still confined to (sub-)kpc scales. J0836 has an estimated black hole mass of log _ 10 ( M _ BH /M_ )=9.48 0.55 (see Sect. sec:quasar below).

sources.summary: The paper observes SDSS J0836+0054 as a z=5.774 radio-loud quasar, finds compact VLBI-scale radio emission, and derives an improved astrometric position.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The equatorial coordinates determined for J0836+0054 are _ J2000 = 8^ h 36^ m 43 86020 and _ J2000 = +0 54 53 2290 .

quotes.quote: We observed SDSS J0836+0054, the most distant radio-loud quasar known at present ( z=5.774 ) with the European Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) Network at the 5 GHz frequency on 2003 November 4. The source is compact at the milli-arcsecond (mas) angular scale with a flux density of 0.34 mJy. The observations with the Very Large Array (VLA) made on the consecutive day support a conclusion that the radio emission from SDSS J0836+0054 is essentially confined within the central 40 pc. Based on our phase-referenced VLBI observation, we obtained the astrometric position of the source accurate to 2 mas.

SDSSp J083643.85+005453.3 (J0836+0054 in short) is one of the highest redshift quasars known to date discovered using the multicolor imaging data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS, Fan et al. \ ) with a redshift z=5.82 . A more accurate redshift value of z=5.774 was determined using near-infrared spectroscopy by Stern et al. \ ( ). The object is one of the twelve z>5.7 quasars found in the SDSS to date (Fan et al. \ ). Among these, J0836+0054 is the only one identified with a previously known radio source with a flux density of 1 mJy at 1.4 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J083643.85+005453.3 as a z=5.81 eROSITA quasar, compares its Chandra and eROSITA X-ray fluxes, and reports compact radio emission evidence.

quotes.quote: We report the X-ray detection of eFEDSU J083644.0+005459, an eROSITA source securely matched to the well-known quasar SDSS J083643.85+005453.3 (z=5.81). The soft X-ray flux of the source derived from eROSITA is consistent with previous Chandra observations. The detection of SDSS J083643.85+005453.3 allows us to place the first constraints on the XLF at z>5.5 based on a secure spectroscopic redshift.

We report here the blind detection of a high-redshift X-ray source (eFEDSU J083644.0+005459), that we could identify as the well-known quasar SDSS J083643.85+005453.3 (z=5.81, fan01 , henceforth SDSSJ08). This quasar was initially discovered by i band dropout selection in the main Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS). CFHQJ14 and SDSSJ08 are the highest-redshift X-ray-selected AGNs known to date. Taking advantage of the synergy between the eROSITA detection and new radio data from the LOw-Frequency Array LOFAR; vanhaarlem13 and the Australian Square Kilometre Array Pathfinder ASKAP, Hotan et al. submitted johnston08 Survey With ASKAP of GAMA-09 + X-Ray (SWAG-X, Moss et al. in prep) programme, we investigate the origin of the X-ray emission from SDSSJ08 and find further evidence for a confined jetted radio structure.

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSSp J083643.85+005453.3 as a newly discovered z=5.82 quasar with broad Ly and NV emission, strong Ly forest absorption, and radio emission.

quotes.quote: We describe the discovery of three new quasars, SDSSp J083643.85+005453.3 ( z=5.82 ), SDSSp J130608.26+035626.3 ( z=5.99 ) and SDSSp J103027.10+052455.0 ( z=6.28 ). The quasar SDSSp J083643.85+005453.3 is a radio source with flux of 1.1 mJy at 20 cm. The spectra of all three quasars show strong and broad Ly +NV emission lines, and very strong Ly forest absorption, with a mean continuum decrement D_A > 0.90 .

The three new quasars, with redshifts of 5.82, 5.99 and 6.28, are the highest-redshift objects with spectral confirmation known to date. The spectra of the new quasars show very strong Ly absorption blueward of the Ly emission, with more than 90 of the flux absorbed. The amount of absorption increases rapidly toward z_ abs 6 .
The black hole masses of these quasars are probably several times 10^9 M_ . The quasars are likely to reside in very massive systems, with the minimum mass of host dark halos 10^ 13 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper presents EVN imaging of the z=5.82 radio quasar SDSS 0836+0054, finding a compact slightly resolved radio source, no multiple lensing, and a precise VLBI position.

quotes.quote: The object SDSSp J083643.85+005453.3 (hereafter: SDSS 0836+0054) is the highest redshift radio-detected quasar known to date ( z=5.82 ) discovered using multicolor imaging data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) fan01 . The object is one of the seven z>5.7 quasars known. Six of them form a complete colour-selected flux-limited sample from an area of 2870 deg ^2 fan03 .

The quasar SDSS 0836+0054 is clearly detected with VLBI and appears somewhat resolved at a linear resolution of 70 pc (Fig. target ). However, the apparent structural extension may be due to the remaining residual phase errors after phase-reference calibration. As seen in Fig. target , the position of SDSS 0836+0054 differs from the optical position reported by fan01 , but is within the given error of 200 mas in both right ascension and declination. The optical position was used in the correlation of our VLBI data as the phase center.

sources.summary: The paper reports EVN and VLA observations of SDSS J0836+0054, detecting a compact high-redshift radio-loud quasar, finding no multiple gravitational lensing, and confirming a steep radio spectrum with likely 5-GHz VLBI detectability.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0836+0054, the most distant radio-loud quasar known to date ( z=5.774 ) is clearly detected with EVN observations at 1.6 GHz in 2002. The radio structure is compact at 10 mas angular scale, with a flux density of 1.1 mJy, close to the total flux density value. We found that the source is unlikely to be multiply imaged by gravitational lensing.

The 5-GHz EVN observation of the quasar made in 2003 is being processed now. Nearly contemporaneous VLA observations confirm the steep radio spectrum of the source. The flux density measured with the VLA (0.43 mJy), and the compact structure make the VLBI detection probable at 5 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper fits J0836+0054 as steep-spectrum and possibly ultra-steep, discussing flux-density discrepancies as likely resolution effects rather than variability.

quotes.quote: J0836+0054 Fitting the spectrum of J0836+0054 (Fig. fig:J0836+0054 ) with a power law gives a spectral index of =-0.89 0.29 . This indicates that the source can be a USS source within the uncertainties. J0836+0054 has 1.4 GHz FIRST and NVSS flux densities of 1.11 0.06 mJy and 2.5 0.5 mJy, respectively. In addition, PCB2003 found a 1.4 GHz flux density of 1.75 0.04 mJy during their observations with the VLA at a resolution of 1.5 arcsec. Since the PCB2003 observations have a higher resolution than FIRST, and a 60 per cent higher flux density, this, along with the flux density difference between FIRST and NVSS, could indicate that J0836+0054 is variable.

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J0836+0054 as the other eFEDS high-redshift quasar, noting its X-ray excess and its role in constraining bright-end high-redshift XLF models.

quotes.quote: Using the same method, we also derived a bolometric correction for the second high-redshift quasar detected in eFEDS, SDSS J0836+0054, from the photometry presented in wolf21 . This source also shows X-ray emission excess.

The detection of SDSS J0836+0054 is consistent with extrapolated models of the XLF from literature. Models with a milder decline in AGN space density in the highest X-ray luminosity bins are favoured by this detection. These findings are corroborated by an analysis of a sample of X-ray-detected high-redshift AGN in the ExSeSS by barlow_hall22 .

sources.summary: The paper compares prior near-infrared observations and redshift measurements for J0836+0054, noting that a corrected C III] redshift differs from its systemic redshift estimate.

quotes.quote: J0836+0054 This QSO was observed by ste03 . From the C 3 1909 line, ste03 derive a corrected Using the 224 km s ^ 1 velocity difference with [ O III ] 5007 found by vdb01 redshift of z = 5.774 0.003 . This is quite different from our systemic redshift of z_ sys = 5.808 0.007 . fre03 measure an uncorrected C 3 ] 1909 and Mg 2 redshift of z = 5.82 from their low resolution NICMOS spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper treats J0836+0054 as a variable radio source, comparing FIRST, NVSS, VLA, and predicted flux densities to distinguish variability from extended emission.

quotes.quote: The FIRST and NVSS flux densities of J0836+0054 are 1.11 0.06 mJy and 2.5 0.5 mJy, respectively. This could indicate that J0836+0054 is extended at angular scales beyond 5 arcsec. However, 2003AJ....126...15P observed J0836+0054 with the VLA in A configuration at 1.4 GHz with a resolution of 1.5 arcsec and found a flux density of 1.75 0.04 mJy. Considering that the 2003AJ....126...15P observations have higher resolution than FIRST and show 60 per cent higher flux density, we conclude that J0836+0054 is variable.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0836+0054 as a highly luminous quasar whose sightline lacks Lyman-limit-system absorption over 24.0 pMpc, suggesting it lies in a large-scale under-density.

quotes.quote: The sightline to quasar J0836+0054 is the sole exception. The lack of LLS absorption extends to a distance of 24.0 pMpc in front of this object, 40 longer than the quasar's expected influence given its M_ 1450 . This suggests that J0836+0054, in addition to being highly luminous, happens to reside in a large-scale under-density (see also Becker21 ).

gmw_00001420.name: J1026+2542
gmw_00001420.overview: J1026+2542 is a highly radio-loud quasar and blazar at a redshift of 5.28 [arXiv:2006.13762, arXiv:1301.0012]. The galaxy hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:1803.01510] and features a clear relativistic jet aligned close to the line of sight [arXiv:2109.08156].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1026+2542 as the strongest sample radio-loud quasar, a z=5.28 blazar with Doppler boosting, VLBI jet motions, and bright unresolved GMRT emission.

quotes.quote: J1026+2542 This quasar was selected from the SDSS catalog with a redshift of 5.28. It is the strongest radio-loud quasar in our sample. The flux density at 1.4 GHz from VLA FIRST ( Helfand2015 ; observation date: 1995 November 22; angular resolution: 5 4 ) is 239.44 0.14 mJy. The VLA NVSS lists a similar S_ 1.4 GHz = 256.9 7.7 mJy ( Condon1998 ; observation date: 1993 December 6; angular resolution: 45 ).

Sbarrato2012, Sbarrato2013 proposed that it is a blazar by constructing a full SED analysis in the radio up to ray regimes. These authors yielded a small jet viewing angle of 3 with respect to the line of sight, and significant Doppler boosting with a large bulk Lorentz factor 13 . Frey2015 , for this object, obtained the first directly jet proper motions (up to 0.1 mas yr ^ 1 ) in z > 5 blazars with EVN VLBI dual-frequency (1.7 and 5 GHz) observations combined with VLBA imaging by Helmboldt2007 . The lower limit Frey2015 obtained for the core brightness temperature, 2.3 10^ 12 K, is consistent with the blazar nature. This quasar is unresolved in the new GMRT 323 MHz observations with the highest intensity among our sample with peak flux density of 317.72 0.33 mJy beam ^ 1 . We also searched the GMRT TGSS data Intema2017 and present the 150 MHz image in the right panel of Figure tgss150 ; this observation took place on 2016 March 15. In addition, we found 20 separate flux density measurements across 72 231 MHz from the GLEAM Hurley-Walker2017 survey, which are listed in Table pdata2 .

sources.summary: The paper uses B2 1023+25 as a z=5.28 comparison blazar, noting prior X-ray and VLBI classifications and a clear relativistic jet aligned near the line of sight.

quotes.quote: Along with these two extreme sources (in terms of their mass and luminosity), we also performed observations on a known high- z , M>10^9M_ jetted source with its jet aligned to our line of sight: SDSS J102623.61+254259.5 (hereafter B2 1023+25, z=5.28 ). We aimed at comparing known extremely massive jetted sources in the same redshift bin. Unfortunately, no 10^ 10 M_ blazar has yet been detected at z>5 ; therefore, we chose a very well-studied source with the largest mass possible. This source was selected as a potential blazar candidate from the SDSS spectroscopic sample cross-correlated with FIRST because of its strong radio flux ( F_ > 100 mJy) and extreme radio-loudness sbarrato13a . observed it in the X-rays and managed to classify it as a blazar thanks to its strong and hard X-ray flux.
B2 1023+25 has already been classified as a blazar because of its radio-loudness, flat radio spectrum, strong and hard X-ray flux and spectrum, and super-luminal motion in VLBI images frey15 .

The source B2 1023+25 shows a clear relativistic jet from VLBI images. We can conclude that all sources have an AGN jet morphology, and next we estimate the possible jet orientation and velocity to derive the relativistic Doppler factor and the intrinsic source parameters. Their radio power and properties are the same as those of sources in the unified model scenario.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1026 + 2542 as a highly radio-loud quasar and blazar candidate with 20 mas extended radio emission and an independently estimated massive black hole.

quotes.quote: Four of the six targets (J1026 + 2542, J1412 + 0624, J1420 + 1205, and J1659 + 2101) have radio loudness R > 2.5 . We define this radio-loudness value as the threshold for being ``highly radio loud''. RLQs satisfying this criterion represent the top 5 of the RLQ population in terms of radio loudness (see the dash-dotted curve in Fig. MiR_fig ). We note that Sbarrato et al. (2012b) have also recently studied J1026 + 2542, claiming that it is a blazar.
The extended radio emission of J1026 + 2542 is on a 20 mas scale; it contributes 40 of the total radio flux (Helmboldt et al. 2007).

Another HRLQ in our sample, J1026 + 2542, has M_ BH estimated by Sbarrato et al. (2012b) based on the peak frequency of disk emission and the total disk luminosity. The estimated range is M_ BH =(1.8--4.5) 10^9M_ .

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes B2 1023+25 as a radio-loud z>5 blazar, finding improved LAT limits, a NuSTAR break like Q0906, and SED models with broad Doppler-factor bounds.

quotes.quote: Comparison with other z>5 Blazars Since their SEDs are quite similar, we next make a comparative analysis for the other three claimed z>5 blazars B2 1023+25, SDSS J0131 0321, and SDSS J114657.79+403708.6 hereafter B2 1023, J0131, and J1146; stgp+13,gstf+14,gtsg15 . These sources are all radio loud (to varying degrees) and thus represent a population of high-mass, spin-dominated BHs in the early universe. We can update earlier characterization of the SEDs around the critical EC peak, by re-measuring the X-ray archival data and by deriving improved Fermi LAT upper limits by using 8 years of Pass-8 data.

For B2 1023, the overall IR-to-X-ray SED we construct is very similar to that reported previously stgp+13,gstf+14,gtsg15 . However our LAT upper limits improve by over 10 and this rules out the highest- _ D or the smallest- _ V models in Figures 2 and 3 of stgp+13 . Moreover our re-analysis of the NuSTAR spectrum (using R=30'' and R=45'' apertures for source and background extraction, respectively) does not agree with their finding of a steeply rising flux (Figure fig:fig5 ). Instead we see a break similar to that of Q0906 (Figure fig:fig2 ).

gmw_00001421.name: J1146+4037
gmw_00001421.overview: J1146+4037 is a high-redshift, radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of approximately 5.006 [arXiv:1803.01510, arXiv:2006.13762]. It is classified as a blazar and exhibits a compact, point-like structure in radio observations [arXiv:1803.01510, arXiv:2006.13762].

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J114657.79+403708.6 as a radio-loud z>5 blazar, noting limited SED data, small SSC flux relative to synchrotron emission, and LAT Doppler-factor bounds.

quotes.quote: Comparison with other z>5 Blazars Since their SEDs are quite similar, we next make a comparative analysis for the other three claimed z>5 blazars B2 1023+25, SDSS J0131 0321, and SDSS J114657.79+403708.6 hereafter B2 1023, J0131, and J1146; stgp+13,gstf+14,gtsg15 . These sources are all radio loud (to varying degrees) and thus represent a population of high-mass, spin-dominated BHs in the early universe. We can update earlier characterization of the SEDs around the critical EC peak, by re-measuring the X-ray archival data and by deriving improved Fermi LAT upper limits by using 8 years of Pass-8 data.

The SEDs of J0131 and J1146 are even less well measured, but do show some differences from the other two. J0131 has very strong thermal disk emission and J1146 has relatively small SSC flux compared to its synchrotron emission. For the these blazars low frequency IR points are useful for estimating the synchrotron component flux, but the lack of hard X-ray measurements leaves the SCC peak frequency almost unconstrained. Thus _ e ( _ ssc,pk ^ obs / _ sy,pk ^ obs )^ 1/2 is similarly unconstrained. The improved LAT upper limits from our analysis do place a bound 16 in both cases, but as small as 4 seems acceptable (Figure fig:fig3 bottom).

sources.summary: The paper describes J1146+4037 as a z≈5.006 SDSS quasar, compact in VLBI observations, point-like at 323 MHz, and later analyzed for spectral turnover origin.

quotes.quote: J1146+4037 This quasar is selected from the SDSS catalog with a redshift of 5.009, which has been improved to 5.0059 by Hewett2010 . Frey2010 observed this target with mas angular resolution using VLBI at 1.6 and 5 GHz, and found that it is a compact source. This quasar is a point source in our 323 MHz observations with S_ 323 MHz, peak = 3.05 0.07 mJy beam ^ 1 .

gmw_00001422.name: J1427+3312
gmw_00001422.overview: J1427+3312 is a radio-loud broad absorption line quasar at a redshift of 6.12 [arXiv:2006.13762, arXiv:0805.0474]. High-resolution radio observations reveal a double structure with two components separated by approximately 160 parsecs, suggesting it is a young compact symmetric object [arXiv:2006.13762, arXiv:1105.2371].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1427+3312 as a z=6.12 broad absorption line quasar with mas-scale double structure, young CSO interpretation, and consistent GMRT/VLA low-frequency fluxes.

quotes.quote: FIRST J1427385+331241 (hereafter J1427+3312) McGreer2006 discovered this quasar by matching sources from the FLAMINGOS Extragalactic Survey (FLAMEX) IR survey to the FIRST survey radio sources with NDWFS counterparts. It is a broad absorption line quasar with a redshift of 6.12, which makes it the second highest redshift quasar in our sample. Momjian2008 presented 1.4 GHz VLBA observations, and found two mas-scale resolved components with a projected distance of 31 mas (174 pc). These authors assumed a CSO model that reveals a kinematic age of 10 ^ 3 yr.

Frey2008 studied the compact radio structure of this target with a resolution of a few mas with EVN VLBI at 1.6 GHz and 5 GHz. They also detected the double structure with a separation of 28.3 mas (160 pc), which confirms that it is a young CSO ( 10^ 4 yr). By fitting the 323 MHz GMRT image of this target, the CASA 2D Gaussian fit tool gives a deconvolved source size of (7 89 2 41) (1 85 0 49) , an integrated flux density of 4.51 0.22 mJy, and a peak flux density of 3.96 0.10 mJy beam ^ 1 . The fact that the deconvolved major axis lies roughly along the synthesized beam major axis makes this size determination questionable and in the following we treat this target as a point source. This target is included in the VLA 325 MHz image of the Bootes field Coppejans2015 , with S_ 325 MHz = 4.80 0.80 mJy, which is consistent with our 323 MHz measurement.

sources.summary: The paper presents EVN detections of the z=6.12 radio-loud quasar J1427+3312, finding a resolved double radio structure resembling young compact symmetric objects.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The equatorial coordinates of the brightness peak are _ J2000 = 14^ h 27^ m 38 58563 and _ J2000 = +33 12 41 9252 .

quotes.quote: The source J1427+3312 ( z=6.12 ) has recently been identified as the first and so far the only known radio-loud quasar at z>6 . We investigated the compact radio structure of J1427+3312 on milli-arcsecond (mas) angular scales, to compare it with that of the second most distant radio-loud quasar J0836+0054 ( z=5.77 ) and with lower-redshift radio quasars in general.

The source was clearly detected at both frequencies. At 1.6 GHz, it shows a prominent double structure. The two components are separated by 28.3 mas, corresponding to a projected linear distance of 160 pc.
The radio spectrum of this feature is steep. The double structure and the separation of the components of J1427+3312 are similar to those of the young ( 10^4 yr) compact symmetric objects (CSOs).

sources.summary: The paper describes J1427+3312 as a first z>6 radio quasar whose VLBI images show a double structure, 28 mas component separation, CSO-like youthfulness, and a steep 5 GHz brighter component.

quotes.quote: The VLBI images of the first z>6 radio quasar, J1427+3312 ( ) revealed a prominent double structure. The two resolved components are separated by 28 mas ( 160 pc).

The structure similar to that of the compact symmetric objects (CSOs) is one of the indications of the youthfulness of J1427+3312. The brighter component also detected at 5 GHz has a steep radio spectrum ( = 0.6 ).

gmw_00001423.name: J1429+5447
gmw_00001423.overview: J1429+5447 is a high-redshift, radio-loud weak-line quasar [arXiv:1105.2371, arXiv:2112.07799] that exhibits compact, steep-spectrum radio emission [arXiv:1105.2371, arXiv:1701.06622] and extreme X-ray luminosity [arXiv:2011.13724]. Its molecular gas is resolved into two distinct peaks, suggesting a gas-rich major merger [arXiv:1105.4199].

sources.summary: The paper presents EVN observations of CFHQS J142952+544717/J1429+5447, a z=6.21 radio-loud quasar, finding compact L-band radio emission, a steep dominant-component spectrum, and mas-scale structure similar to other z~6 quasars.

quotes.quote: The quasar CFHQS J142952+544717 (J1429+5447 in short) was discovered in the Canada-France High-z Quasar Survey (CFHQS; ). With the spectroscopic redshift of z =6.21, it is the most distant radio-loud quasar known to date. The object appears in the Very Large Array (VLA) Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-centimeters (FIRST) survey list as an unresolved ( <5^ ) radio source with an integral flux density of S =2.95 mJy at 1.4 GHz. We observed J1429+5447 with the EVN at 1.6 and 5 GHz, to compare its high-resolution radio structure and spectral properties with those of the two other z 6 quasars already known.

We therefore see the entire L-band radio emission of J1429+5447 originating from a 10-mas region, corresponding to the linear size less than 60 pc. The two-point spectral index for the dominant component of the source is steep, = 1.0 . The total rest-frame 5-GHz monochromatic luminosity of J1429+5447 is 4.5 10^ 26 W Hz ^ 1 , comparable to other high-redshift sources (e.g. ). We can conclude that the mas-scale radio structure of the highest-redshift radio quasar known to date, J1429+5447 ( z =6.21), is quite similar to what we have seen in the other two z 6 quasars (J0836+0054 and J1427+3312).

sources.summary: The paper describes J1429+5447 as the most distant sample quasar, with weak Ly emission, CO line structure, compact VLBI radio emission, and point-source GMRT detection.

quotes.quote: CFHQS J142952.17+544717.6 (hereafter J1429+5447) Willott2010 discovered this quasar in the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope Legacy Survey (CFHTLS) Wide W3 region. This object has strong continuum but a weak Ly emission line at z = 6.21, meaning that it the most distant object in our sample. Wang2011 detected both CO (2 1) line and its underling continuum. The CO line emission shows two prominent peaks that are separated with 1 2 (6.9 kpc). A Gaussian fit to the CO spectrum yielded a source redshift of 6.1831.

Frey2011 detected a single dominant component at both 1.4 GHz and 5 GHz by VLBI toward this target. It was slightly resolved with mas-scale resolution within < 100 pc. The derived brightness temperature on the order of 10^ 8 K, which supports the AGN origin of the radio emission of this quasar. This target is a point source with a peak flux density of 4.91 0.16 mJy beam ^ 1 in our 323 MHz observations from the GMRT.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1429+5447 as a radio-loud z~6 quasar host whose CO(2-1) emission resolves into two comparable components, suggesting a gas-rich major merger.

quotes.quote: J1429+5447 Toward this object strong radio continuum emission was detected in the FIRST survey becker95 and recent VLBI observations frey11 , making it the strongest radio source among the known z 6 quasars and the most distant radio-loud quasar. It has also been detected in dust continuum at 250 GHz with a flux density of 3 mJy (Omont et al. 2011, in prep.). We have detected both CO (2-1) line emission and continuum emission at the line frequency.

The C array imaging of the CO (2-1) line emission from J1429+5447 has resolved the molecular gas into two distinct peaks, with a spatial separation of 6.9 kpc; the quasar position is consistent with the West peak. There is no clear velocity offset ( 26 60 km s^ 1 ) between the two components. These results suggest a gas-rich, major merging system with two distinct components that are comparable in CO luminosity and molecular gas mass.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1429+5447 as a weak-line quasar with the highest measured line ratio in the sample; excluding it with the other WLQ barely changes the sample median.

quotes.quote: There are two quasars in our sample, J1429+5447 and J1257+6349, labeled as weak-line quasars (WLQ) by Shen19a . Their / \ ratios are 6.35 and 3.57, respectively. In their spectra, \ is barely detected. Their equivalent widths are less than 10 , while \ is detected.
Indeed, J1429+5447 has the highest / \ ratio in our sample, while J1257+6349 is marginally within the 16th 84th range. If we exclude these two WLQs, the median / \ ratio decreases from 2.54 to 2.52.

sources.summary: J1429+5447 is compared as another eROSITA-detected z>5 quasar with similar luminosities but steeper radio spectrum, weaker variability, lower radio loudness, and possible beaming differences.

quotes.quote: The other eROSITA-detected z>5 quasar is CFHQS J142952+544717 ( z=6.18 , J1429+5447 in short, 2020MNRAS.497.1842M ). The X-ray luminosity, bolometric luminosity, and black hole mass of \ and J1429+5447 are very similar. They also share some common radio properties: they have similar radio luminosity; J1429+5447 shows a compact structure on arcsec scales and remains unresolved even on the 100-pc scale 2011A&A...531L...5F , and \ in the VLASS images remains compact on sub-arcsec scales. Whereas, they have some different observing characteristics: J1429+5447 has a steep radio spectrum, while the radio spectrum of \ is flat; J1429+5447 has no strong variability, while \ shows significant radio variability on weekly timescales and X-ray variability on monthly timescales; the radio loudness of \ is about one order of magnitude higher than that of J1429+5447. These differences between \ and J1429+5447 may be related to the discrepancy in the relativistic beaming effects of their jets.

In addition, the optical-X-ray spectra of \ and J1429+5447 are flatter than that of radio-loud quasars (i.e., _ ox is lower), suggesting an additional contribution of X-rays from the jet 2011ApJ...726...20M,2019MNRAS.489.2732I .

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1429+5447 as steep-spectrum, while noting AGN-dominated low-frequency emission, two CO components, and thermal hot-dust emission at 250 GHz.

quotes.quote: J1429+5447 OWB2013 and CFC2016 concluded that in the spectrum of J1429+5447 (Fig. fig:J1429+5447 ) the emission below 100 GHz is from AGN activity. WWC2011 found that the CO line emission of the source is resolved into two components that are separated by 1.2 arcsec ( 6.9 kpc), with the optical and continuum source positions being consistent with the western peak. The authors also note that the eastern component is possibly extended with a size of (1.1 0.2) (0.7 0.2) arcsec, which could explain why it is not detected in the continuum observations. OWB2013 also observed J1429+5447 at 250 GHz and concluded that the majority of the 250 GHz emission is thermal emission from hot dust. The authors do however note that it is possible that a significant fraction of the 250 GHz emission could be from AGN driven synchrotron emission. Excluding the 250 GHz OWB2013 value and fitting the spectrum with a power law gives =-0.79 0.04 . We therefore classify J1429+5447 as a steep-spectrum source.

sources.summary: The paper confirms this z=6.18 radio-loud quasar as an extremely X-ray-luminous source with a soft spectrum, excess absorption, little variability, and likely jet-linked X-ray emission.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The corrected X-ray source position is =14^ h 29^ m 52.188^ s , =+54^ d 47^ m 17.86^ s

quotes.quote: The \ observations have confirmed beyond any doubt the association of the X-ray source SRGE J142952.1+544716 discovered by / \ with the quasar \ at z=6.18 . The flux measurements taken with eROSITA and \ at three epochs between Dec. 2019 and July 2020 indicate that the X-ray luminosity of \ remained at an extremely high and nearly constant level for at least a month in the quasar's rest frame. The X-ray spectrum measured with \ in the rest-frame 1.4--72 keV energy band has a simple power-law shape (slightly modified by absorption at low energies) without any significant features (Fig.  fig:rest_spec ).

We suggest that the extreme X-ray luminosity of \ (both the absolute value and the X-ray/optical ratio) is linked with its relativistic jets, the existence of which is evident from the fact that \ is a radio-loud quasar. Specifically, the observed X-ray emission might be produced by inverse Compton scattering of cosmic microwave background (CMB) photons off relativistic electrons in the jets.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1429+5447 as variable from radio flux comparisons and later lists it among sources with indications of variability despite possible GPS or MPS behavior.

quotes.quote: The NVSS flux density of J1429+5447 ( 3.8 0.5 mJy) is higher than that of FIRST ( 3.0 0.2 mJy) and S_ pred =4.0 0.2 mJy, which is equal to or slightly higher than the NVSS flux density. We therefore conclude that J1429+5447 is variable.
The exceptions to this are J0836+0054 , J1146+4037 , J1427+3312 and J1429+5447 for which we found indication of variability or concluded that they are variable in Section subsec:variability .

gmw_00001424.name: J2228+0110
gmw_00001424.overview: J2228+0110 is an optically faint, radio-bright quasar located at a redshift of z=5.95 [arXiv:1105.2047, arXiv:2006.13762]. It is highly radio-loud [arXiv:1105.2047, arXiv:2006.13762] and exhibits an unresolved morphology at survey detection limits [arXiv:1105.2047].

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J2228+0110 as a z=5.95 radio-selected quasar that is optically faint, radio bright, highly radio loud, and morphologically unresolved at survey limits.

quotes.quote: Optical and Radio The quasar, SDSS J2228+0110, was selected for followup using the point spread function (PSF) magnitudes from the co-added imaging catalog of Stripe 82 ( 2009ApJS..182..543A ). It was found near our z band magnitude limit at z = 22.28 and near our i-z color limit at i-z = 1.81 which would have been too faint and too blue to be selected by 2009AJ....138..305J . It is one of the faintest quasars found to date, with M _ 1450 = 24.53. The quasar overlaps the UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey (UKIDSS) sky coverage, but is undetected in the J band placing an upper limit at z-J 1.4 10 UKIDSS Large Area Survey detection limit is J_ AB = 20.9 , http://www.ukidss.org/surveys/surveys.html . The quasar was detected in the radio in the Stripe82 VLA Survey, which has a detection limit of 0.30 mJy.

SDSS J2228+0110 has a measured peak flux density of 0.31 mJy, just above the detection limit, and it is only the fourth z 6 quasar discovered with a flux density f _ 1.4GHz > 0.3 mJy. The faint optical luminosity and relatively high radio luminosity, L_ 5 GHz = 2.55 10 ^ 32 , makes this one of the most radio-loud z 6 quasars ever found, with a radio-loudness of R 1100. There is no discernible morphology from the optical or radio images given that the discovered quasar was at the limits of detection in both wavelengths (see Figure 3).

sources.summary: The paper presents J2228+0110 as an optically faint but radio-bright z=5.95 quasar from SDSS Stripe 82/VLA matching, undetected by GMRT with an upper-limit spectrum point.

quotes.quote: SDSS J222843.54+011032.2 (hereafter J2228+0110) Zeimann2011 discovered this radio-loud quasar by matching optical detections of the deep SDSS Stripe 82 with their radio counterparts in the Stripe 82 VLA Survey. It is an optically faint but radio bright quasar at z=5.95 . It is too faint to be detected in our 323 MHz GMRT observations. The resulting image root mean square (rms) noise level is 0.15 mJy beam ^ 1 , and we adopt 3 rms as the upper limit in our radio spectrum analysis.

gmw_00001425.name: J2245+0024
gmw_00001425.overview: J2245+0024 is a high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of 5.17 [arXiv:2006.13762]. The object exhibits a strong and highly irregular Lyman-alpha emission line profile, but it was not detected in 323 MHz radio observations [arXiv:2006.13762].

sources.summary: The paper describes J2245+0024 as a z=5.17 quasar with a strong irregular Ly emission line, not detected in the GMRT data, giving a radio-spectrum upper limit.

quotes.quote: J2245+0024 Sharp2001 discovered this target in the Public Isaac Newton Group Wide Field Survey with a redshift of 5.17. This object has a strong and highly irregular Ly emission line profile. We did not detect this target in our 323 MHz GMRT observations. The final image rms noise level is 0.12 mJy beam ^ 1 , and we consider 3 rms as the upper limit in our radio spectrum analysis.

gmw_00001426.name: NGC4429
gmw_00001426.overview: NGC 4429 is a barred lenticular galaxy in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1710.01488, arXiv:1609.03559] that features a boxy, peanut-shaped bulge [arXiv:1710.01488]. It hosts a relaxed, flocculent nuclear disk of molecular gas and dust [arXiv:1710.01488, arXiv:1609.03559] containing 217 giant molecular clouds [arXiv:2106.04327].

sources.summary: The paper focuses on NGC4429, a Virgo lenticular galaxy whose ALMA CO data reveal 217 molecular clouds regulated by internal rotation, external gravity, and tidal/shear forces.

quotes.quote: We present high spatial resolution ( 12 pc) Atacama Large Millimeter/sub-millimeter Array ^ 12 CO (J=3-2) observations of the nearby lenticular galaxy NGC4429. We identify 217 giant molecular clouds within the 450 pc radius molecular gas disc. The clouds generally have smaller sizes and masses but higher surface densities and observed linewidths than those of Milky Way disc clouds. An unusually steep size -- line width relation ( R_ ^ 0.8 ) and large cloud internal velocity gradients ( 0.05 - 0.91 km s ^ 1 pc ^ 1 ) and observed Virial parameters ( _ obs,vir 4.0 ) are found, that appear due to internal rotation driven by the background galactic gravitational potential. Removing this rotation, an internal Virial equilibrium appears to be established between the self-gravitational ( U_ sg ) and turbulent kinetic ( E_ turb ) energies of each cloud, i.e. \ _ sg,vir 2E_ turb U_ sg 1.3 .

However, to properly account for both self and external gravity (shear and tidal forces), we formulate a modified Virial theorem and define an effective Virial parameter _ eff,vir _ sg,vir + E_ ext U_ sg (and associated effective velocity dispersion). The NGC4429 clouds then appear to be in a critical state in which the self-gravitational energy and the contribution of external gravity to the cloud's energy budget ( E_ ext ) are approximately equal, i.e. \ E_ ext U_ sg 1 . As such, _ eff,vir 2.2 and most clouds are not virialised but remain marginally gravitationally bound. We show this is consistent with the clouds having sizes similar to their tidal radii and being generally radially elongated. External gravity is thus as important as self-gravity to regulate the clouds of NGC4429.

sources.summary: The paper uses ALMA CO observations to measure the SMBH in barred lenticular NGC4429 and describes its truncated molecular gas disc, low dispersion, and central mass-to-light gradient.

quotes.quote: As part of the mm-Wave Interferometric Survey of Dark Object Masses (WISDOM) project we present an estimate of the mass of the supermassive black hole (SMBH) in the nearby fast-rotating early-type galaxy NGC4429, that is barred and has a boxy/peanut-shaped bulge. This estimate is based on Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) cycle-2 observations of the ^ 12 CO(3--2) emission line with a linear resolution of 13 pc (0 18 0 14). NGC4429 has a relaxed, flocculent nuclear disc of molecular gas that is truncated at small radii, likely due to the combined effects of gas stability and tidal shear. The warm/dense ^ 12 CO(3-2) emitting gas is confined to the inner parts of this disc, likely again because the gas becomes more stable at larger radii, preventing star formation. The gas disc has a low velocity dispersion of 2.2 ^ +0.68 _ 0.65 .

Despite the inner truncation of the gas disc, we are able to model the kinematics of the gas and estimate a mass of (1.5 0.1^ +0.15 _ 0.35 ) 10 ^8 \ for the SMBH in NGC4429 (where the quoted uncertainties reflect the random and systematic uncertainties, respectively), consistent with a previous upper limit set using ionised gas kinematics. We confirm that the V band mass-to-light ratio changes by 30 within the inner 400 pc of NGC4429, as suggested by other authors.

sources.summary: NGC4429 is a Virgo lenticular with a regular nuclear gas and dust disc; CO measurements indicate central excess mass and a heavier central IMF mismatch.

quotes.quote: NGC4429 is a lenticular galaxy in the Virgo cluster with a nuclear disc of gas and dust. The molecular disc is regular and relaxed, with a small hole in its centre (radius 0 45, 36pc; Onishi et al., in prep ). This object has a slight M/L gradient over the extent of the molecular gas disc, with an band M/L of 6. Our CO measurements, however, reveal that the central regions of this object contain more mass than expected, and the M/L varies between 7.5 to 6.5 within the inner kiloparsec. This is also reflected in the IMF mismatch parameter, which is marginally consistent with Salpeter in the outer parts but heavier in the galaxy centre.

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 4429 as an anemic/truncated Virgo galaxy and notes that its prominent circumnuclear disk cannot be reproduced by the simple models, so inner radii are excluded.

quotes.quote: Following the use of the star formation classes introduced by Koopmann Kenney (2004), we can group our sample galaxies as follow Normal galaxies: NGC 4651. Truncated/Normal galaxies: NGC 4351, NGC 4569, NGC 4580, and NGC 4694. Truncated/Compact galaxies: NGC 4064, NGC 4424, and NGC 4606. Anemic or Truncated/Anemic galaxies: NGC 4293, NGC 4429, and NGC 4450.

Galaxies such as NGC 4064 and NGC 4450 present bigger uncertainties resulting from the existence of a central bar that obviously cannot be accounted by an axisymmetric decomposition. The / _ 0 ratio salong the major axis are displayed in Fig fig6 . In most galaxies, the values are roughly constant along the major axis outside the central 5", except in NGC 4293, NGC 4424, NGC 4429, and NGC 4450. In NGC 4429 there is a prominent circumnuclear disk which cannot be reproduced with these simple models. The best / _ 0 ratios, therefore , were determined (Table table5 ) as the average along the major axis outside the central 5", except for NGC 4429 where we use distances greater than 12" in order to exclude the circumnuclear disk.

gmw_00001427.name: J072326-732656
gmw_00001427.overview: J072326-732656 is a compact, high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=8.495 [arXiv:2207.13020]. It is characterized by a very low gas-phase metallicity and exhibits emission line properties that closely resemble nearby Green Pea galaxies [arXiv:2207.13020].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes J072326-732656 as the highest-redshift and most extreme JWST ERO galaxy, with z=8.495, very low gas metallicity, compact size, and Green Pea-like emission properties.

quotes.quote: J072326-732656 (04590) J072322-732606 (06355) J072320-732604 (10612) SMACS 0723 SMACS 0723.3-7327 table 1 Finding Peas in the Early Universe Rhoads et al. Finding Peas in the Early Universe with JWST James E. Rhoads Astrophysics Science Division, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, 8800 Greenbelt Road, Greenbelt, Maryland, 20771, USA James.E.Rhoads@nasa.gov Isak G. B. Wold Astrophysics Science Division, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, 8800 Greenbelt Road, Greenbelt, Maryland, 20771, USA Department of Physics, The Catholic University of America, Washington, DC 20064, USA Center for Research and Exploration in Space Science and Technology, NASA/GSFC, Greenbelt, MD 20771 Santosh Harish School of Earth and Space Exploration, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ 85287, USA Keunho J. Kim Department of Physics, University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH 4521, USA John Pharo School of Earth and Space Exploration, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ 85287, USA Sangeeta Malhotra Astrophysics Science Division, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, 8800 Greenbelt Road, Greenbelt, Maryland, 20771, USA Sangeeta.Malhotra@nasa.gov Austen Gabrielpillai Astrophysics Science Division, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, 8800 Greenbelt Road, Greenbelt, Maryland, 20771, USA Tianxing Jiang School of Earth and Space Exploration, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ 85287, USA Huan Yang Las Campanas Observatory, Carnegie Institution of Washington, Casilla 601, La Serena, Chile The Early Release Observations (EROs) of JWST beautifully demonstrate the promise of JWST in characterizing the universe at cosmic dawn. We analyze the ERO spectra of three z 8 galaxies to determine their metallicities, gas temperatures and ionization. These galaxies offer the first opportunity to understand the physical properties of epoch-of-reionization galaxies through detailed rest-optical emission line spectroscopy. We show that these objects have metal abundances 12+ O/H 6.9 - 8.2 , based on both the T_e method and on a recent calibration of the R_ 23 metallicity indicator. Since the spectra are some of the earliest science data from JWST, we compare several line ratios with values expected from robust physics, to validate our measurement procedures. We compare the abundances and emission line ratios to a nearby sample of Green Pea galaxies--- a population of nearby emission line galaxies whose UV properties resemble epoch-of-reionization galaxies, and which often have large Lyman continuum escape fractions. The JWST data show striking further similarities between these high redshift galaxies and nearby Green Peas.

We have analyzed the rest-frame optical spectra of three epoch-of-reionization galaxies from the JWST Early Release Observations on the \ field. These objects are all strong line emitters, with spectra reminiscent of nearby Green Pea galaxies, and also of narrowband-selected extreme emission line galaxies at intermediate redshifts. This result supports earlier conclusions that Green Peas are among the best nearby analogs to high redshift galaxies. The highest redshift and most extreme among these galaxies, \ at z= 8.495 , has a very low gas phase metallicity, 6.9 0.15 from the T_e method, and 7.1 from the Jiang2019 calibration of the strong line R _23 method. Its / \ ratio may demand the presence of a heating mechanism beyond photoheating by a young starburst. The other two galaxies, \ at z=7.664 and \ at z=7.659 have appreciably higher metallicities, in the range 7.7 < < 8.2 . They demonstrate empirically that galaxy formation and stellar nucleosynthesis can achieve a metal abundance comparable to the Magellanic Clouds Russell1992 within just 700 Myr after the Big Bang. All three of these galaxies share the compact sizes and high surface brightnesses that characterize \ emitting galaxies across a wide range of redshifts, from z 6.5 down to z 0 Malhotra2012,Kim2020,Kim2021 .

gmw_00001428.name: J072322-732606
gmw_00001428.overview: J072322-732606 is a high-redshift, compact emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of z=7.664 [arXiv:2207.13020]. It exhibits physical properties and rest-frame optical spectra that are highly reminiscent of nearby Green Pea galaxies [arXiv:2207.13020].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes J072322-732606 as a z=7.664 JWST ERO galaxy with rest-optical line measurements, higher metallicity than the most extreme source, and compact Green Pea-like properties.

quotes.quote: J072326-732656 (04590) J072322-732606 (06355) J072320-732604 (10612) SMACS 0723 SMACS 0723.3-7327 table 1 Finding Peas in the Early Universe Rhoads et al. gmw_00001429.name: RX J0008.0+1450
gmw_00001429.overview: RX J0008.0+1450 is a nearby active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1007.5317]. It is classified as a gamma-ray loud source and is considered a potential accelerator site for ultra-high-energy cosmic rays [arXiv:1007.5317].

sources.summary: The paper includes RX J0008.0+1450 among seven nearby gamma-ray loud 1LAC AGNs associated with UHECR directions within 16.9 degrees and considered potential accelerators.

quotes.quote: We identify 7 `` ray loud'' AGNs which are associated with UHECRs within 17^ and are likely candidates for the production sites of UHECRs: Centaurus A, NGC 4945, ESO 323-G77, 4C+04.77, NGC 1218, RX J0008.0+1450 and NGC 253.

The potential UHECR accelerators Our results pinpoint 7 `` ray loud'' 1LAC AGNs as the potential sites for the production of PAO UHECRs (see Table tab:agn ): Cen A, NGC 4945, ESO 323-G77, 4C+04.77, NGC 1218, RX J0008.0+1450 and NGC 253 (ordered by increasing number of associated UHECRs at the angular distance 16.9^ ).
Of the 27 PAO events, 17 are located within 16.9^ of 7 nearby 1LAC AGNs: Centaurus A, NGC 4945, ESO 323-G77, 4C+04.77, NGC 1218, RX J0008.0+1450 and NGC 253.

gmw_00001430.name: Q 0144 3938
gmw_00001430.overview: Q 0144 3938 is a high-luminosity, radio-quiet quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0405458]. Its spectrum is best modeled with a warm absorber component that has excess absorption, alongside a broad iron line [arXiv:astro-ph/0405458].

sources.summary: The paper models Q 0144 3938 as a high-luminosity radio-quiet QSO whose spectrum favors a broad iron line and a warm absorber component with excess absorption.

quotes.quote: Allowing the width of the line in Q 0144 3938 to vary improved the fit slightly over a narrow line model (null probability of 1.7 10 ^ 2 ); the best-fit line was found to have an intrinsic width of 0.15 keV.

As Table broadband shows, the objects were generally best fitted with one or two BBs in addition to the power-law (fits 5 and 6); however, the fit to Q 0144 3938 improves enormously with the addition of a warm absorber component (modelled with absori in Xspec ), in the rest-frame of the AGN.
The model for Q 0144 3938 includes a similar value for excess absorption as for the blackbody fit.

gmw_00001431.name: PG 1634+706
gmw_00001431.overview: PG 1634+706 is a highly luminous active quasar located at a redshift of approximately 1.34 [arXiv:2403.17865, arXiv:1307.6799]. Its X-ray spectrum exhibits a power-law continuum, a broadened iron emission line, and a soft excess component [arXiv:2403.17865, arXiv:astro-ph/0405458].

sources.summary: The paper reports 2019 SRG and complementary observations of quasar PG 1634+706, measuring its 1-30 keV spectrum, iron line, and modest X-ray variability over hours to years.

quotes.quote: In the fall of 2019, during the in-flight calibration phase of the SRG observatory, the onboard \ and Mikhail Pavlinsky \ telescopes carried out a series of observations of PG 1634+706 — one of the most luminous (an X-ray luminosity 10^ 46 erg/s) quasars in the Universe at z<2 . Approximately at the same dates this quasar was also observed by the \ observatory. Although the object had already been repeatedly studied in X-rays previously, its new observations allowed its energy spectrum to be measured more accurately in the wide range 1--30 keV (in the quasar rest frame). Its spectrum can be described by a two-component model that consists of a power-law continuum with a slope 1.9 and a broadened iron emission line at an energy of about 6.4 keV.

The X-ray variability of the quasar was also investigated. On time scales of the order of several hours (here and below, in the source rest frame) the X-ray luminosity does not exhibit a statistically significant variability. However, it changed noticeably from observation to observation in the fall of 2019, having increased approximately by a factor of 1.5 in 25 days.

sources.summary: PG 1634+706 has an associated absorber with Ly and doublet detections, broader-than-instrumental lines, inconclusive partial coverage, and ion kinematics offset by about 20.

quotes.quote: PG 1634+706 ( =1.3415) -- This associated absorption-line system is detected in Ly , and the 1031.926, 1037.617 doublet (Figure fig:pg1634 ). Higher order Lyman series lines are not detected. The 1238.821, 1242.804 doublet is covered, but not detected. The lines are broader than the instrumental profile, but a partial coverage analysis is inconclusive. The coverage fraction is marginally inconsistent with unity at the 1--2 \ level, but this may be a result of noise or unresolved saturation.

sources.summary: The paper finds PG 1634+706 is better fit with a soft-excess component, though extra rest-frame absorption is also favored because the low-energy spectrum rolls over.

quotes.quote: For three of the QSOs, upward curvature was found at low ( < 1--2 keV observer's frame) energies, indicating soft excess emission; although the panel for PG 1634+706 does not show an obvious excess of counts at lower energies, it was found to be better fitted by the inclusion of a soft excess component (see Section broad ).

However, for the case of PG 1634+706, including an additional rest-frame absorption component [ N _ H = (16 2) 10 ^ 20 cm ^ 2 ] actually improves the fit, despite there being no evidence for such absorption when modelling the spectrum with a power-law and blackbody.

Blackbody spectra curve over at lower energies, while power-laws don't; Figure se shows that the spectrum of PG 1634+706 does roll over slightly at the low energies, hence the requirement for the excess absorption.

gmw_00001432.name: NGC 83
gmw_00001432.overview: NGC 83 is a giant elliptical galaxy with a surface brightness profile that follows an R^1/4 law [arXiv:0801.0591, arXiv:astro-ph/0508330]. It hosts a regular, well-relaxed central disk of molecular gas and dust [arXiv:0801.0591, arXiv:astro-ph/0508330] that shows evidence of star formation activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0508330].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 83 as a late-merger/accretion elliptical with an R^1/4 profile, a regular central cold gaseous dust disk, unresolved submillimeter emission, and possible late-stage gas accretion.

quotes.quote: NGC 083 and NGC 759 are elliptical galaxies with surface brightness profiles that are fit by the ^ 1/4 law e.g. you05, wik97 . Using deep CCD V and R images, you05 confirmed in detail that NGC 83 is classically fit by the ^ 1/4 law from about radii 6'' to 80'' and that within about radii 6'' it has a regular-shaped cold gaseous dust disk.

Consistent with dust emitting components that are likely co-incident with the compact gaseous disk detections in NGC 83 you05 and NGC 759 wik97 , both these galaxies are not resolved in the submm images presented in Figures fig:ngc83 and fig:ngc759 . The DSS2 blue and red optical images of these galaxies are saturated; and here, the V-R extinction maps of you05 are compared with SHARC II emission maps of the dust. The compact size derived from the V-R extinction map of the dust disk in NGC 83 by you05 is smaller in extent than the 350 m continuum beam, consistent with the deconvolved, compact submm size of this galaxy measured from the presented data, and that submm emission from the dust-disk of NGC 83 (or NGC 759) being simply unresolvable with SHARC II.
The central disks in NGC 083 and NGC 759 could represent a late stage in the merging of two disk galaxies or accretion of gas-rich material.

sources.summary: NGC 83 is mapped as a giant elliptical with a relaxed molecular gas and dust disk, evidence for star formation, and clues to internal or external gas origin.

quotes.quote: In pursuit of these clues we have used the BIMA millimeter array to map CO emission in the giant elliptical galaxies NGC 83 and NGC 2320 and to search for CO emission from the S0 galaxy NGC 5838.
The molecular gas in NGC 83 is well relaxed, but both CO and dust in NGC 2320 show asymmetric structures which may be linked to a recent acquisition of the gas.
Radio continuum and FIR fluxes of NGC 83 suggest star formation activity.

gmw_00001433.name: NGC 5838
gmw_00001433.overview: NGC 5838 is an inclined lenticular galaxy [arXiv:2201.05080] located approximately 22.2 Mpc away [arXiv:1410.5120] that features a thin bar [arXiv:2201.05080] and a boxy bulge [arXiv:astro-ph/0512346]. The galaxy hosts a central nuclear dust disk [arXiv:2201.05080] with tenuous, circumnuclear star formation [arXiv:0912.0274] and an asymmetric gas distribution [arXiv:2201.05080].

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 5838 with a Nuker profile, adopts a 22.2 Mpc distance, finds a 10^8 solar-mass black hole fit, and rejects the no-BH model.

quotes.quote: NGC5838 has a nuclear star cluster of 5 10 ^7 M _ (Scott Graham 2013).

NGC 5838 Calculation of a predicted stellar velocity dispersion profile was described for galaxies with nuker profiles in Paper I. NGC 5838 has such a profile (Lauer et al 2007). Figure 7 shows the nucleus of NGC 5838 and Figure 8 shows a fit with M/L = 30 and a black hole of 1 10 ^8 M _ .
Note that Lauer et al assume V-H = 2.39 in converting NICMOS data to visual magnitudes. We also adopted their distance of 22.2 Mpc. The no BH model is rejected with 98 confidence.

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 5838 among circumnuclear star-forming fast rotators, physically distinct from widespread star-forming systems, but describes its ongoing star formation as tenuous.

quotes.quote: Circumnuclear Star-Forming Fast Rotators The \ galaxies (NGC 524, NGC 4459, NGC 4526, NGC 5838, NGC 5845) appear to represent a completely different physical state than the \ systems described above. In Figure sfrmstar , these galaxies are seen to be those star-forming systems with higher masses and therefore lower molecular gas fractions and specific star formation rates. Consequently, the current star formation events do not dominate the SSP ages, all of which are measured to be intermediate to old in the regions of star formation. Unlike in the \ galaxies, these regions of star formation are spatially very distinct, with all of the star formation, younger SSP ages, ionized gas, molecular gas, dust, and stellar kinematic structures being spatially coincident in well-defined central disks (see the definition of this class in SFdyn ).

In contrast, the presence of on-going star formation in other \ galaxies (NGC 524, NGC 5838, NGC 5845) is more tenuous. These galaxies have more tentative PAH detections ( maybeSF ) and only marginally detected younger SSP ages and metallicity gradients. Nevertheless, the PAH emission remains spatially coincident with thin, regular dust disks \ and with embedded stellar kinematic structures (Figure starsgas ).

sources.summary: The atlas describes NGC 5838 as an inclined lenticular with a thin bar, minor-axis rotation, nuclear dust disk, and asymmetric gas that may trace either disk or outflowing processes.

quotes.quote: NGC 5838 (see Fig. Figure_AB9 ): This is an inclined lenticular galaxy ( i = 72 , see Table. Table_galaxieslisted ), with a thin bar Molaeinezhad2016,Laurikainen2017 , fast, regular disc rotation Emsellem2004 . There is reported minor axis rotation, probably produced by asymmetry effects in the velocity maps due to the presence of a nuclear dust disc FalconBarroso2003 . This dust disc is subtle, but visible in our broad-band image. Our narrow-band image precludes to interpret if the gas is lying in the galaxy disc or it may be produced by outflowing processes given its asymmetry. found a regular dust disc and a relatively small scale of the gas distribution (maximum extension up to 10 \ north-east from the nucleus), being H similar to our H distribution.

sources.summary: NGC 5838 is discussed as an S0 galaxy targeted for a deep CO search; the claimed CO detection was not confirmed despite prior single-dish and SAURON context.

quotes.quote: A deep search for CO emission from NGC 5838 is also presented.
NGC 83, NGC 2320, and NGC 5838 were selected for CO mapping because they are early type galaxies with relatively strong CO detections in the single-dish surveys of wiklind95 [ WCH95 ] and knapp96 .
The claimed CO detection in NGC 5838 has not been confirmed, but we are still able to compare stellar and gas kinematics for NGC 2320.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 5838's bulge is classified elliptical but boxy, has a positive age gradient, and its disk remains super-solar but less enhanced than the bulge.

quotes.quote: For instance, L u tticke et al. classify NGC 5838's bulge as elliptical but describe it as boxy.
At least ten galaxies (IC 302 and NGCs 266, 765, 2487, 2599, 2916, 3883, 5020, 5375, and 5838) have a positive age gradient (larger age with increasing radius).
The disk of NGC 5838 is super-solar but less enhanced than its bulge.

gmw_00001434.name: ESO 059-G001
gmw_00001434.overview: ESO 059-G001 is a dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2309.07919] characterized by a low inclination and close-to solid-body rotation [arXiv:0906.4654]. It exhibits low levels of star formation overall [arXiv:2210.01556], with a metal-poor nuclear star cluster that has assembled primarily through the accretion of globular clusters [arXiv:2210.01556].

sources.summary: The paper includes ESO 59-01 in its MUSE dwarf-galaxy sample and says observational limits prevent decisive resolved SFR-stellar-mass conclusions for it.

quotes.quote: Our sample contains a total of nine galaxies: ESO 59-01, IC 1959, NGC 1487, NGC 1796, NGC 853, UGC 3755, UGC 5889, NGC 4592 and UGC 8041. The first seven were observed in 2020 and 2021 as part of the MUSE programme 0108.B-0904 (PI: Fahrion) making use of adaptive optics-supported wide-field mode. The last two were retrieved from the ESO Science Archive. The nine galaxies display different morphologies and NGC 1487 deserves a special note given that it is an ongoing merger (see e.g. buzzo_et_al_2021 for a more detailed description of this galaxy).

Figure fig:SFR-sigma_9 shows an overall correlation between _ SFR _ * . Observational limitations do not allow for decisive conclusions in the cases of ESO 59-01, UGC 5889 and UGC 8041. Moreover, the different behaviours within NGC 4592, observed qualitatively in Fig. fig:amazing , lead to significant scatter and no statistical evidence for an overall correlation in this galaxy. It can be seen that the trends are not the same for all galaxies. NGC 1796, NGC 4592, UGC 8041 appear to have the tightest correlations in the sample.

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 59-01 as a low-star-formation dwarf whose metal-poor NSC mainly formed through globular-cluster accretion, with minor ongoing central star formation.

quotes.quote: ESO 59-01 shows low levels of star formation across the galaxy (see Bulichi in prep.), but we find a small peak of star formation at the NSC position. The gaseous and stellar components in the NSC agree in their line-of-sight velocities, further suggesting that this ionised gas is associated with low-level of star formation currently occurring in the NSC. The NSC SFH is clearly dominated by an old and metal-poor component ( > 2 Gyr, < -1.0 dex) that comprises 97 of the mass and the NSC is more metal-poor than the circumnuclear region.

Consequently, it is likely that the NSC in ESO 59-01 has assembled most of its mass early-on through the accretion and mergers of GCs. However, the ongoing star formation and the minor young population in the NSC suggest that both channels have contributed to its formation, although with strongly unequal shares. Consequently, we identify the GC-accretion channel to be the dominant one in the formation of this NSC.

sources.summary: ESO 059-G001 is treated as a southern dwarf galaxy with harmonic decomposition results, low inclination, close-to solid-body rotation, and a full-curve mass decomposition.

quotes.quote: The four southern dwarf galaxies, ESO 059-G001, ESO 215-G?009, NGC 5408, and IC 5152, are selected from the ``Local Volume \ Survey'' LVHIS LVHIS project webpage: www.atnf.csiro.au/research/LVHIS/ ; Koribalski2008,Koribalski2009 .

Parodi2002 report ``rudiments of spiral arms'' in ESO 059-G001, which can, however, not be seen in the harmonic components (probably due to their weakness and the relatively low spatial resolution of the \ data).
ESO 059-G001 is dominated by close-to solid body rotation and additionally, the inclination is low.

gmw_00001435.name: ESO 215-G?009
gmw_00001435.overview: ESO 215-G?009 is a southern dwarf galaxy [arXiv:0906.4654]. The galaxy has a strongly varying and very low inclination within its inner 3.5 kiloparsecs, which complicates the modeling of its neutral gas distribution and kinematics [arXiv:0906.4654].

sources.summary: ESO 215-G?009 is analyzed as a southern dwarf whose low, varying inclination affects modeling; unlike most sample galaxies, NFW halo fits can be comparable or better.

quotes.quote: The neutral gas distribution in ESO 215-G?009 and its kinematics (including the derivation of a rotation curve) have been studied in detail by Warren2004 .
ESO 215-G?009 ESO 215-G?009 has a strongly varying and very low inclination in the inner 3.5 kpc (see Fig.
Therefore, we decide to ignore the unconstrained case for the subsequent analysis of ESO 215-G?009 and to use the constrained case instead.

gmw_00001436.name: NGC 5408
gmw_00001436.overview: NGC 5408 is a Magellanic irregular dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1004.4757, arXiv:0906.4654] located at a distance of 4.81 Mpc [arXiv:1004.4757]. Its nucleus is undergoing a violent burst of star formation [arXiv:1004.4757], driving gas outflows and at least one expanding superbubble across its disk [arXiv:1004.4757].

sources.summary: NGC 5408 is an IB(s)m dwarf with a violently star-forming nucleus and ULX; the paper favors an outflow scenario and suggests at least one expanding superbubble across the disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 5408 (HIPASS J1403--41) is classified as an IB(s)m galaxy. It was first studied by Bohuski1972 who found that its nucleus consists of several bright \ regions and appears to be undergoing a violent burst of star formation. As the galaxy reveals an ultra-luminous X-ray source, NGC 5408 X-1, very close to the main \ regions, it was a popular object to study over the last decade e.g., Soria2004,Soria2006,Strohmayer2007,Lang2007,Kaaret2009 . This X-ray source has recently been argued to harbour an intermediate-mass black hole Strohmayer2009 . The distance of NGC 5408 of D_ TRGB = 4.81 Mpc was obtained by Karachentsev2002 .

Therefore, the outflow scenario is much more likely. From the morphology and the kinematics, we suggest that NGC 5408 harbours at least one expanding superbubble. In comparison to IC 4662, this galaxy shows outflows almost everywhere across the disc.

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 5408 radio, infrared, and emission-line maps, finding weak radio emission in eastern infrared/Hα blobs and a sub-galactic mismatch with star-forming regions.

quotes.quote: LVHIS 065: NGC 5408 In Figure fig:ngc5408 we compare the radio continuum image with Spitzer 24 \ image and \ image.

The radio continuum emission is not fully consistent with the infrared or \ emission. Some strong infrared/ \ blobs at the eastern side of the galaxy are very weak in radio. It clearly shows that the radio continuum emission does not strictly follow the star forming regions at sub-galactic scale, which makes it more complicated to explain the global FRC see also 2013A&A...557A.129T .

sources.summary: NGC 5408 is included as a southern dwarf galaxy; the paper presents harmonic results, omits a stellar component in mass fitting, and notes its slow inner rotation rise.

quotes.quote: The four southern dwarf galaxies, ESO 059-G001, ESO 215-G?009, NGC 5408, and IC 5152, are selected from the ``Local Volume \ Survey'' LVHIS LVHIS project webpage: www.atnf.csiro.au/research/LVHIS/ ; Koribalski2008,Koribalski2009 .
In case of NGC 5408, we do not fit a stellar component as the M/L value has to be far below 0.1 in order to fit the observed rotation curve.
NGC 5408 The \ rotation curve of NGC 5408 rises very slowly in the inner 2 kpc.

gmw_00001437.name: IC 5152
gmw_00001437.overview: IC 5152 is an exceptionally isolated dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:2103.07476] with ongoing star formation [arXiv:1009.2660] and a low metallicity [arXiv:astro-ph/0301492, arXiv:1009.2660]. The galaxy features gas spread across its face [arXiv:astro-ph/0612063], highly segmented star-forming regions [arXiv:1806.05447], and a constant inclination with radius [arXiv:0906.4654].

sources.summary: The paper treats IC 5152 as the largest Local Group field dwarf and predicts its satellite abundance, virial radius, survey pointings, and expected detectable satellites under several abundance-matching models.

quotes.quote: The models predict a mean of 2-6 satellites with M_* above 10^3 , 1 - 4 above 10^4 , and 1 - 2 above 10^5 for a galaxy like IC 5152.

As an example of computing the number of galaxies in a field of view, IC 5152 is located at a distance of 1.7 Mpc Zijlstra99 . DECam has a 2.2^ field of view, resulting in a 33 kpc observed radius. At a Moster-model-estimated total halo mass of 5.2 10^ 10 , IC 5152 has a virial radius of 101 kpc .

Using Fig. fig:K(R,Z) with Z=1.5 , the number of satellites reported for the virial volume is multiplied by 0.54 to yield 1.5 expected satellites in the line of sight. An equivalent calculation for the GK16 model produces 1.8 expected satellites, 2.0 for the GK14 model, and 0.7 for the Brook model. If we were to survey the whole virial volume of IC 5152, we would require an additional 8 distinct pointings, and would expect to find only 1-2 more satellites.

sources.summary: The paper studies IC 5152 as a nearby dwarf irregular with disputed Local Group membership, extended gas, star-forming regions, and near-infrared evidence for young embedded clusters.

quotes.quote: IC 5152 (=IRAS 21594--51, ESO 237-27), a dwarf irregular galaxy (Sdm IV V), is one of the best objects available to study starbursts due to its rather close distance. On the other hand, a very bright star (HD 209142, V=7.9 , K_s=7.14 ) in the north-western part of the galaxy makes it difficult to obtain deep exposures. Basic information of IC 5152 is summarized in Table 1. It has been controversial whether IC 5152 is a member of the Local Group or not.

The image of the galaxy shows that gas is spread out on the face of IC 5152. This suggested that there should be many newly formed young stellar populations on the disk. The young populations are also contained in the massive young clusters which are hardly seen on optical images due to surrounding gas clouds.

sources.summary: IC 5152 is selected as an SMC-mass irregular dwarf Magellanic analog and described as exceptionally isolated, with DELVE-DEEP imaging covering a roughly 3-degree halo region.

quotes.quote: DELVE-DEEP targets four isolated dwarf galaxies at 1.4--2.1 ( lumdist and deep_targets ). These galaxies were chosen as MC analogs: NGC 55 and NGC 300 have stellar masses roughly comparable to the LMC, while Sextans B and IC 5152 are within an order of magnitude of the SMC ( 0.1-0.8 M_ , SMC ). NGC 55 is an irregular, barred spiral galaxy deVaucouleurs:1991 ; therefore, it closely resembles the LMC not only in stellar mass but also in morphology. Unlike the LMC, NGC 55 is almost edge-on, which makes it well suited for stellar content studies. NGC 300 is an almost pure disk galaxy and is similar to M33, an LMC-mass satellite of M31. Both NGC 55 and NGC 300 belong to the nearby Sculptor Group, which is actually an unbound filamentary structure extended along our line of sight Karachentsev:2003 . Both Sextans B and IC 5152 are irregular dwarf galaxies. While Sextans B is a member of a very loose group of dwarf galaxies (the NGC 3109 association; Tully:2006 ), IC 5152 is an exceptionally isolated object, with the nearest neighbor being NGC 55 at a distance of 800 kpc Karachentsev:2002 .

DELVE-DEEP performs deep DECam imaging around four isolated dwarf galaxies: Sextans B, NGC 55, NGC 300, and IC 5152. DELVE-DEEP will achieve 5 depths of g = and i = for each target with total integrated exposure times of 4500 in g and 3000 in i . The nominal depth can be achieved in 15 300 exposures in g and 10 300 exposures in i . However, three of the four DELVE-DEEP targets (NGC 55, NGC 300, and IC 5152) reside in the DES footprint; thus, we decrease the required exposure time by 900 to account for the existing DES data and target 12 300 g band exposures and 7 300 i band exposures for these targets. The DELVE-DEEP fields are chosen to roughly cover the angular region corresponding to the virial radius of the SMC r_ vir 110 ; Dooley:2017b at the distance of each target ( deep_targets ). This radius corresponds to 3 for NGC 55, NGC 300, and IC 5152, but it is significantly larger for Sextans B due to its proximity ( 4.4 ).

sources.summary: The paper includes IC 5152 in a three-galaxy low-metallicity dwarf sample mapped with Spitzer/IRS to study spatially resolved PAH emission, star-forming environments, and metallicity-dependent PAH behavior.

quotes.quote: The three galaxies chosen for this sample are NGC 55, NGC 3109, and IC 5152 (see Table galaxysample ).
