sources.summary: The paper reassigns GN10 to a newly detected radio source, noting faint infrared emission and possible association with a disturbed Paper III counterpart.

quotes.quote: GN10: The Paper III identification has an IRAC detection, however there is a new 4.5 radio detection 2.5 arcseconds from this system. The new radio source has a faint IRAC detection but no optical counterpart. There is very faint MIPS emission which extends between these two sources. The Paper III counterpart is at z=1.344 and therefore it should be fairly bright at 24 m if it is a submm source. Since it is not, we conclude that the new radio source is the most likely submm counterpart, but note that the Paper III counterpart and this new radio source could be associated. The optical counterpart from Paper III is classified as being asymmetric which is an indication of a merging, or disturbed, system.

gmw_00001452.name: GN19
aliases_external: GN19 / SMM J123707.7+621411 first IRAC counterpart
gmw_00001452.overview: GN19 is a high-redshift galaxy at z=2.49 formed by a gas-rich major merger of two massive, dust-obscured starburst galaxies [arXiv:1808.04372]. The system is a candidate Compton-thick object with strong intrinsic X-ray absorption [arXiv:0910.2464] and resides within a clumpy, large-scale filamentary overdensity [arXiv:2302.10323].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GN19's X-ray counterpart with the optically brighter source, finds strong intrinsic absorption, and treats it as a candidate Compton-thick SMG with a reflection-dominated fit.

quotes.quote: Of these GN04, GN19 and GN39 have X-ray counterparts. In the case of GN19, the X-ray counterpart is clearly identified with the first counterpart listed by pope06 , i.e. the optically brighter source.
As can be seen from Table fits , four of the X-ray detected submm galaxies have spectra consistent with large intrinsic absorption ( N_ H >10^ 23 cm ^ 2 : GN04, GN19, GN22 and GN39).

Three sources are candidate Compton thick objects. GN04 (Northern counterpart), GN19 and GN39 (Northern counterpart) have large column densities ( 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 ) and are well fitted by the Compton reflection dominated model but the transmission model provides almost as good a fit.

sources.summary: The paper identifies GN19 as a z=2.49 merger of two massive dusty starburst galaxies, with compatible CO flux, radio and submillimeter detections, and extended CO from a gas-rich merger.

quotes.quote: COLDz.GN.3 : We identify this source with CO(1--0) line emission from GN19, a merger of two massive, bright dust-obscured starbursting galaxies at z =2.49 found by and characterized in detail by , , and . It is detected by the 5.5 GHz eMERGE survey, with a flux of 9.6 1.7 Jy eMerge . Its line flux is compatible with the previously measured total flux of 0.33 0.04 Jy km s ^ 1 from . This source is also detected at 1.4 GHz, with a flux of 28 4 Jy and 33 4 Jy for the W and E components, respectively Morrison10 , and at 850 m with a significance of 7.9 and a flux of 6.5 1.1 mJy Geach17 .

Furthermore, one of our highest SNR line emitters in the GOODS-N field (GN19) and a galaxy with optical spectroscopic redshift (COLDz.GN.S1), also appear extended ( 40-70 kpc) in CO observations due to a major gas-rich merger in this galaxy (see also Riechers_GN19,Ivison_GN19 ).

sources.summary: The paper finds multiple catalog overdensities around SMM J123707 with similar properties and includes it among SMGs whose overdensity appears filamentary or clumpy.

quotes.quote: However, in two cases, namely the SMGs SMM J123634 and SMM J123707, there are multiple overdensities that are detected when using the L18 and B19 catalogs. These correspond to distinct overdensity peaks in the PPM plots at redshifts that are formally consistent with that of the SMG (see Appendix app:ppmplots , where the PPM plots are reported). Altogether, for a given photometric redshift catalog (B19 or L18), the multiple overdensities reported for SMM J123634 and SMM J123707, separately, have similar properties in terms of significance, size, projected coordinates, and thus miscentering _ ov (see the density maps in Appendix app:ppmplots ).

These aspects suggest that these multiple peaks correspond to the same overdensity that is fragmented at different redshifts by the PPM procedure. In other cases (e.g., HDF850.1, SMM J123634, SMM J123606, SMM J123618, SMM J123707, SMM J123711, and SMM J123712) our detected overdensities have evidence for large-scale filamentary or clumpy structures, as typically found in still-assembling protoclusters such as SSA22 at z=3.09 Steidel98,Umehata2019 , the Spiderweb at z=2.16 Jin21 , and Hyperion at z=2.45 Cucciati2018 .

gmw_00001453.name: COLDz.GN.S1
gmw_00001453.overview: COLDz.GN.S1 is a massive main-sequence galaxy at a spectroscopic redshift of z=2.320 [arXiv:1808.04372]. It is associated with two potentially interacting galaxies and exhibits a spatially extended CO line candidate likely driven by a major gas-rich merger [arXiv:1808.04372].

sources.summary: COLDz.GN.S1 is a CO line candidate exactly matched to a z=2.320 spectroscopic galaxy, spatially extended, associated with two potentially interacting galaxies, and likely a massive main-sequence system.

quotes.quote: One more galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of z_ spec =2.320 is perfectly matched, in position and redshift with a SNR=4.6 CO line candidate at coordinates J2000 12:36:49.10 +62:18:14.0 and z_ 10 =2.3192 0.0003 , which we name COLDz.GN.S1 (Figure fig:radio_cand_figs ). This CO line candidate is significantly extended (FWHM of 9.0^ 0.5^ 74 4 kpc) and appears to be associated with two, potentially interacting, galaxies with a projected separation of 20 kpc.

In particular, our sub-sample of independently confirmed CO emitters contains previously known starbursts like AzTEC-3 (by design), GN10, GN19, and HDF850.1 but also COLDz.COS.1, 2 and 3 and COLDz.GN.S1 which belong to the massive end of the Main Sequence at z 2 (Pavesi et al., in prep.). Furthermore, one of our highest SNR line emitters in the GOODS-N field (GN19) and a galaxy with optical spectroscopic redshift (COLDz.GN.S1), also appear extended ( 40-70 kpc) in CO observations due to a major gas-rich merger in this galaxy (see also Riechers_GN19,Ivison_GN19 ).

gmw_00001454.name: GP1
gmw_00001454.overview: GP1 is a compact dwarf Green Pea galaxy with one of the highest star formation rates in its class [arXiv:1810.09318, arXiv:1906.09653]. Although optically classified as star-forming [arXiv:1906.09653, arXiv:2403.19635], its strong radio detections and soft X-ray emission suggest it may host an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1906.09653, arXiv:2403.19635].

sources.summary: The paper treats GP1 as a high-SFR Green Pea with elevated metallicity, soft-band X-ray detection, and contamination checks around the extraction region.

quotes.quote: GP1 has one of the largest SFRs in both the parent and the extended samples. GP1, GP2 and GP3 lie in the star-forming section of the diagram, close to the separation line. GP2 and GP3 are in the middle of the GP cloud and on the Kewley line, while GP1 is more offset, due to its slightly higher metallicity.

GP1 is clearly observable as an unresolved point source only in the soft X-ray bands (0.2 -1 keV and 1--2 keV; Figure image_gp_1 ). The first two sources were detected and were named as 3XMM J074936.7+333717 (GP1) and 3XMM J082247.4+224145 (GP2). Thus, we do not expect any significant contribution of these stars to the detected X-ray emission of GP1.

sources.summary: The paper studies SDSS J074936.77+333716.3 as one of two extreme Green Pea galaxies: MIR data suggest possible AGN activity, but X-rays favor star formation or heavy obscuration.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we discuss the presence of AGNs in two low-mass galaxies ( M_ 10^ 9 M_ ) SDSS J074936.77+333716.3 and SDSS J082247.66+224144.0 (hereafter, and ), i.e., the two highest star-formation rate (SFR) Green Pea galaxies (GPs). As explained below, they are optically classified as non-AGN hosts, but their MIR properties are consistent with having AGNs. Their basic properties can be found in Table tab:inf_gps .

From the 20 ksec data, however, we find no significant hard X-ray emission. By contrast, soft X-ray emission with 0.5--8 keV luminosities of 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 is significantly detected in both targets, which can be explained only by star formation (SF). A possible reason for the lack of clear evidence is that a putative AGN torus absorbs most of the X-ray emission.

sources.summary: GP1 is one of three targeted Green Pea galaxies; it is X-ray bright, radio-detected in several bands, has a standard power-law radio SED, and may host an AGN.

quotes.quote: These three selected sources are referred to by Svoboda19 as GP1, GP2 and GP3 The physical properties and X-ray observations are discussed in Svoboda19 . . The X-ray observations with XMM-Newton detected extreme X-ray luminosities for star forming galaxies of L_ 2-10 keV 10^ 42 erg s^ 1 in two of the three targets (GP1 and GP2), a factor > 5 larger than the estimated luminosity from theoretical or empirical relations for star forming galaxies of similar mass range (see Svoboda19 for the details). In the BPT diagram Baldwin81 , GP1 is classified as strictly star forming, while GP2 and GP3 show line ratios which are on the border between the AGN-like and star forming classification Cardamone09 .

In the archival data, GP1 was strongly detected in the LoTSS catalogue with a flux of 2.6 mJy, although it may also be affected by artefacts from a bright off-axis source. GP1 shows a standard power-law SED with the spectral index of = -0.68 0.01 . With a strong radio detection at several radio frequencies, GP1 shows a closer similarity to the Seyfert galaxies than to the star-forming GPs, suggesting that it may host an AGN.

gmw_00001455.name: GP2
gmw_00001455.overview: GP2 is a star-forming Green Pea galaxy [arXiv:1810.09318, arXiv:1906.09653] that exhibits borderline active galactic nucleus optical line ratios [arXiv:2403.19635]. It is detected in both X-ray [arXiv:1810.09318, arXiv:1906.09653] and radio bands, showing a steep synchrotron-like radio spectral energy distribution [arXiv:2403.19635].

sources.summary: The paper describes GP2 as a star-forming Green Pea detected in all X-ray bands, with confirmed 3XMM detection and a well-fitted power-law spectrum.

quotes.quote: GP1, GP2 and GP3 lie in the star-forming section of the diagram, close to the separation line. GP2 and GP3 are in the middle of the GP cloud and on the Kewley line, while GP1 is more offset, due to its slightly higher metallicity. GP2 is present in all of the energy bands (Fig. image_gp_2 ).

The first two sources were detected and were named as 3XMM J074936.7+333717 (GP1) and 3XMM J082247.4+224145 (GP2). The flux and luminosity calculated for the 0.5-8 keV are in the rest frame of the galaxy, i.e. derived from the observed flux in the 0.39-6.28 keV band for GP 1 and the 0.41-6.58 keV band for GP2. The X-ray continuum of both spectra was well fitted by the power-law model, resulting in a C statistics fit goodness C=1241 (1937 degrees of freedom) for GP1 and C=1116 (1937 degrees of freedom) for GP2.

sources.summary: The paper studies SDSS J082247.66+224144.0 as one of two extreme Green Pea galaxies: MIR data suggest possible AGN activity, but X-rays favor star formation or heavy obscuration.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we discuss the presence of AGNs in two low-mass galaxies ( M_ 10^ 9 M_ ) SDSS J074936.77+333716.3 and SDSS J082247.66+224144.0 (hereafter, and ), i.e., the two highest star-formation rate (SFR) Green Pea galaxies (GPs). As explained below, they are optically classified as non-AGN hosts, but their MIR properties are consistent with having AGNs. Their basic properties can be found in Table tab:inf_gps .

From the 20 ksec data, however, we find no significant hard X-ray emission. By contrast, soft X-ray emission with 0.5--8 keV luminosities of 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 is significantly detected in both targets, which can be explained only by star formation (SF). A possible reason for the lack of clear evidence is that a putative AGN torus absorbs most of the X-ray emission.

sources.summary: GP2 is a targeted Green Pea galaxy with excess X-ray luminosity, borderline AGN-like optical line ratios, C- and X-band radio detections, and a steep synchrotron-like radio SED.

quotes.quote: These three selected sources are referred to by Svoboda19 as GP1, GP2 and GP3 The physical properties and X-ray observations are discussed in Svoboda19 . . The X-ray observations with XMM-Newton detected extreme X-ray luminosities for star forming galaxies of L_ 2-10 keV 10^ 42 erg s^ 1 in two of the three targets (GP1 and GP2), a factor > 5 larger than the estimated luminosity from theoretical or empirical relations for star forming galaxies of similar mass range (see Svoboda19 for the details). In the BPT diagram Baldwin81 , GP1 is classified as strictly star forming, while GP2 and GP3 show line ratios which are on the border between the AGN-like and star forming classification Cardamone09 .

Sources GP1 and GP2 are detected in both C and X bands and have comparable fluxes in both the bands. The L-band observation of GP1 was not performed during the observation cycle, and the GP2 observation in the L-band is contaminated by the artefacts from a particularly bright off-axis radio source. GP2 shows an SED with a steeper power law with the spectral index of =-0.95 0.17 .

gmw_00001456.name: GP3
gmw_00001456.overview: GP3 is a Green Pea galaxy classified as a star-forming dwarf system [arXiv:1810.09318]. It exhibits borderline line ratios between star-forming and active galactic nucleus classifications [arXiv:2403.19635], but it remains undetected in both X-ray and radio observations [arXiv:1810.09318, arXiv:2403.19635].

sources.summary: The paper includes GP3 among the star-forming Green Pea targets but reports that its shortened observation yielded only an X-ray flux upper limit.

quotes.quote: GP1, GP2 and GP3 lie in the star-forming section of the diagram, close to the separation line. GP2 and GP3 are in the middle of the GP cloud and on the Kewley line, while GP1 is more offset, due to its slightly higher metallicity. The useful exposure of GP3 shrank from 61 to 25.5 ks, which was insufficient for the detection of the source.

In contrast, GP3 remained undetected. Although there seems to be some increased flux in the 1--2 keV image of GP3 in Figure image_gp_3 , this feature was not found to be statistically significant using the SAS tool edetect chain . We derived the flux upper limit F_ 2-10 keV = 7.2 10^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , corresponding to L_ GP3, 2-10 keV = 0.77 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 (assuming a power-law spectrum with = 2 ).

sources.summary: GP3 is a targeted Green Pea galaxy with borderline AGN-like line ratios but no X-ray, JVLA L-band, or archival radio detection in the observations discussed.

quotes.quote: These three selected sources are referred to by Svoboda19 as GP1, GP2 and GP3 The physical properties and X-ray observations are discussed in Svoboda19 . . At the same time, the third target, GP3, was entirely undetected, even in the deeper follow-up observations. In the BPT diagram Baldwin81 , GP1 is classified as strictly star forming, while GP2 and GP3 show line ratios which are on the border between the AGN-like and star forming classification Cardamone09 .

The GP3 observations were performed with incorrect target coordinates, and thus, the source is outside the field of view for the C- and X-band observations, while it is still within the field of view in the L-band, but close to the edge of the primary beam, where the RMS noise is higher. The source GP3 is within the field of view in the L-band, but there is no significant detection. GP3 is not detected in any archival surveys.

gmw_00001457.name: host galaxy of SN 2020bqj
gmw_00001457.overview: The host galaxy of SN 2020bqj is a faint, low-mass galaxy [arXiv:2008.04056]. It is characterized by a low total star formation rate, though it exhibits a comparatively high specific star formation rate [arXiv:2008.04056].

sources.summary: The paper models the host galaxy of SN 2020bqj as a faint, unusually low-mass SN Ibn host with low total SFR but comparatively high specific SFR.

quotes.quote: To investigate the properties of the host galaxy of SN 2020bqj we modelled its SED based on archival data retrieved from the Legacy Surveys dey2019 . We measured the brightness of the host using the aperture photometry tool presented in Schulze2018a that is based on Source Extractor version 2.19.5 Bertin1996a . To calibrate the instrumental magnitudes, we measured the brightness of stars from SDSS in the same way.

Based on the derived parameters, the host of SN 2020bqj is an outlier in the SN Ibn subclass, with in particular a low galaxy mass. This is demonstrated in Fig. fig:ptf_hosts , where the derived properties of the host of SN 2020bqj are shown together with those of a sample of SN Ibn hosts from iPTF, where mass and SFR was derived in a consistent way (Schulze et al., to be submitted ). The host galaxy masses of the nine SNe Ibn observed in iPTF ranged between 10^8 to 10^ 10 M _ , which puts the mass of the host of SN 2020bqj two orders of magnitude lower than the least massive SN Ibn host in iPTF. In terms of total SFR, the host of SN 2020bqj is low but within uncertainties consistent with the lower end of the SFR distribution of the iPTF sample.

gmw_00001458.name: host of GRB 051022
gmw_00001458.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 051022 is a luminous blue compact star-forming galaxy [arXiv:1201.4947] located at a redshift of approximately 0.8 [arXiv:1201.4947, arXiv:1301.5903]. It is characterized as a young, ongoing merger [arXiv:1301.5903] with active star formation [arXiv:1201.4947, arXiv:1305.2941] and relatively high metallicity [arXiv:1301.5903].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the late radio source as host-galaxy emission, argues it is extended, and derives a modest radio star-formation rate above the UV estimate.

quotes.quote: As a result, the source detected in our late-time observations is almost certainly not associated with the GRB afterglow and, therefore, is likely associated with the host galaxy. The source also appears extended: we re-weighted the data within the IMAGR task to produce images of varying resolution between 1.5 and 0.4 ; the flux is seen to drop (by about 2 ) at progressively higher resolution. This effect can also be seen as some limited north-south extension in the image even at the standard resolution. This further supports a host-galaxy origin of the detected emission.

Our sole unambiguous host-galaxy detection, GRB 051022, has a radio star-formation rate of 74 20 M_ yr ^ 1 . This is higher, but not much higher, than our optically-derived estimates: our UV-based estimate for this source (from SED fitting including dust absorption; Perley+2013a ) is 26 7 M_ yr ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper presents GRB 051022 as a z = 0.807 luminous blue compact star-forming host, with no radio detection and constraints arguing against a globally dusty star-forming galaxy.

quotes.quote: Millimeter and radio interferometer observations detected the afterglow and pinpointed the position of the host galaxy at z = 0.807 cast07, rol07 . The host galaxy is a luminous blue compact star-forming galaxy with a rest-frame B band magnitude of M_B = -21.8  mag cast07 . The optical/NIR afterglow was not detected despite a bright X-ray afterglow and a high absorption column density. The X-ray spectrum of the afterglow shows a strong absorption corresponding to N_ H = 3 - 9 10^ 22  cm ^ 2 naka06, cast07 . The galaxy shows active star formation with SFR( [ O ii ] ) = 34.64  M_  yr ^ 1 sava09 and SFR(UV) = 23.85  M_  yr ^ 1 sven10 .

Our results suggest that the host galaxy of GRB 051022 is not likely a dusty star-forming galaxy. The X-ray afterglow spectrum suggests a high column density of neutral hydrogen naka06, cast07 . state that the extinction in the host galaxy of GRB 051022 ( A_V = 1.0 ) derived from its SED cannot account for the afterglow properties, assuming a Galactic dust-to-gas ratio.

sources.summary: The host of GRB 051022 is described as a bright, young high-SFR ongoing merger at z=0.809, with relatively high metallicity.

quotes.quote: GRB 051022 was a High-Energy Transient Explorer 2 (HETE-2) burst Nakagawa+2006 rapidly followed by a range of other instruments, including . Early nondetections at optical wavelengths quickly motivated deeper searches and NIR observations, all of which reported only upper limits on an optical afterglow; comparison of these limits to the X-ray flux unambiguously indicates a very large dust attenuation column ( A_V > 9.3 mag). However, a bright host-galaxy candidate was immediately evident even in small-telescope images. This GRB and its host have already been the subject of intensive studies by several authors CastroTirado+2007,Rol+2007 , which clearly characterize it as a young, high SFR ongoing merger at z=0.809 . It seems to have a relatively high metallicity Graham+2009 .

gmw_00001459.name: host of GRB 061222A
gmw_00001459.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 061222A is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.088 [arXiv:0905.0001]. It is characterized by very blue colors [arXiv:0905.0001, arXiv:1301.5903], a strong Lyman-alpha emission feature [arXiv:0905.0001, arXiv:1301.5903], and extremely low mean dust extinction [arXiv:1301.5903].

sources.summary: Source A near GRB 061222A is identified as the host galaxy, has very blue colors, and shows a likely Lyman-alpha line at z = 2.088.

quotes.quote: Two blended but seemingly distinct sources are observed near the afterglow position (Figure fig:061222A ): one (source A) coincident with the IR transient and a second (source B) offset by about 1 \ to the southeast. We identify the former as the host galaxy. The two objects have similar colors, though photometry is complicated by the close blending, especially in the redder filters where neither object is well-detected. Both galaxies are quite blue, with (B-V)_ AB 0.0 mag, (V-I)_ AB 0.5 mag, (I-z)_ AB 0.3 mag. Only object B is detected in the infrared, but both galaxies are clearly very blue in IR colors as well: for object A, (I-K)_ AB < 1.0 mag; for object B, (I-K)_ AB = 0.8 0.3 mag.

The fainter, northern object (A), which we identify as the host galaxy, has a strong emission line at 3758 . No flux is observed at this position in the southern object (B). At the same time, between two sky lines on the red side another bright emission line is clearly observed at 8014 in this case consistent only with the position of object B. The spectra and putative lines of both objects are shown in Figure fig:061222Aspec . The strong line in the blue part of the host-galaxy spectrum strongly suggests Lyman- at a redshift of z = 2.088 .

sources.summary: The host of GRB 061222A is a blue, Ly-alpha-emitting dark-GRB host with marginal Spitzer detection, very low mean extinction, and heterogeneous internal dust.

quotes.quote: GRB 061222A is one of the most obscured afterglows in the sample. The only reported detection in the optical/NIR bands is an early-time Gemini/NIRI K band point, even though this GRB was among the brightest events of that year in X-rays and gamma-rays. Deep observations in all other filters resulted only in upper limits, although these are not as constraining as the K band point. As reported by , the host is quite blue, and in fact shows a strong Lyman- emission feature. It is the only dark GRB host known to exhibit this feature MilvangJensen+2012 .

Consistent with the very blue color of this object reported by , the Spitzer data only marginally detect the host (it is one of only a few sources in the sample which do not have a strong Spitzer detection), but the source is well detected by HST . Given the very blue rest-frame UV color (which may indicate an even younger starburst than our 5 10^7 yr minimum value) the extinction is extremely low, converging to the physical limit of A_V=0 for nearly all trials, even if a significantly lower metallicity is assumed (the extremely blue color and low mean extinction mark the host of GRB 061222A as a dramatic outlier compared to most other dark GRB hosts in our sample). This is particularly curious given the tremendous extinction evident in the afterglow SED, and suggests extreme heterogeneity in the galaxy's internal dust distribution: either the dust is localized to a small number of very optically thick clouds and the GRB sightline happened to intersect one, or the bulk of the star formation is actually heavily obscured.

gmw_00001460.name: host of GRB 060923A
gmw_00001460.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 060923A is coincident with an infrared transient [arXiv:0905.0001] and is dust-obscured [arXiv:1301.5903]. It is marginally detected across multiple optical bands [arXiv:0905.0001, arXiv:1301.5903], with photometry that does not indicate a highly dust-obscured stellar population [arXiv:0905.0001].

sources.summary: The GRB 060923A host is coincident with the IR transient and marginally detected in optical bands, with moderately red colors but no strong evidence for heavy galaxy-wide dust.

quotes.quote: However, later optical follow-up by identified a host galaxy exactly coincident with the IR location, marginally detected in our imaging as well in V and I bands (Figure fig:060923A ). It is not detected in B or RG850 . estimate that for z = 2.8 about A_V 2.6 would be sufficient to explain the inferred absorption. The host galaxy is fairly but not remarkably red in the observed-frame optical: (B-V)_ AB 0.5 and (V-I)_ AB = 1.0 0.4 . A nondetection in RG850 rules out continuation of this trend further to the red, implying that the spectral energy distribution (SED) flattens towards the rest-frame optical, inconsistent with a highly dust-obscured source.

Additional photometry will be necessary to reliably constrain the extent of extinction and other properties of the host, but as with most other galaxies in our sample the host-galaxy photometry does not demand large amounts of dust.

sources.summary: The host of GRB 060923A is dust-obscured, reanalyzed with additional Gemini, Spitzer, and HST photometry, and marginally detected across many optical filters.

quotes.quote: GRB 060923A was the subject of the study by , who report deep optical limits and a faint K band detection of the afterglow (establishing the afterglow as dust obscured) and a basic characterization of the host galaxy. We also previously discussed this object as part of the study of . Our reanalysis here includes all of these data in addition to further, unpublished photometry from Gemini-N/NIRI and Spitzer (cold mission; PI Fox) as well as recent HST observations. While the host has been observed in a large number of (mostly optical) filters, the detection is marginal in all of them.

gmw_00001461.name: host of GRB 070802
gmw_00001461.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 070802 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=2.45 [arXiv:0810.2897, arXiv:1108.0674]. It is a moderately red galaxy with a low stellar mass, a relatively young stellar population, and an estimated star formation rate of 10 to 16 solar masses per year [arXiv:1301.5903, arXiv:1108.0674].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the z=2.45 GRB 070802 host as the first clear GRB host with a 2175 dust bump, measures LMC-like extinction, and detects the host in R and K bands.

quotes.quote: We report the clear detection of the 2175 dust absorption feature in the optical afterglow spectrum of the gamma-ray burst (GRB) GRB 070802 at a redshift of z=2.45 . This is the highest redshift for a detected 2175 dust bump to date, and it is the first clear detection of the 2175 bump in a GRB host galaxy, while several tens of optical afterglow spectra without the bump have been recorded in the past decade. The derived extinction curve gives A_V=0.8 - 1.5 depending on the assumed intrinsic slope. Of the three local extinction laws, an LMC type extinction gives the best fit to the extinction curve of the host of .

The host galaxy K and R band observations of the host galaxy of GRB 070802 were obtained with the ESO VLT during the nights of 27 September 2007 and 5 October 2007 (57 and 65 days after the GRB). At the afterglow position, a source is clearly detected with R = 25.03 0.10 (Fig. fig:finding ). A faint source is also visible in K , with magnitude K = 21.70 0.25 . The centroid of the host is offset by 0.15 0.04 arcsec with respect to the afterglow position (1.2 kpc at z = 2.455 ).

sources.summary: The host of GRB 070802 is optically detected but weak in Spitzer, implying a low stellar mass and young population after a dust-reddened afterglow.

quotes.quote: The optical afterglow was also observed spectroscopically with the VLT, establishing an absorption redshift of 2.454 Eliasdottir+2009 . Both the GROND SED and the VLT spectrum show a clearly reddened continuum with a significant detection of the 2175 \ dust bump. The actual degree of dust attenuation is borderline for inclusion: estimate A_V = 1.23 0.17 mag, only marginally establishing A_V 1 mag.

We add to this HST and Spitzer observations of the source from our programs. The host position in the 3.6 m IRAC image was affected by column pull-down originating from a bright star elsewhere in the image; we removed this effect by adding flux to this column in the vicinity of the host until the artifact disappeared and then performed photometry in the usual way, but this point should nevertheless be used with caution. While well detected in the optical bands, the host of GRB 070802 is only weakly detected by Spitzer , indicating a low stellar mass and relatively young population. We estimate an SFR of 16 M_ yr ^ 1 and a mass of 5 10^9 M _ .

sources.summary: The host of GRB 070802 is presented as a z=2.452, moderately red galaxy with a 4000 break, little internal reddening, measured luminosity, mass, and star formation.

quotes.quote: The host of GRB 070802 The host of GRB 070802 at z=2.452 was discovered in deep FORS R and ISAAC K band imaging 2009ApJ...697.1725E .

To construct the optical/NIR host SED, the R 25.2 mag host is further observed with EFOSC/NTT in i and HAWK-I/VLT in the J band. The galaxy is moderately red ( (R-K)_ AB 1.8 mag ) and its SED (Fig. hostfigs ) shows a 4000 break, but the age of the dominant stellar population is not well constrained by the available data ( 1 Gyr). There is no strong evidence for internal reddening, and the best-fit absolute magnitude is M_B=-21.4 0.2 mag , which is 0.6 L^* at z 2.5 with (M_ ) = 9.7^ +0.2 _ 0.3 . Using the rest-frame UV flux derived from the galaxy model fitting, an estimate for the SFR of 10_ 7 ^ +30 /yr, and the SSFR of 2 Gyr ^ 1 can be derived.

gmw_00001462.name: host of GRB 080207
gmw_00001462.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 080207 is a massive, extremely red galaxy [arXiv:1301.5903, arXiv:1202.1434] at a spectroscopic redshift of 2.086 [arXiv:1301.5903, arXiv:1205.4036]. It is classified as an ultraluminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:2007.11874] characterized by dust extinction [arXiv:1106.3988, arXiv:1505.06743] and a rich reservoir of molecular gas [arXiv:1709.00424].

sources.summary: The host of GRB 080207 is a very red, massive, dust-extinguished dark-burst host at spectroscopic redshift 2.086 with uncertain SFR but well-constrained high mass.

quotes.quote: GRB 080207 was followed quickly with deep imaging at the VLT and not detected in any filter, including in the NIR. The corresponding upper limit on the extinction ( A_V > 2.0 mag) is actually not as strong as that of most other sources in our sample since the X-ray afterglow faded quite rapidly, but the depth of the nondetections (coupled with a very high X-ray N_ H ) clearly identify this event as a dust-extinguished dark burst. The host galaxy of GRB 080207 has been studied in detail by , and . The photometric redshifts presented in the latter two papers ( z=1.74^ +0.34 _ 0.18 and z=2.2^ +0.2 _ 0.3 , respectively) have also recently been confirmed by X-shooter spectroscopy that establishes the redshift of 2.086 Kruehler+2012b .

Because the source is so red (in terms of R-K it is the reddest object in the sample), and the optical detections are all at relatively low significance, there is significant degeneracy between a mature population and a highly dust-obscured, rapidly star-forming one. This results in a large range of uncertainty in the SFR and A_V , and even an SFR of zero is only ruled out at about 1 , although the clear detection of several emission lines in the NIR with X-shooter suggests that the actual value is probably not far from the best fit, 50 M _ yr ^ 1 (the dust-uncorrected H SFR from the X-shooter provides a lower limit of 10--15 M _ yr ^ 1 ). In addition, the HST magnitudes are somewhat discrepant (by about 0.5 mag, many times the photometric error) from ground-based photometry in the nearby filters, leading to an unavoidably poor fit. The mass is not affected by these issues and is well constrained at 1.2 10^ 11 M _ , a large value intermediate between (but consistent with) the estimates provided by and .

sources.summary: The paper finds radio emission near the GRB 080207 host, notes ambiguous host/afterglow origin, and describes the host as a dusty, rapidly star-forming system detected at 24 microns.

quotes.quote: The source is detected individually (at lower significance) in both of these windows separately, with a flux density of 18.9 6.6 Jy at 5.15 GHz and 12.7 6.3 Jy at 6.30 GHz. Unfortunately, this imposes only a relatively weak constraint on the spectral index of the source ( = -2.0 3.9 ) and does not rule out either model. To evaluate the spatial extent and location of the source, we created an additional map with a (non-optimal) resolution of 0.75 0.75 (FWHM). The point-source flux in this map is slightly less than, but statistically consistent with, the flux in the default-resolution map, suggesting a pointlike source. Its location ( = 13:50:02.96, = +07:30:07.4) is within the uncertainty region of the Chandra location Hunt+2011,Svensson+2012 , and roughly halfway between the brightest optical component of the host and the fainter northern component.

The host galaxy of GRB 080207 has been detected at 24 m with MIPS Hunt+2011,Svensson+2012 . The 24 m flux is commonly used as an independent measure of the obscured star-formation rate of a galaxy, and the inferred value of several hundred M_ yr ^ 1 Hunt+2011,Svensson+2012 is consistent with the notion of this being a heavily dust-obscured and rapidly star-forming system. Star-formation therefore almost certainly contributes at least somewhat to the radio flux observed, although as the inferred radio SFR is even larger than this ( 850 M_ yr ^ 1 ; sec:sfrs ) an afterglow-dominated origin is not ruled out.

sources.summary: Object B is identified as the most likely GRB 080207 host, an elongated extremely red galaxy inside the XRT error circle with a confirmed redshift near 2.1.

quotes.quote: In addition, the ISAAC image shows another, elongated source (B; 1 6 0 9 ) within the XRT error circle that has a very faint R_C band counterpart with R_ AB 26.5 . On the GROND images we do not detect these sources in any band, only upper limits can be provided (Table tab:PhotomGROND ). Object B is very red, (R-K)_ AB 4.7 mag. Its color and morphology defines it as an ERO galaxy. Given its position within the 90 .l. error circle, we consider B as the most likely GRB host candidate.

Therefore, we conclude that this is the host galaxy of GRB 080207. derived a photometric redshift of about 2.2 for this galaxy, which was subsequently confirmed spectroscopically (z=2.086; Kruhler2012a ).

sources.summary: The paper characterizes the GRB 080207 host as a z~2 dark-GRB host with ALMA FIR continuum and marginal [C II] detections, main-sequence SFR, and low [C II]/FIR ratio.

quotes.quote: GRB 080207 host GRB 080207 host is one of the most explored dark GRB host galaxies at z 2 . After the GRB detection by Swift /BAT Racusin2008 , deep optical and NIR follow-up observations were performed e.g., Kuepcue2008,Cucchiara2008,Fugazza2008 . No optical and NIR afterglows were detected. The dust extinction along the line of sight to GRB 080207 is A _ V > 2.0 mag Perley2013a .

[ C  ii ] 158 m emission of the GRB 080207 host was marginally detected around the expected frequency for z=2.0858 Kruhler2012 at the position of the GRB 080207 host by removing the continuum emission (Fig. fig2 a). The velocity offset between [ C  ii ] 158 m and optical emission lines is 155 km s ^ 1 measured by a Gaussian fitting. The CO(1-0) velocity of the GRB 080207 host galaxy also shows an offset from the optical emission lines Hatsukade2019 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 080207 host as a faint, extremely red dust-obscured galaxy at photometric redshift about 2.2 with high SFR, large stellar mass, and significant extinction.

quotes.quote: We present optical, near-infrared, and \ IRAC and MIPS observations of the host galaxy of the dark gamma-ray burst . The host is faint, with extremely red optical-infrared colors ( R-K = 6.3 , 24 / R- band flux 1000 ) making it an extremely red object (ERO) and a dust-obscured galaxy (DOG). The spectral energy distribution (SED) shows the clear signature of the 1.6 \ photometric ``bump'', typical of evolved stellar populations. We use this bump to establish the photometric redshift \ as 2.2 ^ +0.2 _ 0.3 , using a vast library of SED templates, including M 82. The star-formation rate (SFR) inferred from the SED fitting is 119 yr ^ 1 , the stellar mass 3 10^ 11 , and \ extinction from 1-2 mag.

The ERO and DOG nature of the host galaxy of the dark \ may be emblematic of a distinct class of dark GRB hosts, with high SFRs, evolved and metal-rich stellar populations, and significant dust extinction within the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 080207 host as a dark-GRB galaxy at z=2.086, massive, red, vigorously star-forming, dusty, and the highest-metallicity z>2 host in the sample.

quotes.quote: GRB 080207 ( z=2.086 ) is also a well-discussed dark GRB 2011ApJ...736L..36H, 2012MNRAS.421...25S . Its host is detected by various facilities from the optical to radio 2012A&A...545A..77R, 2013ApJ...778..128P , and has a very high stellar mass ( M_ 10^ 11 ), extremely red optical/NIR colors and is vigorously forming stars ( SFR_ H 90 ).

Because of the large A_V 2 , is not detected and the applied strong-line diagnostics are thus based exclusively on . We measure an oxygen abundance around solar, = 8.74 0.15 . This is the GRB host with the highest metallicity at z>2 in the sample. Its metallicity, however, is averaged over the multiple knots that are detected at the spatial resolution of HST 2012MNRAS.421...25S , which in theory could host a range of metallicities.

sources.summary: The paper fits the GRB080207 host with a small AGN component, classifies it as a ULIRG, and argues ALMA data drive a higher SFR estimate.

quotes.quote: We found that the best-fit SED contains an AGN component excluding the radio emission with the smallest reduced ^2 value of 2.32. The flux upper limits reported in previous studies are consistent with the best-fit template (Fig. fig2 ). The AGN fraction of the best-fit model is 0.0012 0.0007 .

Interestingly, all of the four different conditions suggest that this host galaxy is an ULIRG because its IR luminosity is larger than 10^ 12 L_ , which is consistent with both previous papers. However, the SFR derived by the SED fitting in this work is much higher ( 50 to 100 ) than that in both Hunt2014 and Hashimoto2019 . The IR luminosity of the best-fit template corresponds to SFR _ Kennicutt =305(M_ yr^ 1 ) assuming Kennicutt's law (Eq. Kennicutt1998 ). This value is much closer to the SFR derived from the SED fitting analysis in this work than that in previous papers. Our new SFR measurement is more reasonable than that in previous studies if GRB hosts follows the Kennicutt's law. The reason for the difference is due to the new ALMA data.

aliases_in_paper: host galaxy of Gamma Ray Burst (GRB) 080207
sources.summary: The paper reports CO(3-2) emission from the z=2.086 GRB 080207 host, finding a gas-rich star-forming galaxy with typical gas fraction and depletion time.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of the CO(3-2) emission line from the host galaxy of Gamma Ray Burst (GRB) 080207 at z = 2.086. This is the first detection of molecular gas in emission from a GRB host galaxy beyond redshift 1. We find this galaxy to be rich in molecular gas with a mass of 1.1 10^ 11 M_ assuming _ CO = 4.36 M_ ( K km s^ 1 pc^2)^ 1 . The molecular gas mass fraction of the galaxy is 0.5, typical of star forming galaxies (SFGs) with similar stellar masses and redshifts. With a SFR_ FIR of 260 M_ yr^ 1 , we measure a molecular-gas-depletion timescale of 0.43 Gyr, near the peak of the depletion timescale distribution of SFGs at similar redshifts.

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 080207 host as observed in deep molecular-line searches, lying on the main sequence, and falling in the dark-galaxy regime.

quotes.quote: Observations and Data Reduction ht We observed the hosts of GRB 060814 and 080207 with the IRAM 30m telescope (proposal 172-16; PI: M.J.M.), equipped with the Eight MIxer Receiver www.iram.es/IRAMES/mainWiki/EmirforAstronomers Carter2012A&A...538A..89C . We implemented the wobbler-switching mode and the Fourier Transform Spectrometers 200 (FTS-200) providing 195 kHz spectral resolution and 16 GHz bandwidth in each linear polarisation. The observations for both targets were executed between 2017-Feb-01 and 2017-May-22 and lasted in total 13.1 hr on-source for GRB 060814 and 17.8 hr for GRB 080207.
We find that GRBs 060814 is about seven times above the MS, while GRB 080207 lies on the corresponding MS within the scatter.

The estimated upper limit /CO temperature brightness ratio of <0.1 for the GRB 080207 hosts places it in the regime of dark galaxies. Very high gas density between 10^5 and 10^6 cm ^ 3 have also been inferred for this galaxy using a \ marginal detection Hashimoto2019MNRAS.488.5029H , which is consistent with the proposed dark nature of the host.

sources.summary: The paper confirms the luminous, massive GRB 080207 host at z=2.086 and highlights it as an extreme dusty dark-burst system with high X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: GRB 080207 The luminous and massive host of the dark ( _ OX 0.3 ) GRB 080207 was studied in detail photometrically by 2011ApJ...736L..36H and 2011arXiv1109.3167S , yielding photometric redshifts of z^ phot 2 . The spectroscopic redshift based on  and  is z^ spec =2.086 , and thus confirms the estimation by the preceding authors.
Clearly, dark (which are predominantly dusty) bursts have significantly higher N_ H,X values with its most extreme example GRB 080207 exceeding N_ H,X >10^ 23 cm ^ 2 (Figure  nhhist ).

gmw_00001463.name: host of GRB 090417B
gmw_00001463.overview: The host of GRB 090417B is a galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.345 [arXiv:1005.1675, arXiv:1301.5903]. It is a red galaxy [arXiv:1005.1675] with a moderate stellar mass [arXiv:1301.5903], substantial dust extinction [arXiv:1005.1675], and a recently declining star formation rate [arXiv:1301.5903].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J135846.65+470104.5 as the likely host of dark burst GRB 090417B, an approximately L* galaxy at z=0.345 with red colors, Milky-Way-like luminosity, star formation, and substantial extinction.

quotes.quote: This is a fairly small value, which suggests that SDSS J135846.65+470104.5 is likely to be the host galaxy of GRB 090417B. A chance alignment probability of P_ ch 10^ 3 is large enough that one can not rule out the possibility of a misidentification. A visual examination of the NOT images shows that the XRT error circle includes a significant fraction of the SDSS galaxy (see Figure FIGURE:not_field ).
SDSS J135846.65+470104.5 has a colour of B R = +1.91 0.13 , making it a fairly red galaxy.

We have shown that the host of the dark GRB 090417B is very likely to be SDSS J135846.65+470104.5, an L^* galaxy at z = 0.345 . This galaxy has an overall star-formation rate that is at least as great as that of the Milky Way, and an overall V band extinction of A_V 3.5 mag, which is dustier than typical GRB host galaxies, but not greatly different from the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The host of GRB 090417B is a bright SDSS galaxy at z=0.345 with a Balmer break, moderate stellar mass, and low current SFR.

quotes.quote: GRB 090417B was a moderately bright but very long burst; its prominent X-ray afterglow was immediately recognized to be coincident with an SDSS galaxy GCN9140 whose redshift was measured early to be 0.345 GCN9156 . Given this bright host, afterglow upper limits reported in the GCN Circulars are not straightforward to interpret (image subtraction against a later reference epoch would be necessary); in the very conservative assumption that the afterglow is no brighter than the quoted host-galaxy magnitudes, the A_V > 1 mag criterion is marginally satisfied, though in all likelihood the real limits are much deeper than anything currently quoted and so the actual extinction is probably significantly larger.

Like some other bursts in the sample, the strong Balmer break points toward a declining SFR despite a relatively young stellar age. For a constant SFR we derive a best-fit value of 3 M _ yr ^ 1 , but a significantly better fit is obtained if the current SFR is decoupled from the assumption of a constant star-formation history (in which case it converges to 0.35 0.2 M _ yr ^ 1 , suggesting a recent decline in the SFR). The stellar mass is moderate at 3.5 10^9 M _ , intermediate between the SMC and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). Interestingly, while the host of GRB 090417B is a relatively low-mass galaxy (like other low- z GRB hosts), it does not appear to be forming stars at a particularly high rate for its mass (quite unlike most other low- z GRB hosts).

gmw_00001464.name: EGS 29337
gmw_00001464.overview: EGS 29337 is a bright, near point-like galaxy [arXiv:2207.06628] spectroscopically confirmed as a Lyman-alpha emitter at a redshift of approximately z = 7.47 [arXiv:2301.07072, arXiv:2309.06656]. The galaxy features metal-poor, highly ionized gas [arXiv:2301.07072] and exhibits a strong photometric excess from extreme optical line emission [arXiv:1606.01304, arXiv:2309.06656].

sources.summary: The paper describes CEERS-698 as a bright z=7.47 Ly emitter with extreme optical line ratios, metal-poor highly ionized gas, broad [O III], and low Ly escape fraction.

quotes.quote: CEERS-698 was first identified in Roberts-Borsani2016 as a UV-bright dropout (EGS-zs8-2) with an IRAC [ 4.5 ] excess suggesting large [ O III ] +H EW ( 2564 ; Table tab:sed_fitting ), a young light-weighted age ( 3.8 Myr), and very efficient ionizing photon production ( [ _ ion / erg ^ 1 \ Hz ] =25.8 ). The source was spectroscopically confirmed with Keck/MOSFIRE via detection of Ly (EW =9.3 ) at z_ Ly =7.477 in Stark2017 , following tentative identification in Roberts-Borsani2016 . CEERS/NIRSpec observations detect a suite of rest-frame optical lines in CEERS-698, revealing line ratios that reflect the extreme ionizing nature of the SED. The large O32 ( 14.72 ) and Ne3O2 ( 0.87 ) ratios are rarely seen in normal star-forming galaxies at lower redshifts, pointing to gas with a very large ionization parameter ( U =-1.85 ).

Photoionization models suggest metal poor gas ( 12+ [ O/H ] =7.81 ), consistent with the direct value implied by the tentative detection of [ O III ] 4363 (see Table tab:photoionization ). Ly is detected with NIRSpec with similar flux and EW ( 9.5 ) to that measured previously with Keck. Using the systemic redshift derived from the rest-frame optical lines ( z_ sys =7.470 ), we are able to compute a Ly velocity offset of 545 184 km s ^ 1 (Fig. fig:lya_offset ). The NIRSpec detections of Ly and H indicate that the transmission of Ly is very low ( 0.045 0.015 ), suggesting the majority of Ly is scattered out of the NIRSpec micro-shutter.

sources.summary: The paper describes EGS-zs8-2 as a bright CANDELS galaxy with very red IRAC color, extreme optical-line flux excess, and confirmed Ly emission at z=7.477.

quotes.quote: EGS-zs8-2 EGS-zs8-2 is another bright (H _ 160 =25.1) galaxy identified in CANDELS imaging by RB16. The IRAC color of EGS-zs8-2 ( [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] = 0.96 0.17 ) is redder than EGS-zs8-1, likely reflecting yet more extreme optical line emission. We estimate a rest-frame [ OIII ] +H equivalent width of 1610 302 \ is required to reproduce the flux excess in the [ 4.5 ] filter.

A 4.7 emission feature was identified by Roberts-borsani2015 at a wavelength of 1.031 m. RB16 tentatively interpret this feature as Ly . We obtained a Y-band spectrum of EGS-zs8-2 with the goal of verifying the putative Ly detection. The spectrum we obtained shows a 7.4 emission line at 1.0305 m (Figure fig:egs-zs82 a), confirming that EGS-zs8-2 is indeed a Ly emitter at z _ Ly =7.477 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies EGS-zs8-2 as a UV-luminous z=7.48 Ly source near EGS-zs8-1, with its field showing five high-redshift sources and a larger overdensity.

quotes.quote: These are EGS-zs8-1 at z_ spec = 7.7302 0.0006 ( Oesch2015 ; see also Stark2017 ; Tilvi2020 ), EGS-zs8-2 at z_ spec = 7.4770 0.0008 Borsani2016,Stark2017 , and EGSY-z8p7 at z_ spec = 8.683^ +0.001 _ 0.004 ( Zitrin2015 ; see also Mainali18 ). All of these sources are expected to exhibit strong radiation fields due to high-equivalent width [ OIII ] +H emission lines identified through their Spitzer/IRAC photometry . Indeed, highly excited UV lines have been confirmed in the MOSFIRE spectra of EGS-zs8-1 ( C iii ; Stark2017 ) and EGSY-z8p7 ( N v ; Mainali18 ).

Additionally, the galaxy group around EGS-zs8-1 is separated by only 9.1 pMpc from the other UV-luminous, spectroscopically confirmed source EGS-zs8-2 at z=7.48. This distance is smaller than the average bubble radius of 10 pMpc expected at the end of cosmic reionization Wyithe2005,Geil2017 , and points at a larger scale overdensity in this field at this redshift. One of the main goals of our follow-up observations was to further characterize this overdensity through fainter sources. Hence, we centred two of our HST pointings around EGS-zs8-1 and EGS-zs8-2, respectively (orange and yellow outlines in Fig fig:fieldLayout ).
The EGS-zs8-2 field resulted in 5 sources.

sources.summary: The paper identifies EGS 29337 as a near point-like z=7.4 galaxy also found in another catalog, using it to show compact sources can be bona-fide galaxies.

quotes.quote: We find that only EGS 29337 is detected in both catalogs (EGSY-0120800269) with the separation of =0. 11 , which is well within the PSF uncertainty. In fact, this object has also been spectroscopically confirmed as as a z_ ph =7.4 galaxy rborsani2016ApJ,Stark2017MNRAS . This means that some near point-like sources are in fact bona-fide galaxies. We discuss the implications in Section sec:disc . Our SED modeling in Figure fig:eazybagsSED also supports these observations.
In fact, EGS 29337 has been shown to be one of the brightest z>7 known with large SFR Stark2017MNRAS,rborsani2016ApJ .

sources.summary: The paper identifies WERLS 69492, also EGS-zs8-2, as its highest-SNR secure LAE, with prior MOSFIRE detections, z=7.4763 photometry consistency, and strong nebular-emission excess.

quotes.quote: WERLS 69492 is a known LAE, first reported as EGS-zs8-2 in 2016roberts-borsani , who detect \ at 4.7 with MOSFIRE and measure a redshift of = 7.4777; this observation is later supported by 2017stark , who report a 7.4 \ detection for the target. It is in our secure sample and is our highest SNR \ detection, with SNR = 7.7. The target is on mask wmme01 in the EGS field and is covered by CANDELS (ID: 69492) but does not fall in the CEERS JWST /NIRCam coverage. We fit the CANDELS photometry and find a photometric redshift of = 7.8^ +0.2 _ 0.4 , consistent within uncertainty with our derived = 7.4763. The Bagpipes SED fit suggests excess from nebular emission, which is supported by the strong photometric excess in IRAC Channel 2 (4.5 ) of 1.19 mag corroborated by 2016roberts-borsani .

sources.summary: The paper includes EGS-zs8-2 among its mature z~8 galaxy sample, fitting it with spheroidal models and finding a photometric redshift around z~8.75 and properties similar to EGS-zs8-1.

quotes.quote: The galaxies EGS2 and EGS1 Best-fits with error bars of the two other sample galaxies are presented on Fig  EGS1E and EGS2 on Fig  EGS2E . Most other outputs as metallicity, masses after calibration are on Table  Results .
For EGS2, the best-fitted SED shape within a few \ gives the photometric redshift z_ LyB =8.75 ^ +1.25 _ 0.95 . All other properties given on Table  Results show the similarity of these two galaxies.

gmw_00001465.name: SDSS J2219-0938
gmw_00001465.overview: SDSS J2219-0938 is a galaxy system featuring a main galaxy and a companion galaxy [arXiv:2302.01547]. Although the system exhibits two apparent cores separated by a projected distance of about 5480 parsecs, emission line flux ratios disfavour a dual-core system interpretation [arXiv:2302.01547].

sources.summary: The paper tests whether SDSS J2219-0938 is a dual-core system and disfavors that interpretation using Balmer-line flux ratios from its main and companion galaxies.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: central position of RA=22:19:24.98 and DEC=-09:38:21.6

quotes.quote: Then, the precious dual core system in SDSS J2219-0938 is discussed. After subtracting pPXF code determined stellar lights, double-peaked narrow Balmer emission lines are confirmed in the main galaxy with confidence level higher than 5 , but single-peaked narrow Balmer emission lines in the companion galaxy. Through measured fluxes of emission components, f_ e, /f_ , is around 0.82, different from f_ e, /f_ , 0.52 , to disfavour a dual core system for the double-peaked narrow Balmer emission lines in SDSS J2219-0938.

Left panel of Fig. img shows the 25.8 25.8 colorful photometric image with central position of RA=22:19:24.98 and DEC=-09:38:21.6. Right panel of Fig. img shows the image in contour, leading to two apparent cores with central positions (brightness peak positions) of (RA=334.8548, DEC=-9.63938) and (RA=334.8541, DEC=-9.63932) marked as red pluses, leading to the projected space distance about 2.6 (5480pc at redshift 0.0948) between the two nuclei.

gmw_00001466.name: NGC 4150
gmw_00001466.overview: NGC 4150 is a low-luminosity lenticular galaxy [arXiv:0712.4189, arXiv:0801.3969] located in the outskirts of the Virgo cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0210414]. It features a compact central molecular gas disk [arXiv:0712.4189, arXiv:0812.2594], a counter-rotating young stellar core [arXiv:0712.4189, arXiv:0812.2594], and a spiral dust disk likely fueled by a recent minor merger [arXiv:1112.3641].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4150 as a low-luminosity lenticular with compact central molecular gas, dust structures, and a young counterrotating stellar core not clearly matched by CO.

quotes.quote: NGC 4150 shows a relatively small amount of molecular gas in a very compact structure at the center of the galaxy. We fit the integrated spectrum in Figure n4150spect with a Gaussian whose central velocity is 239   20 , which we take as the systemic velocity of the CO emission. This CO velocity is in good agreement with the stellar velocity measurements of 226   22 \ fisher95 , 208   30 \ UZC , and 219   10 \ emsellem04 . CO emission is detected over a velocity range of 180 , and the bulk of this gas is within a few arcseconds of the nucleus of the galaxy (Figure n4150mom0 ).

There is a counterrotating stellar core in the central arcseconds of NGC 4150 and it is also visible in the position-velocity diagram of Figure n4150majax mcdermid06a, mcdermid06b . This counterrotating core is believed to have formed recently as the galaxy shows a very strong radial age gradient. Curiously, though, the core does not appear to have a counterpart in the molecular gas at the current resolution and sensitivity.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4150 as a lower-mass star-forming early type with modest SFR, older SSP ages, less molecular gas, high line ratios, and young populations beyond present PAH regions.

quotes.quote: Widespread Star-Forming Fast Rotators In Figure sfrmstar , the \ galaxies (NGC 3032, NGC 3156, NGC 3489, NGC 4150, NGC 4550) are seen to be those star-forming early-type galaxies with lower stellar masses. Given the comparable molecular content of all star-forming galaxies in our sample (see Table tbl:sfr ), the \ galaxies are consequently also characterized by higher gas fractions and specific star formation rates. As a result, the current star formation event dominates the single stellar population (SSP) ages, which are measured to be young throughout these systems ( < 1 3 Gyr; Kuntschner et al. in prep ). This is consistent with the location of the \ galaxies in Figure ks ; these systems have molecular gas and star formation surface densities similar to those found in late-type galaxies undergoing global star formation.

NGC 3156, NGC 3489, NGC 4150; SFR = 0.03 0.06 \ ) have SSP ages older by 1 2 Gyr, an order of magnitude less molecular gas, and high / \ ratios that are inconsistent with significant on-going star formation .

Lower SFRs in a \ galaxy also correlates with an increasingly irregular star formation and gas distribution, from a regular, disk-like morphology (NGC 3032) to disturbed ionized and molecular gas distributions (NGC 3489; Figure Main-SF and Crocker et al. in prep ) to highly asymmetric molecular gas distributions (NGC 4550; Cro+09 ). These increasingly irregular morphologies additionally correspond to more centralized star formation at lower SFR (compare NGC 3032, NGC 3156, and NGC 4550 in Figure profiles a ). In the absence of a replenishing gas reservoir, \ galaxies will experience declining SFRs, causing them to move to the left in Figure sfrmstar ; the system with the most intense star formation (NGC 3032) will therefore be expected to eventually move towards the other \ galaxies. Likewise, there is evidence that the \ galaxies with lower SFRs previously had more intense star formation events that extended to larger radii, implying earlier positions to the right of their current location in Figure sfrmstar . In particular, the young SSP ages (1 3 Gyr in NGC 3156, NGC 3489, NGC 4150; 6 Gyr in NGC 4550; Kuntschner et al. in prep ) found throughout these galaxies, beyond the star-forming regions observed in PAH emission, indicate that star formation activity occurred out to larger radii in the past than in the present.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes NGC 4150 as a low-luminosity S0 galaxy with a smooth exponential profile, dusty and young central region, some gas, and a counter-rotating circumnuclear disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 4150. This is a low-luminosity S0 galaxy with a smooth photometric profile, which is fit well by an exponential law from the very center out to at least = 80''   [ 12 ] . The central region shows traces of dust observed against the bright stellar background. UV observations reveal a bright nucleus, indicative of the presence of young stars  [ 18 ] . The inner part of the galaxy exhibits small amounts of molecular and atomic gas  [ 4 ] .
The kinematics of the inner region of the galaxy was studied earlier as part of the SAURON project  [ 20 ] . The circumnuclear region ( <5'' ) appears to host a counter-rotating disk.

sources.summary: NGC 4150 has a small inner gas disk connected to larger faint H I plumes, indicating accreted gas and a history similar to NGC 3489.

quotes.quote: The final category (denoted D, for disc/ring) refers to galaxies where most of the \ is found in a fairly regularly rotating disc or ring. This category contains the galaxies NGC 2685, NGC 3032, NGC 3414, NGC 3489, NGC 4150 and NGC 4278, while also the only cluster galaxy detected (NGC 4262) falls in this category. In NGC 2685 and in NGC 4278 the \ disc is large, i.e. extending beyond the bright optical body, while in NGC 3032, NGC 3489 and in NGC 4150 the \ forms a small, inner gas disc.
The accretion and inner discs in NGC 3489 and NGC 4150 appear to have a similar history, where NGC 4150 is probably at a slightly more evolved stage.

The inner gas discs that are seen in NGC 2768, NGC 3489 and NGC 4150 connect to large, faint \ plumes that are seen at larger radii, clearly showing that these gas structures formed from accreted gas.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4150 unresolved in WFPC2 imaging, sets a TRGB lower distance limit, and argues it is likely on the Virgo cluster outskirts.

quotes.quote: The core of this lenticular galaxy is crossed by a curved dusty furrow (see insert in Fig. 2). In spite of its low radial velocity, V_ LG = 198 km s ^ 1 , NGC 4150 appears unresolved into stars on the WFPC2 images. Its TRGB magnitude appears to exceed I_ lim = 25 mag (beyond our detection limit), yielding a lower limit of 6.3 Mpc for its distance. We suggest that NGC 4150 belongs to the Virgo cluster outskirts, and not to the CVn I cloud.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4150 as an S0 with a small dusty molecular disk, a misaligned counter-rotating young stellar core, and cold gas from recent mergers fueling elevated star formation.

quotes.quote: The smaller molecular disk in NGC 4150 (disk radius: 0.5 kpc, mass: 0.55 10^8 M_ ) is kinematically oriented with a dusty disk that obscures optical light. However, NGC 4150 contains a counter-rotating core of younger stars having a spatial orientation that differs curiously from that of the molecular gas and rotates in the opposite sense. This suggests that the molecular disk in NGC 4150 also resulted from one or more recent mergers.

The two S0 galaxies with the largest 24 m residuals and L_ FIR /L_ Ks in Figure 5 -- NGC 3032 and 4150 -- have evidently acquired cold gas and dust in recent mergers, and their current star formation rates exceed the rate that mass is supplied by stellar mass loss.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 4150 as intermediate between unsettled and settled gas classes, with a spiral dust disk, evidence for a recent minor merger, high molecular-to-atomic gas fraction, and mid-range line ratios.

quotes.quote: NGC 4150 may bridge the two classes; recent HST WFC3 imaging reveals a dust disc with spiral structure and stellar population analysis suggests a recent ( 1  Gyr) minor merger crockett11 . observations show a low-column density cloud of atomic hydrogen, possibly leftover from this minor merger morganti06 . However the overall molecular-to-atomic gas fraction is quite high, making NGC 4150 more similar to members of the settled disc group. Its molecular line ratios lie in the middle of the ranges we observe.

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC4150 as a known CO detection with consistent line properties, small or irregular dust features, and membership among the youngest compact KDCs detected in CO.

quotes.quote: NGC3489 and NGC4150 were recently detected by ws03 with the IRAM 30 m. They find a wider profile for NGC3489, but with a total bandwidth which is twice smaller, while NGC4150 is consistent.

The dust morphologies across the sample range from well developed large disks ( 10 diameter; e.g. \ NGC2685, NGC3032, NGC4459 and NGC4526) to small nuclear features ( 5 ; e.g. \ NGC3156, NGC4150 and NGC4477) and even larger scale irregular patches or lanes (e.g. \ NGC3156, NGC4150, NGC3489 and NGC4278).
Interestingly, half of the compact KDCs are detected in CO (NGC3032, NGC4150 and NGC7457; the three youngest ones), but none of the large KDCs are.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 7165 as having an almost exponential surface-brightness profile, bright nucleus, nearly constant color, and smooth elliptical overall appearance.

quotes.quote: UGC 7165 : has a SBP that is close to an exponential, and also has a bright nuclear part. The color is almost constant over the whole radius range, apparently including the central part. But the resolution of our data is insufficient to discuss the center in any detail. The galaxy as a whole has a smooth elliptical appearance.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4150 as an example object, arguing that its ovular shape resembles a protoplanetary cloud and reinterpreting it as a collapsing cosmic-scale protoplanetary disk.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:cloud_v_elliptical compares a sketch of a protoplanetary cloud to the observed object NGC 4150. The ovular, egg-like shape of NGC 4150 matches the shape of a protoplanetary cloud. Therefore, the best explanation is that this so-called ``elliptical galaxy" is actually a protoplanetary cloud which is likely in the process of collapsing into a cosmic-scale protoplanetary disk, from which a plethora of planetary systems will be born.

gmw_00001467.name: AX J131831+3341
gmw_00001467.overview: AX J131831+3341 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of 0.653 [arXiv:astro-ph/0012501] that hosts an absorbed quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0012502]. The host galaxy features a point-like nucleus and an asymmetric, extended component [arXiv:astro-ph/0012501] with colors consistent with a post-starburst stellar population [arXiv:astro-ph/0012502].

sources.summary: The paper identifies AX J131831+3341 as an absorbed QSO whose asymmetric extended host is large, luminous, disk-like in profile, and consistent with a young stellar population.

quotes.quote: AX J131831+3341 is an AGN at a redshift of 0.653, discovered during the optical identification of hard X-ray sources in the ASCA Large Sky Survey (LSS; Akiyama et al. 2000, hereafter Paper I). The optical spectrum of the object shows strong emission lines, such as broad Mg II 2800 , narrow [ O II ] 3727 , and narrow [ O III ] 5007 , but no broad H emission line. Its small H to- [ O III ] 5007 equivalent width ratio [ log(H / [ O III ] ) =-0.54 ] is comparable to those of Seyfert 1.8--2 galaxies (Winkler 1992).

The galaxy has a point-like nucleus and a peculiar extended component. The profile of the nucleus is well represented by the point spread function (PSF) in the image. The peculiar structure extends 8^ away from the nuclear component. The structure has an asymmetric feature: it extends more in the southeastern direction than in the northwestern direction. In the southeastern region, there is a gap running from the center to the edge of the component.

sources.summary: The paper resolves AX J131831+3341 into a red absorbed QSO nucleus and an extended host component whose colors favor a post-starburst galaxy interpretation.

quotes.quote: The extended component is thought to be the host galaxy of the QSO. The optical absolute magnitude of the component is similar to those of the brightest host galaxies of QSOs (Paper II). The V-R and R-I colors of the component are consistent with a 1 Gyr-old stellar population model without absorption. If we introduce optical extinction ( A_V=1 - 3 mag) in the host galaxy, the colors are also explained by a disk model at any ages or an elliptical at ages of less than 0.1 Gyr (Paper II). The I-K and R-I colors of the extended component are plotted in figure 3 (filled circle).

gmw_00001468.name: SDSSJ120136.02+300305.5
gmw_00001468.overview: SDSSJ120136.02+300305.5 is a quiescent, optically inactive galaxy at a redshift of z=0.146 [arXiv:1202.5900, arXiv:1404.4933]. It hosted a soft X-ray tidal disruption event with deep, recurring light curve dips that suggest the presence of a supermassive black hole binary at its core [arXiv:1505.01093, arXiv:1404.4933].

sources.summary: The review presents SDSSJ120136.02+300305.5 as a soft X-ray TDE with a well-covered fading lightcurve, intermittent drops, no radio jet, and a compact binary-SMBH interpretation.

quotes.quote: Among these, SDSSJ120136.02+300305.5 has the best-covered first-year lightcurve (Saxton et al. 2012), based on follow-ups with XMM-Newton and Swift . Overall, the X-rays continue fading after high-state. Additional large-amplitude variability is apparent on the timescale of weeks (Fig. 4). The X-ray spectrum of SDSSJ120136.02+300305.5, observed with XMM-Newton weeks and months after high-state is very soft (no photons detected beyond 2-3 keV), but is not well fit with black-body emission.

The lightcurve of the TDE from SDSSJ120136.02+300305.5 (Sect. 2.2, Fig. 4) does show the overall downward trend expected after tidal disruption. However, one month after the peak, the X-ray emission suddenly dropped by a factor >50 within a week and the source was no longer detected by Swift . X-rays re-appeared after 115d, and then dropped a second time.

Simulations by Liu et al. (2014) have shown, that the lightcurve of SDSSJ120136.02+300305.5 is well consistent with a binary SMBH model (Fig. 4) with a primary mass of 10^6 M _ , a mass ratio q 0.1 and semi-major axis of 0.6 milli-pc (mpc).

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J120136.02+300305.5 as a quiescent z=0.146 galaxy whose nuclear X-ray flare faded like a tidal disruption event, with weak optical lines and no detected radio jet.

quotes.quote: was detected in an slew from June 2010 with a flux 56 times higher than an upper limit from ROSAT, corresponding to L _ X 3 10^ 44 . It has the optical spectrum of a quiescent galaxy (z=0.146). Overall the X-ray flux has evolved consistently with the canonical t ^ 5/3 model, expected for returning stellar debris, fading by a factor 300 over 300 days. In detail the source is very variable and became invisible to between 27 and 48 days after discovery, perhaps due to self-absorption. The X-ray spectrum is soft but is not the expected tail of optically thick thermal emission.

Optical spectra taken 12 days and 11 months after discovery indicate a deficit of material in the broad line and coronal line regions of this galaxy, while a deep radio non-detection implies that a jet was not launched during this event. X-rays: galaxies -- galaxies:individual:SDSS J120136.02+300305.5 --

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J120136.02+300305.5 as an optically inactive TDE host whose X-ray dips are consistent with a milliparsec SMBH binary.

quotes.quote: Here, we apply that model to the light curve of the optically inactive galaxy SDSS J120136.02+300305.5, which was identified as a candidate TDE with XMM-Newton . We show that the deep dips in its evolving X-ray light curve can be well explained by the presence of a SMBHB at its core. A SMBHB model with a mass of the primary of M_ BH = 10^7 M_ , a mass ratio q 0.08 , and a semimajor axis a_ b 0.6 mpc is in good agreement with the observations.

gmw_00001469.name: MG J0414+0534
gmw_00001469.overview: MG J0414+0534 is a heavily reddened, radio-loud quasar at redshift z=2.64 [arXiv:0711.4267, arXiv:1808.05571]. The system is gravitationally lensed into four images by a foreground early-type galaxy at z=0.96 [arXiv:1102.3433, arXiv:1808.05571], and it hosts a highly luminous water maser associated with its active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1204.2941].

sources.summary: The paper discusses MG0414+0534 as heavily reddened, weighs lens-galaxy versus host-galaxy dust, and finds its quasar colours best explained by homogeneous foreground lens-galaxy dust.

quotes.quote: This is an interesting case of a quasar that is heavily reddened. There have been claims both that the reddening is caused by dust in the lens galaxy, and in the host galaxy. lawrence reason that the most likely cause is dust extinction in the lens galaxy. They argue that (1) the observed spectrum can be well-explained by dust at intervening redshifts They used the wrong lensing redshift, but according to our analysis with colour comparisons the claim is still valid for the correct redshift. , while extinction in the host galaxy is unable to reproduce the spectrum for Galactic extinction and barely for SMC-like extinction; (2) the separation between the light paths for the different images at the host is too small to explain the difference in colours between the images; in contrast, a separation at the lensing galaxy would be able to reproduce the colour difference better; and (3) two of the reddest quasars that are known, MG 0414+0534 and MG 1131+0456, both have an intervening galaxy, and it is unlikely that both have a foreground galaxy, and that the foreground galaxy is not responsible for the extremely red colours.

tonry suggest that the red colours of MG0414+0534 are caused by dust extinction in the host. Their arguments are that (1) the visible arc of the host galaxy is bluer than the quasar images and that the extinction must be uniform over a large region, because there is little difference in extinction between quasar images, and no notable difference in colour along the arc; and (2) that the colour and surface brightness of the lens galaxy agrees well with that of a passively-evolving early-type galaxy on the Fundamental Plane keeton . When fitting for dust extinction, we found that the colours of the quasar could be explained by presence of dust in the lens galaxy, but not by dust in the host galaxy alone. All images gave reasonably similar values of R_V , indicating that the foreground galaxy has a homogeneous dust content.

sources.summary: The paper identifies MG0414+0534 as a distant, likely passively evolving early-type lens with nearby substructure and a high stellar-mass fraction relative to B1030.

quotes.quote: MG0414 at z=0.960 is the second most distant lens of our sample. Judging by its luminosity and colour, the lens is likely to be a passively evolving early-type galaxy TOK99 . find an object close to image B visible only in I-band, which might contribute to the lensing effect. Our reconstructed mass map also shows increased surface density at the position of the object. RXJ0911 is located on the outskirts of a cluster MCM01 .

Comparing the two intermediate mass lenses B1030 and MG0414 in the middle panels of Fig. comp1 , we find that their cumulative total mass curves are very similar. However, the stellar mass of B1030 is just 30 that of MG0414, independent of the radius. If we consider the stellar radial scale, we find that MG0414 has 4 times the stellar mass of B1030. Their baryon fractions approach values from f_s 0.05 (B1030) to f_s 0.17 (MG0414) at the outermost radius to which we have estimates.

sources.summary: The paper monitors the lensed quasar MG J0414+0534, finding an exceptionally luminous high-redshift water maser associated with its AGN and stable radio continuum and maser emission.

quotes.quote: Our first confirmed high-redshift water maser was found toward the lensed quasar MG J0414+0534 at z=2.64 ( ). The line was originally detected with the Effelsberg radiotelscope and subsequently confirmed with the EVLA that found the emission to be coincident with the lensed images of the quasar (A1 and A2). The apparent isotropic luminosity of the line ( 10,000 L _ , after correcting for the estimated lens magnification) makes the water maser in MG J0414+0534 not only the most distant but also one of the most luminous water masers ever detected and suggests that the emission is associated with the AGN. Based on the large line width of the Effelsberg and EVLA spectra, our initial hypothesis on the origin of the maser was that the emission is associated with the jet(s) of the radio loud quasar. In order to reveal possible variations in the maser flux density, typical of the masers produced by the interactions between a molecular cloud and a radio jet, and determine if a correlation exists between the maser and the continuum emission, we monitored the line and the radio continuum in MG J0414+0534 with the 300-m Arecibo telescope during a time interval of 15 months.

We have monitored the redshifted (rest frequency: 22 GHz) radio continuum and maser emission in MG J0414+0534 for 15 months at 6 week intervals and found that both are surprisingly stable. Absolute deviations of the continuum flux from the mean are on average comparable with the flux calibration uncertainty (7 ). The 6 GHz (observed frequency) continuum flux density of MG J0414+0534 thus remained nearly constant for the duration of the entire monitoring period, with an average flux density of 0.71 0.02 Jy, the error being the standard deviation of the mean.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes ALMA observations of MG J0414+0534, a four-image lensed quasar whose host dust forms an Einstein ring and whose continuum and CO emission show flux-ratio anomalies.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of archival observations with the Atacama Large (sub-)Millimetre Array (ALMA) of the gravitationally lensed quasar MG J0414+0534, which show four compact images of the quasar and an Einstein ring from the dust associated with the quasar host galaxy. We confirm that the flux-ratio anomalies observed in the mid-infrared and radio persists into the sub-mm for the continuum images of the quasar. We report the detection of CO (11--10) spectral line emission, which traces a region of compact gas around the quasar nucleus. This line emission also shows evidence of a flux-ratio anomaly between the merging lensed images that is consistent with those observed at other wavelengths, suggesting high-excitation CO can also provide a useful probe of substructures that is unaffected by microlensing or dust extinction. However, we do not detect the candidate dusty dwarf galaxy that was previously reported with this dataset, which we conclude is due to a noise artefact.

MG J0414+0534 is a radio-loud quasar at z=2.64 that is gravitationally-lensed into four images by a foreground galaxy at z=0.96 . While the optical and near-infrared fluxes of this lensed quasar are effected by microlensing and extinction, the anomalous fluxes of images A2 and B, relative to image A1, persist at mid-infrared and radio wavelengths. The nature of the flux-ratio anomalies are quite complex; image B is thought to be affected by an optically-luminous companion galaxy to the main lens (object X; Falco:1997,Ros:2000,Minezaki:2009 ), whereas image A2 is thought to be perturbed by a dark sub-halo with a mass 10^ 7  M _ (within the Einstein radius; Macleod:2013 ).

sources.summary: MG J0414+0534 is presented as a red quasar with blended high-excitation CO emission, an ultra-fast outflow, and molecular-outflow energetics that make an energy-driven scenario unlikely.

quotes.quote: For MG J0414 + 0534, the CO (7--6) line is so broad that it is blended with the [ CI ] (2--1) line. We fit Gaussians to both lines simultaneously with the systemic redshift fixed to the value found for the molecular gas Barvainis:1998,Stacey:2020 .

Ultra-fast outflows have been detected in X-ray spectra of high-redshift quasars Chartas:2021 including the red quasar MG J0414+0534, which contains an ultra-fast outflow with velocity 0.3 Dadina:2018 that could potentially power the observed molecular outflow. The kinematics of the wind is a key factor to discriminate between the two scenarios: the momentum flux for an energy-driven outflow is expected to be p_ out v_ UFO v_ out L_ AGN , where v_ out and v_ UFO are the velocities of the molecular outflow and ultra-fast outflow, respectively Costa:2018b . For the inferred L_ AGN =10^ 47.2 ergs s^ 1 , we predict an energy-driven mass outflow rate ( M = p / v_ out ) of 10^ 6 for the gas probed by the CO (11--10).
